The fragile ceasefire in West Asia endured yet another risk as Israel and Iran
engaged directly for over two days
, after which Iran and the US also traded strikes. Iran launched around 20-24 ballistic missiles in the north of Israel on Sunday evening and the early hours of Monday in response to Israel’s air strikes on the Dehiya, the southern suburb of Shia Muslims in the city of Beirut, which is a Hezbollah stronghold and effective headquarters (after Tehran).
Israel has been in a precarious situation with Hezbollah for the last two months since Trump announced a ceasefire with Iran. Israel was not a party to the timing of the ceasefire, and it hasn’t shown trust in the process that started from Islamabad thereafter.
Sitting here in Tel Aviv, I see daily reports, surveys and public statements of Israel’s political class, ruling coalition, as well as the opposition leaders, expressing suspicion about Trump’s “deal” diplomacy. In fact, in addition to wondering about whether the hardliners in Tehran will get on board, Trump may also have to worry about whether Israel’s political class will cooperate or not.
Benjamin Netanyahu
is set to face national elections soon. He has already “lost” a war in Iran, and he is unlikely to want to be seen as losing another one with Hezbollah in the north of Israel.
Israel’s existential fears emanate from Iran’s nuclear programme and its ballistic missiles, but a far more critical national security issue has been its fight with Hezbollah in southern Lebanon, which borders Israel. Close to 1,00,000 Israelis have been displaced because of the constant rocket and drone attacks by Hezbollah. Civil society groups are gathering food for the people of the north as the “rockets and sirens never stopped for them”. Iran has seemingly allowed Hezbollah to carry on the proxy war. This is among the growing, sometimes public rifts between Trump and Netanyahu about
output:

- **Key actors**:
  - **Israel** (led by **Benjamin Netanyahu**, facing elections), **Iran** (backing **Hezbollah**), **Hezbollah** (Lebanese Shia militant group), **US** (**Donald Trump** mediating ceasefire), **Hamas** (indirectly linked via regional proxy wars).
- **Why it matters for India**:
  - **Energy security**: **West Asia** supplies **~60% of India’s crude oil imports** (2025-26); disruptions risk **inflation, fiscal deficit, and NDA’s economic agenda**.
  - **Diplomatic leverage**: India’s **non-aligned stance** (engaging **Iran via Chabahar Port**, **Israel via defense ties**) complicates neutrality; **Trump’s "deal diplomacy"** may pressure India to pick sides.
  - **Counterterrorism**: **Hezbollah** (designated as a terrorist org by **UNSC 1701**) and **Hamas** (linked to **ISI** via **Pakistan**) pose threats to **Indian expats in Gulf** and **Kashmir insurgency**.
  - **Nuclear non-proliferation**: **Iran’s nuclear program** (violation of **JCPOA**) and **Israel’s "red lines"** (strike thresholds) impact **NSG membership** and **India’s civil nuclear deal**.
  - **Soft power**: **India’s I2U2 (India-Israel-USA-UAE)** alliance may face strain if **Trump’s mediation fails**, affecting **Quad 2.0** dynamics.
- **Significance**:
  - **Proxy war escalation**: **Hezbollah’s 100,000+ rockets** (since 2024) have displaced **~1,00,000 Israelis**, mirroring **Kargil-like humanitarian crises**—relevant for **GS Paper 3 (Security)**.
  - **Trump-Netanyahu rift**: **Leaked calls** ("Netanyahu is crazy") expose **US-Israel alliance fragility**, akin to **2019 US-Iran tensions** (post-**Soleimani strike**).
  - **Ceasefire diplomacy**: **Islamabad’s role** (as per article) hints at **Pakistan’s influence** in **Iran-Hezbollah-Hamas axis**, critical for **India’s neighborhood first policy**.
  - **Historical parallels**: **1996 Hezbollah-Israel war** (post-**Qana massacre**) vs. **2026 escalation**—test of **UNIFIL’s mandate** (Lebanon’s **UN peacekeeping force**).
  - **Economic fallout**: **Oil price volatility** (Brent crude **$90+/barrel** in 2026) threatens **India’s **PLI schemes** and **Make in India** manufacturing costs.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- **The **Dehiya suburb** of **Beirut**, a **Hezbollah stronghold**, was targeted by **Israel** in **June 2026**, triggering **Iran’s retaliatory ballistic missile strikes** (20-24 missiles) on **northern Israel**, marking the **first direct Iran-Israel conflict** since the **1996 Lebanon War**.
- **Hezbollah**, backed by **Iran**, has conducted **over 1,00,000 rocket and drone attacks** since **2024**, displacing **~1,00,000 Israelis**, with **UNIFIL (UN Interim Force in Lebanon)** struggling to enforce **UNSC Resolution 1701** (2006 ceasefire terms).

- **Israel’s Mossad** claimed a **"legendary intelligence victory"** in **September 2024** by **jamming Hezbollah’s communication pagers**, but the group has since **recovered**, symbolizing **cyber warfare’s evolving role** in modern conflicts (relevant for **GS Paper 3: Security**).
- **India’s oil imports from West Asia** (including **Iran** and **Gulf states**)

Today, Prime Minister Narendra Modi reaches a historic milestone, becoming the longest-serving elected Prime Minister of India in continuous office, completing 4,399 days since first assuming this responsibility on May 26, 2014. For India, this is a moment of historic significance. For the world, it reflects the confidence that the people of the world’s largest democracy have repeatedly placed in a leader who has guided their nation through a transformative period.
For me, however, this occasion is more than a political milestone. It is an opportunity to reflect on a relationship that has evolved over the years — one that I value deeply. I have come to know Prime Minister Modi as a friend, regard him as an elder brother, and look up to him as a mentor whose dedication to public service continues to inspire me.
My friend
Politics and diplomacy are often described as being built on national interests and institutional relationships. While this is true, the strongest and most enduring partnerships between nations are also nurtured by genuine human connections and friendships founded on trust and mutual respect.
I first met Prime Minister Narendra Modi in 2014, when I had the privilege of attending his swearing-in ceremony as Prime Minister of India. From that very first meeting, his warmth and graciousness left a lasting impression on me. What began as an official interaction soon evolved into a personal friendship that I have come to value deeply over the years.
Soon thereafter, Prime Minister Modi chose Bhutan as the destination of his first overseas visit after assuming office. That decision carried a powerful message about the importance he attached to the special ties between our two countries. It reaffirmed the deep bonds of goodwill and shared destiny that connect our peoples and reflected the affection and regard he has always held for Bhutan.
In all my interactions with him, what has consistently stood out is his sincerity, humility, and remarkable ability to
output:

  - **Significance**:
    - **Domestic**: Symbolizes **decade-long governance continuity**, critical for **long-term policy execution** (e.g., **Make in India**, **Digital India**, **GST**, **infrastructure push**).
    - **International**: Reinforces India’s **global standing** as a **stable democracy** and **voice of the Global South**.
    - **Bilateral**: Highlights **India-Bhutan ties**, described as **"special friendship"** with **historical trust**, **developmental support**, and **strategic cooperation**.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- **Prime Minister Narendra Modi** has surpassed **Manmohan Singh** to become India’s **longest-serving elected Prime Minister in continuous office**, completing **4,399 days** (as of **June 2024**) since taking charge on **May 26, 2014**.
- **Bhutan** considers **India** its **"closest friend and most trusted partner"**, a relationship strengthened under **PM Modi’s leadership**, including support for **Gelephu Mindfulness City**, a **visionary urban project** aligned with **Bhutan’s Gross National Happiness (GNH) philosophy**.
- **PM Modi’s first overseas visit** after assuming office in **2014** was to **Bhutan**, underscoring the **priority given to bilateral ties** and the **"special bonds"** between the two nations.
- The **Gelephu Mindfulness City** initiative in **Bhutan**, supported by **India**, aims to integrate **urban development with mindfulness and sustainability**, reflecting **Bhutan’s unique governance model** centered on **Gross National Happiness (GNH)** over GDP.
- **Barai Bhai** is a **traditional term in Bhutanese culture** signifying **elder brotherly affection, trust, and guidance**, used by **Bhutan’s PM** to describe his relationship with **PM Modi**, highlighting **personal and political trust** in bilateral diplomacy.

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
#### **Political/Administrative**
- **Democratic Legitimacy and Governance Continuity**:
  - **Modi’s tenure** (2014–2024) marks **10 years of stable governance**, critical for **policy implementation** (e.g., **GST**, **Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code**, **infrastructure megaprojects**).
  - **Inter-linkage**: Contrast with **coalition governments** (e.g., **UPA, NDAs**) where **policy paralysis** often hindered reforms. **Single-party majority** enabled **bold economic and social reforms**.
  - **Challenge**: **Opposition allegations of "majoritarianism"** and **erosion of federalism** (e.g., **Article 370 abrogation**, **CAA-NRC debates**) remain contentious in **GS Paper 2 (Polity)**.

- **India-Bhutan Relations: A Model of Soft Power and Development Partnership**:
  - **Bhutan’s foreign policy** is **pro-India**, rooted in **geopolitical vulnerability** (landlocked, dependent on **India for trade, defense, and hydropower**).
  - **India’s role**:
    - **Development aid**: **$500 million Line of Credit (LoC)** (2022) for **hydropower projects** (e.g., **Mangdechhu, Punatsangchhu**).
    - **Strategic support**: **Defense cooperation** (e.g., **Indian Army’s training, border infrastructure**).

Meta and Reliance Industries on Tuesday announced a major expansion of their strategic partnership with plans to develop an AI-enabled data centre in India, strengthening the country’s role in the global artificial intelligence infrastructure race.
The announcement highlights Meta’s increasing focus on India, one of its largest and fastest-growing markets. The company says the facility will help bring the infrastructure needed to power its products, services and future AI capabilities closer to millions of users in the country.
Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg described the project as an important milestone in the company’s global infrastructure strategy.
“We’re proud to be working with Reliance to build our first AI-enabled data centre in India. This world-class facility in Jamnagar will help us scale our AI infrastructure globally while deepening our long-term investment in India’s economy,” Zuckerberg was quoted as saying in the press release.
The Jamnagar facility is expected to be powered by renewable energy and cooled using desalinated seawater, making it one of the more environmentally conscious data centre projects announced in the region. Meta said it will bear the full cost of the energy and water required to operate the facility.
The project also aligns with Meta’s broader push to expand the computing capacity required to support advanced artificial intelligence systems. As companies race to build increasingly powerful AI models, demand for large-scale data centres and high-performance computing infrastructure has surged worldwide.
Story continues below this ad
Reliance Industries Chairman Mukesh Ambani said the agreement demonstrates India’s growing importance in the global AI ecosystem.
“This partnership with Meta marks a transformative moment for India’s digital infrastructure. Building India’s first built-to-suit AI data centre for a global technology leader of Meta’s scale demonstrates India’s readiness to be at the forefront of the global AI revolution,” Am

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
A Supreme Court-appointed panel examining student suicides has flagged a “complete absence” of any dedicated statutory, regulatory or institutional framework to prevent such deaths in India’s higher education institutions, while finding that a majority of colleges and universities surveyed lack even basic mental health support services.
In its interim report, made public this week, the National Task Force (NTF) found that 65 per cent of 2,119 higher education institutions surveyed did not provide access to mental health service providers, while 73 per cent lacked a full-time mental health professional.
The panel, which submitted the report to the court in November last year, also reported significant shortcomings in grievance redressal systems and institutional responses to student distress.
“The most glaring gap is the complete absence of any direct statutory, regulatory or institutional framework to address and prevent suicides. Most interventions are generic and reactive. The only document that provides possible modes of intervention, which is the ‘Suicide Prevention Strategy’ (a 2022 document of the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare), is abstract with no clear implementation guidelines,” the panel has noted.
“Given the complex structure of the civil society in India, where caste, class, economics, aptitude, region, and language have enormous diversity, a tailored implementation strategy to suit each demographic is critical,” it said.
The report also noted that in institutions that had seen student suicides recently, “institutional empathy towards students was missing, and harsh, callous backlash followed.”
“They (the institution) denied their culpability, suppressed all protests, increased surveillance on students, and, in the case of a private university, imposed stringent institutional controls on students. In another instance, where students protested for three days after a case of suicide in a nursing college, while the principal was made to leave, the b
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**: The **Supreme Court**-appointed **National Task Force (NTF)** on student suicides submitted an **interim report** highlighting the **absence of a dedicated statutory/regulatory framework** for mental health in **higher education institutions (HEIs)**.
  - **When**: Report submitted in **November 2024**, findings released in **2025**; NTF constituted in **March 2024** after **IIT Delhi student suicides**.
  - **Where**: Survey covered **2,119 HEIs** (3.5% of total in India) and **2.43 lakh students**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Chair**: **Justice S. Ravindra Bhat** (former **Supreme Court Judge**).
  - **Members**: **Dr. Alok Sarin** (Psychiatrist, **Sitaram Bhartia Institute**), **Dr. Seema Mehrotra** (**NIMHANS**), **Mary E. John** (former **Centre for Women’s Development Studies** director).
  - **Stakeholders**: Students, faculty, parents, **HEIs**, **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW)**.

- **Why it matters**:
  - **UPSC Angle**: Exposes **systemic failures** in **mental health governance**, **student welfare**, and **institutional accountability** in **higher education**—a **GS Paper 2 (Polity/Education) and GS Paper 4 (Ethics)** issue.
  - **India Angle**: **65% HEIs lack mental health services**; **73% have no full-time counselors**; **56 HEIs reported suicides (2020–2025)**. Highlights **caste/class discrimination**, **scholarship delays**, and **administrative callousness**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent**: First **judicial probe** into **student mental health** in India; calls for **legal reforms** in **higher education governance**.
  - **Context**: Aligns with **NITI Aayog’s mental health strategy** and **WHO’s global suicide prevention guidelines**.
  - **Implications**: **Right to Education (RTE) vs. institutional neglect**; **Article 21 (Right to Life)** vs. **administrative apathy**.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- The **National Task Force (NTF)** on student suicides, headed by **Justice S. Ravindra Bhat**, found that **65% of 2,119 surveyed higher education institutions (HEIs)** in India **do not provide access to mental health service providers**, while **73% lack a full-time mental health professional**, as per its **interim report submitted to the Supreme Court in November 2024**.
- The **Suicide Prevention Strategy (2022)**, issued by the **Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (MoHFW)**, is the **only government document** addressing student suicides but lacks **clear implementation guidelines**, according to the **NTF report**.
- The **Supreme Court** constituted the **12-member NTF** in **March 2024** after two student deaths at **IIT Delhi**, with **six ex officio members** including experts from **NIMHANS** and **Sitaram Bhartia Institute**.
- Of the **2,119 HEIs surveyed**, **56 reported one or more student suicides between April 2020 and March 2025**, revealing **institutional failures in grievance redressal** and **mental health support**, per the **NTF’s field visits to 13 institutions**.
- The **Internal Complaints Committees (ICCs)** mandated under the **POSH Act (2013)** were found to be **ineffective** in **sexual harassment cases**, with students reporting **suppression of complaints** and **biased proceedings** during the **NTF’s investigations**.

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
- **Social**:

  - **Gender & harassment**: **POSH Act (2013) mandates ICCs**, but **NTF found systemic failures**—students faced **suppression of sexual harassment cases** and **administrative backlash** (e.g., **nursing college students "marked

The Elon Musk owned-SpaceX Starlink on Wednesday said it is in discussions with the Centre and has received positive responses regarding its role in supporting the India’s connectivity objectives. The satellite internet provider earlier applied for permission to launch satellite communication services in India. While it had already received a letter of intent from the government, the final licence is yet to be granted.
Is Starlink rollout stalled by government?
Responding to a
Bloomberg
report that cited unnamed sources and claimed that the government has “effectively stalled approvals” for Starlink’s commercial rollout. The report said that the government was concerned over the use of Starlink satellite terminals during the Iran conflict. Lauren Dreyer, vice-president of Starlink Business Operations, however, dismissed the report.
Posting on X on Wednesday, Dreyer said Starlink continues to hold “active and productive” discussions with the Indian government and described reports suggesting otherwise as misleading and based on unverified claims.
She stated that the company has complied with all regulatory and compliance requirements and has maintained a transparent and responsible approach throughout its engagement with the government.
India has already granted licences to two other satellite communication players — Bharti Group-backed Eutelsat OneWeb and Jio-SES (Space Technology Ltd). Both companies are currently awaiting spectrum allocation before they can begin offering services, reported news agency
PTI
.
‘Remote areas focus for Starlink’
Dreyer said the company has received consistently encouraging feedback regarding Starlink’s technology and its ability to contribute to India’s efforts to improve connectivity, particularly in remote and underserved areas.
She reiterated Starlink’s commitment to the Indian market and said the company remains focused on working closely with the government to introduce its services i

- The **Iran conflict** context highlights **India’s cautious approach to foreign satellite tech**, potentially linking to **national security protocols** under **Section 7 of the IT Act, 2000** (critical infrastructure protection).

---

#### **Economic**

#### **Political/Administrative**

- **Federal vs. Central Control**:

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Scientists at CSIR-CFTRI in Mysuru are exploring ways to combine AI-driven technologies into food processing, safety and post-harvest management.
                                          | Photo Credit: M.A. Sriram
Artificial intelligence is making its way into the research laboratories of the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research–Central Food Technological Research Institute (CSIR-CFTRI) in Mysuru, with scientists exploring ways to integrate AI-driven technologies into food processing, food safety, and post-harvest management for societal benefit.
Although AI-based research initiatives have gained momentum only recently, CFTRI scientists are working to leverage the technology to address longstanding challenges in the food sector.
One key area of focus is tackling fungal contamination in food grains and other commodities during post-harvest storage. Food products stored in silos and warehouses are often vulnerable to fungal infestations, leading to spoilage and deterioration in quality.
To address this issue, CFTRI has developed an ozone-based technology that is believed to be effective in preventing fungal contamination. The technology is currently capable of treating food stocks ranging from 50 kg to 100 kg. Scientists are now developing a prototype that can be scaled up for use in large warehouses, where food grains are stored in quantities running into lakhs of tonnes.
“Data collection has been completed, and logistics-related aspects are being worked out. We are making progress on the project,” said Giridhar Parvatam, Director of CFTRI.
Another area in which AI is being deployed is onion storage and sorting. Onion crops are often affected by fungal infections, resulting in significant post-harvest losses. CFTRI is developing an AI-enabled technology that can identify and separate healthy onions from fungal-infected ones. The institute is also developing AI algorithms for grading and sorting food products to enable faster and more accurate quality asses
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**: **CSIR-CFTRI (Mysuru)** is integrating **AI-driven technologies** into **food processing, safety, and post-harvest management** to combat spoilage, fungal contamination, and improve efficiency.
- **Key actors**:
  - **CSIR-CFTRI** (under **Ministry of Science & Technology**)
  - **Director: Dr. Giridhar Parvatam**
  - **Focus areas**: Ozone-based fungal control, AI-enabled onion sorting, space foods, and sports nutrition.
- **Why it matters**:
  - Addresses **post-harvest losses** (estimated **~30% of food grains** in India).
  - Aligns with **Atmanirbhar Bharat** and **SDG 2 (Zero Hunger)**.
  - Leverages **AI for agriculture**, a priority under **National AI Strategy (2020)**.
- **Significance**:
  - **Scalable tech** for warehouses storing **lakhs of tonnes** of grains.
  - Potential to **reduce mycotoxin exposure** (linked to **cancer and liver diseases**).
  - First-of-its-kind **AI + ozone hybrid system** for food safety in India.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- **The **Council of Scientific and Industrial Research–Central Food Technological Research Institute (CSIR-CFTRI)**, located in **Mysuru, Karnataka**, is developing **AI-driven solutions** for **post-harvest food management**, including **ozone-based fungal decontamination** and **AI-enabled sorting of onions**.
- **India’s post-harvest food losses** are estimated at **~30% for grains**, costing the economy **over ₹92,000 crore annually**, as per the **NITI Aayog’s 2021 report on "Food Processing & Nutrition"**.
- **CSIR-CFTRI’s ozone technology** can currently treat **50–100 kg of food stocks** and is being scaled up for **warehouses storing lakhs of tonnes**, with **data collection already completed** (as of **June 2026**).
- **AI applications in agriculture** are a key focus under **India’s National AI Strategy (2020)**, which aims to leverage **AI for doubling farmers’ income by 2025**.
- **Mycotoxins** (produced by fungi like **Aspergillus and Fusarium**) are **carcinogenic** and linked to **liver cirrhosis and immune suppression**, making fungal control critical for **public health** under **Ayushman Bharat**.

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
- **Economic**:
  - **Reduces food wastage** (₹92,000 crore/year loss) and **lowers procurement costs** for **FCI and state warehouses**.
  - **AI sorting** can **increase onion export competitiveness** (India is the **2nd-largest onion producer** after China).
  - **Space foods & sports nutrition** projects align with **ISRO’s Gaganyaan mission** and **Khelo India initiatives**, requiring **high-protein, shelf-stable foods**.

- **Social**:
  - **Mycotoxin exposure** disproportionately affects **marginal farmers and low-income groups** reliant on stored grains.
  - **AI sorting** can **reduce manual labor risks** (e.g., fungal spore inhalation in warehouses).
  - **Nutrition security** improves via **contaminant-free grains**, linking to **Poshan Abhiyaan** and **ICDS schemes**.

- **Environmental**:
  - **Ozone treatment** is **chemical-free**, unlike **fumigants (e.g., phosphine)**, which are **toxic and banned in the EU**.
  - **Reduces pesticide residues** in food, addressing **residue-based export rejections** (e.g., **EU’s MRL violations**).
  - **AI optimizes storage conditions**, cutting **energy use in warehouses** (aligned with **India’s Net Zero 2070 pledge**).

- **Political/Administrative**:
  - **CSIR-CFTRI’s work** complements **AgriStack (digital agriculture mission)** and **PM-KISAN’s storage subsidies**.
  - **Scalability challenge**: **Public-private partnerships (PPPs)** needed to deploy tech in **state-run godowns** (e.g., **FCI, NAFED**).
  - **Regulatory hurdles**: **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** must certify **ozone-treated grains** under **Food Safety and Standards (Contaminants, Toxins, and Residues) Regulations, 2011**.

- **Inter-linkages**:
  - **

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Customer relationship management software major Salesforce on Tuesday said it will equip one million learners across India with AI-focused skills by 2030.
It will collaborate with academic institutions, industry leaders and government agencies, President and CEO, Salesforce - South Asia Arundhati Bhattacharya said at a programme to mark the 10 years of the company’s Centre of Excellence (CoE) here. A virtual internship programme with AICTE to empower students with hands-on project experience will be one of the components.
“Our commitment to skill one million learners by 2030 reflects a firm belief that India’s workforce is not just ready for the AI economy [but also] ready to lead it. At Salesforce, we are committed to ensuring that potential does not go unrealised and that the next generation of technology leaders emerges from right here,” she said.
Published
- June 10, 2026 12:35 am IST
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**: **Salesforce** announced a **10-year commitment** to equip **1 million learners in India** with **AI-focused skills by 2030**, marking the **10th anniversary** of its **Centre of Excellence (CoE)** in India.
- **Key actors**:
  - **Salesforce** (global CRM & AI software leader)
  - **Arundhati Bhattacharya** (President & CEO, Salesforce South Asia)
  - **AICTE** (All India Council for Technical Education)
  - **Academic institutions, industry leaders, and government agencies** (collaborators).
- **Why it matters**:
  - Aligns with **India’s AI vision** (NITI Aayog’s **AI Task Force, 2018**) and **Skill India Mission**.
  - Addresses **youth unemployment** (India’s **working-age population: 68%**, per **World Bank 2023**).
  - Boosts **Make in India** via **homegrown AI talent**.
- **Significance**:
  - **First major corporate-led AI skilling initiative** at scale in India.
  - **Virtual internships with AICTE** bridge **theory-practice gap** in engineering education.
  - Reflects **private sector’s role in ESDM (Electronics & IT)** under **PLI schemes**.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- **Salesforce’s Centre of Excellence (CoE) in India** completed **10 years** in **2026**, focusing on **AI and cloud computing** skilling, with a **1-million-learner target by 2030**.
- The initiative includes a **virtual internship programme** in collaboration with the **All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE)**, a **statutory body** under the **Ministry of Education**, established by the **AICTE Act, 1987**.
- **India’s AI Task Force (2018)**, chaired by **Vijay Raghavan**, recommended **AI integration in education** to address **unemployment among graduates** (India’s **unemployment rate: 7.8% in 2023**, per **CMIE**).
- **Skill India Mission**, launched in **2015** under **PMKVY (Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana)**, aims to **skill 400 million Indians by 2025**, with **AI and digital skills** as a priority sector.
- **Electronics & IT sector** received **₹76,000 crore** under **Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes**, with **AI/ML startups** eligible for **tax benefits under Section 80-IA** of the **Income Tax Act, 1961**.

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
- **Economic**:
  - **Job creation in AI**: India’s **IT-BPM sector employs 5 million**, but **AI could add 9-10 million jobs by 2025** (NASSCOM). **Salesforce’s initiative** complements **PLI 2.0** for **semiconductor and AI chip manufacturing**.
  - **Private-public partnership (PPP)**: Aligns with **NEP 2020’s focus on industry-academia collaboration** (Syllabus: **Education, GS Paper 2**).
  - **Export potential**: Trained AI professionals can boost **India’s IT services exports (₹2.5 lakh crore in 2023)** by targeting **global AI demand** (US, EU, Japan).

- **Social**:

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
P. Kruthivas, president-elect, CREDAI Chennai, highlights that a substantial amount of land has already been acquired in Parandur for the second airport project.
                                          | Photo Credit: B. VELANKANNI RAJ
The Confederation of Real Estate Developers’ Associations of India (CREDAI) has stressed the need for a second airport for Chennai, arguing that the city risks losing investment and economic opportunities if aviation infrastructure is not expanded.
“The question is no longer whether Chennai needs a second international airport. The real question is whether the city can afford to delay it any further,” said P. Kruthivas, president-elect, CREDAI Chennai, and managing director, Pushkar Properties Private Limited.
“We are already late. Many airlines are shifting their operations to Bengaluru. Air France has already exited Chennai. Cathay Pacific, which earlier operated daily flights between Chennai and Hong Kong, has reduced its services. All this is happening because the current airport lacks the capacity to support further growth,” he said.
To a query on the Parandur project, Mr. Kruthivas said: “We need a second airport. There is no doubt about that. As for Parandur, it makes more sense. It has taken us three to five years to reach this stage. If we scrap the project now, how will that benefit anyone? Building an airport is not like building a highway. It takes years and cannot be done overnight.”
He highlighted that a substantial amount of land had already been acquired in Parandur. “Ecological concerns must certainly be examined — I’m not saying they shouldn’t be. But sustainability and growth have to go hand in hand,” he said. “The Parandur site is strategically located along the Chennai–Bengaluru industrial corridor, with significant electronics, automobile, and warehousing activity nearby. It is also supported by planned infrastructure, including Outer Ring Road and Chennai Metro Rail Phase II extension up to Thirumazhisai. More
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**: **CREDAI Chennai** (Confederation of Real Estate Developers’ Associations of India) advocates for the **second airport in Chennai**, emphasizing **Parandur** as the preferred site due to **land acquisition** and **infrastructure readiness**.
- **Key actors**:
  - **P. Kruthivas** (President-elect, **CREDAI Chennai**; MD, **Pushkar Properties**).
  - **Air France**, **Cathay Pacific** (airlines reducing/halting operations due to **Chennai Airport’s capacity constraints**).
  - **Government of Tamil Nadu** (responsible for land acquisition and project approval).
- **Why it matters**:
  - **Economic growth**: Chennai risks losing **investment and regional dominance** to **Bengaluru, Hyderabad, and Dubai**.
  - **Infrastructure bottleneck**: Current **Chennai International Airport (MEP)** may hit **saturation by 2028–2032**.
  - **Global competitiveness**: Poor connectivity impacts **exports, tourism, and FDI**.
- **Significance**:
  - **Strategic location**: Parandur aligns with the **Chennai–Bengaluru Industrial Corridor** and **Outer Ring Road/Metro Phase II**.
  - **Ecological vs. developmental trade-off**: Balancing **green concerns** with **economic urgency**.
  - **Precedent**: Similar to **Delhi’s Noida Airport** or **Mumbai’s Navi Mumbai Airport** projects.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- **The **Chennai International Airport (MEP)** is projected to reach **saturation between 2028 and 2032**, despite upgrades increasing capacity to **35 million passengers per annum (MPPA)**.
- **Parandur**, the proposed site for Chennai’s **second airport**, is **65 km from the city**, strategically located along the **Chennai–Bengaluru Industrial Corridor** and supported by **Outer Ring Road** and **Chennai Metro Phase II extension**.
- **Land acquisition** for the **Parandur airport project** has already progressed significantly, with **substantial parcels acquired**, as highlighted by **CREDAI Chennai**.
- **Air France** has **exited Chennai**, while **Cathay Pacific** reduced flights to **Hong Kong**, citing **infrastructure limitations** at **MEP Airport**.
- **Section 4** of the **Airports Economic Regulatory Authority of India (AERA) Act, 2008** governs airport tariffs, but **land use and environmental clearances** fall under **State and Central government jurisdictions** (e.g., **MoEFCC**, **Tamil Nadu State Government**).

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
- **Economic**:
  - **Regional competition**: Chennai’s **declining aviation hub status** risks **capital flight to Bengaluru (Kempegowda Airport)** and **Hyderabad (RGIA)**, which have **better connectivity and cargo facilities**.
  - **Export and tourism impact**: Poor connectivity **increases logistics costs** and **deters international business travelers**, hurting **IT/ITeS, pharmaceuticals, and textiles** sectors.
  - **Job creation**: A second airport could generate **~50,000 direct/indirect jobs** (comparable to **Delhi’s Noida Airport** impact).

- **Political/Administrative**:
  - **Federal vs. state tensions**: **Land acquisition** and **environmental clearances** require coordination between **Central (MoCA, MoEFCC)** and **Tamil Nadu State** governments.
  - **Project delays**: Similar to **Navi Mumbai Airport** (delayed due to **land acquisition disputes**), **Parandur faces ecological concerns** (e.g., **wetland impacts**, **coastal regulation zone**).
  - **Public-private partnership (PPP) model**: Success depends on **risk-sharing mechanisms** (e.g., **AERA’s tariff regulations** vs. **private investor expectations**).

- **Environmental**:
  - **Ecological trade-offs**: Parandur’s proximity to **coastal wetlands** and **industrial zones** raises **biodiversity vs. development** debates (cf. **Supreme Court’s **Vellore Wetlands judgment, 2020**).
  - **Climate resilience**: Airports must comply with **National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)** and **Paris Agreement goals**, balancing **carbon footprint** with **growth**.

- **International Relations**:
  - **Global hub competition**: Chennai must improve **direct long-haul routes** to compete with **Dubai (DXB)**, **Singapore (SIN)**, and **Bangkok (BKK)** for **South-East

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
What we do not save, we lose, and with it, a piece of what it means to be human.
Over the last month, renovation has been taking place at the Egmore Government Museum at the cost of some of Tamil Nadu’s most prized idols and sculptures. Construction equipment is propped up beside ancient temple pillars and idols. The antiques, many of which are more than 1,000 years old, had been seized by the Idol Wing of the Tamil Nadu Police, and then stored here.
Earlier reports in
The Hindu
suggest that the Idol Wing of Tamil Nadu seized several thousand artefacts over the years, including centuries-old stone sculptures and idols, carved stone pillars, intricately worked wooden pieces, temple cars, vahanas or ritual vehicles for gods, , among others and were stored in a haphazard manner at  the rear-end of the unit’s premises that is part of the CB-CID Economic Offences Wing at Guindy in Chennai back in 2019. The police claimed that the museum did not allocate space for these idols to be placed there. Subsequently though, these idols were placed inside the museum campus, with little concern or care for its storied heritage. This remains the status till date.
Pillars lie with construction rubble
                                                            | Photo Credit: 
                                Special Arrangement
The renovation work by the Public Works Department is currently taking place at the Geology Wing of the museum constructed  in 1851. The structure has reportedly suffered 60% damage with time and is spread across 5,500 square feet. Workers say that they are currently painting the structure. Beside the Geology section, on a vacant plot of museum land, lie statues and pillars, seized from across the world, by the Idol Wing. They have been placed on the floor, close to construction equipment that churn out concrete. The beams that hold a building together can be seen resting atop these historic sculptures. The idols themselves peek out of a thick layer of strewn l
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**: **Egmore Government Museum (Chennai)** is undergoing renovation, but **1,000+ year-old idols and sculptures** (seized by **Tamil Nadu Police’s Idol Wing**) are being stored **without proper care**, exposed to **construction debris, dust, and summer heat**.
- **Key actors**:
  - **Tamil Nadu Police’s Idol Wing** (seized artefacts).
  - **Public Works Department (PWD)** (conducting renovation).
  - **Department of Museums, Tamil Nadu** (responsible for upkeep).
  - **Activists, historians (e.g., Pradeep Chakravarthy), and visitors** (highlighting neglect).
- **Why it matters**:
  - **Cultural heritage erosion**: **Hoysala, Chola, and Buddhist-era artefacts** (11th–18th century) risk **permanent damage** due to **poor storage and exposure**.
  - **Governance failure**: **Lack of coordination** between **police, museums, and PWD** despite **legal and historical significance**.
  - **Global relevance**: **Largest collection of Roman coins outside Rome** and **Chola bronzes** are at stake.
- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for heritage mismanagement**: Similar issues exist in **other state museums** (e.g., **Madras Museum’s neglected collections**).
  - **Legal loopholes**: **No dedicated law** for **seized artefacts’ preservation**; relies on **police custody** and **museum policies**.
  - **Economic loss**: **Million-dollar artefacts** (e.g., **granite/limestone sculptures**) could be **irreparably damaged**, affecting **tourism and cultural diplomacy**.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- The **Egmore Government Museum**, established in **1851**, houses the **largest collection of ancient Roman coins outside Rome** and **Chola-era bronzes**, making it a **UNESCO-recognized heritage site** under **India’s National Monuments Act, 1958**.
- **Tamil Nadu Police’s Idol Wing** has seized **thousands of artefacts** (including **Hoysala (10th–14th century) and Chola sculptures**) but stores them **without climate control**, violating **Section 11 of the Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains Act, 1958** (which mandates **protection of seized antiquities**).
- The **Geology Wing of the museum**, covering **5,500 sq. ft.**, has suffered **60% structural damage** due to **neglect**, requiring **PWD intervention**—a case study for **heritage conservation failures** in **state-run institutions**.
- **Pradeep Chakravarthy**, a historian, identified **Buddhist-influenced panels** (possibly **pre-17th century**) among the neglected idols, linking **South Indian art** to **Central Indian and Southeast Asian trade routes** (relevant for **Ancient Indian History**).
- The **2019 report by The Hindu** highlighted **haphazard storage** of **stone, bronze, and wooden artefacts** in **Guindy police premises**, later shifted to the museum **without proper infrastructure**—a **violation of the National Cultural Policy, 2013** (which emphasizes **decentralized heritage preservation**).

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
- **Cultural & Historical**:
  - **Erosion of tangible heritage**: The **Chola bronzes** (UNESCO-recognized) and **Hoysala sculptures** (Dravidian architecture) represent **India’s intangible cultural heritage**, yet **lack institutional safeguards**. This mirrors **global cases** like **Palmyra’s destruction** (Syria) and **Bamiyan Buddhas** (Afghanistan), where **conflict and neglect** led to losses.
  - **Inter-linkage with GS Paper 1 (Art & Culture)**: The **Idol Wing’s seizures** reflect **smuggling challenges** (e.g., **red sandstone idols smuggled to UAE**), requiring **strengthened **Antiquities and Art Treasures Act, 1972** enforcement.

- **Administrative & Legal**:
  - **Fragmented governance**: **Police (seizure), PWD (renovation), and Museums Department (custody)** operate in **silos**, with **no single authority** for **seized artefacts**. This violates **Principle 1 of the New Delhi Declaration (2016)** on **heritage conservation**, which advocates **multi-stakeholder coordination**.
  - **Legal gaps**: The **Ancient Monuments Act, 1958** and **Antiquities Act

terming it a “gamechanger from security perspective and [the perspective of] national integration”.

output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**: **Breakthrough ceremony** of the **13.14 km Zojila Tunnel** (world’s longest **single-tube, bi-directional** road tunnel at **11,578 ft altitude**), connecting **Jammu & Kashmir (J&K) and Ladakh**, held on **June 10, 2026**.
- **Key actors**:
  - **Nitin Gadkari** (Union Minister, **Road Transport & Highways**),
  - **Omar Abdullah** (**J&K Chief Minister**),
  - **Manoj Sinha** (**Lieutenant Governor, J&K**),
  - **MEIL (Megha Engineering & Infrastructures Ltd.)** (executing agency for **NHIDCL**),
  - **Indian Army** (strategic beneficiary).
- **Why it matters**:
  - **All-weather connectivity** between **Kashmir Valley and Ladakh**, reducing travel time from **2 hours to 30 minutes**.
  - **Strategic security** for **Indian Army** logistics in **Ladakh (China border)**.
  - **Economic boost** for **J&K and Ladakh** via tourism, trade, and employment.
  - **Engineering feat** in **extreme Himalayan conditions** (altitude **2,900–3,310 m**, snowfall, complex geology).
- **Significance**:
  - **PM Modi’s infrastructure push** under **Bharatmala Pariyojana** and **Sagarmala**.
  - **Symbol of national integration** post-**J&K reorganization (2019)**.
  - **Cost optimization**: Completed at **₹6,800 crore** (vs. initial estimate of **₹12,000 crore**).
  - **Safety features**: **Ventilation system, fire detection, CCTV, cross-passages**.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- The **Zojila Tunnel** is the **world’s longest single-tube, bi-directional road tunnel** at an **altitude of 11,578 ft (3,529 m)**, built between **Sonamarg (J&K) and Minamarg (Ladakh)** under the **Bharatmala Pariyojana**, reducing travel time from **2 hours to 30 minutes**.
- The project was executed by **Megha Engineering & Infrastructures Limited (MEIL)** for the **National Highways and Infrastructure Development Corporation Limited (NHIDCL)** at a **cost of ₹6,800 crore**, despite initial estimates of **₹12,000 crore**.
- The tunnel spans **13.14 km** and is built at altitudes ranging from **2,900 m to 3,310 m**, showcasing India’s engineering capability in **extreme Himalayan conditions** with **snowfall, harsh weather, and complex geology**.
- **Key safety features** include a **modern ventilation system, automatic fire detection, advanced CCTV surveillance, and cross-passage facilities** for pedestrians, aligning with **international tunnel safety standards**.
- The **breakthrough ceremony** was attended by **Union Minister Nitin Gadkari, J&K CM Omar Abdullah, and LG Manoj Sinha** on **June 10, 2026**, marking a milestone in **India’s infrastructure development** and **strategic connectivity** for the **Indian Army** in **Ladakh**.

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
- **Strategic Security**:
  - Enhances **Indian Army’s operational efficiency** in **Ladakh (China border)**, enabling **faster movement of troops and logistics** year-round, critical for **border security and countering China’s infrastructure push (CPEC)**.
  - **Inter-linkage**: Connects with **GS Paper 2 (Security)** — **India-China border disputes, Ladakh standoff (2020–present), and infrastructure as a force multiplier**.

- **Economic & Regional Development**:
  - **Boosts tourism** in **Kashmir and Ladakh** by ensuring **all-weather connectivity**, reducing **accidents and avalanche risks** (historically, **NH-1D was closed for 6 months annually**).
  - **Employment generation**: Direct jobs for **local workers** and indirect benefits for **transport, hospitality, and trade sectors**.
  - **Inter-linkage**: Aligns with **PM Gati Shakti National Master Plan** and **UTs’ development post-reorganization (2019)**.

- **Engineering & Technological Marvel**:
  - Demonstrates **India’s capability in high-altitude

input:
A general view shows the plenary hall during an international replenishment conference for the United Nations Green Climate Fund in Bonn, Germany. File
                                          | Photo Credit: Reuters
India has called for the shrinking pool of climate finance and a widening adaptation finance gap to be tackled head-on at the United Nations climate negotiations-linked talks under way in Bonn, Germany. It has urged that a Paris Agreement provision, which obliges developed countries to provide funds to developing nations, be given dedicated agenda space to enable substantive progress.
The intervention came in India’s statement to the 64
th
session of the UNFCCC Subsidiary Bodies (SB64), delivered by Harkeerat Randhawa of the Ministry of External Affairs (MEA). India associated itself with the positions taken on behalf of the Group of 77 and China (G-77), the Like-Minded Developing Countries (LMDC) and the BASIC bloc (Brazil, South Africa, India, China).
The Bonn meeting runs from June 8 to 18 and is the mid-year session of the two subsidiary bodies — on implementation (SBI) and on scientific and technological advice (SBSTA) — that prepare draft decisions for the annual Conference of the Parties. It is the first multilateral climate conference since COP30, held in Belém, Brazil, last November, and is tasked with turning those outcomes into negotiable text ahead of CoP31. That summit will be hosted in Antalya, Turkey in November, which holds the formal presidency and Australia presiding over the negotiations.
Mitigation Work Programme
India pressed for the dialogue on unilateral trade measures — a reference to carbon border levies such as the EU’s Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) — to address their adverse effects on developing countries’ climate action, anchored in Article 3.5 of the Convention. It cautioned that the Mitigation Work Programme’s facilitative, non-prescriptive character be preserved, that the adaptation goal remain balanced and P
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**: **India’s intervention** at the **64th session of the **UNFCCC Subsidiary Bodies (SB64)** in **Bonn, Germany (June 8–18, 2026)**, a mid-year climate negotiation to operationalize **COP30 (Belém, Brazil, Nov 2025)** outcomes ahead of **COP31 (Antalya, Turkey, Nov 2026)**.
- **Key actors**:
  - **India** (via **MEA’s Harkeerat Randhawa**), aligned with **G-77 & China**, **Like-Minded Developing Countries (LMDC)**, and **BASIC bloc (Brazil, South Africa, India, China)**.
  - **Developed nations** (e.g., **EU**, **Australia**—presiding over negotiations).
  - **UNFCCC** subsidiary bodies: **SBI (Implementation)** and **SBSTA (Science & Tech)**.
- **Why it matters**:
  - **Climate finance gap**: India highlighted the **shrinking pool of climate finance** and **adaptation finance deficit**, demanding **Paris Agreement Article 9** (finance obligations for developed nations) be prioritized.
  - **CBAM & trade wars**: India opposed **EU’s Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM)** under **Article 3.5 (UNFCCC)**, citing adverse impacts on developing economies.
  - **Just Transition & CBDR-RC**: Pushed for **equity-based operationalization** of the **Just Transition Mechanism**, rejecting "one-size-fits-all" mitigation mandates.
- **Significance**:
  - **Implementation phase**: SB64 focuses on **Global Goal on Adaptation**, **Global Stocktake (2024–2028)**, and **Sharm el-Sheikh Mitigation Work Programme (MWP, expiring 2026)**.
  - **Geopolitical leverage**: India’s stance reflects its **emerging economy status** vs. **historical emitters**, balancing **energy access (coal, renewables)** with **climate commitments**.
  - **Precedent for COP31**: Outcomes will shape **2026–2030 climate action**, including **new Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)**.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- The **64th session of the **UNFCCC Subsidiary Bodies (SB64)** is being held in **Bonn, Germany (June 8–18, 2026)**, the first major climate negotiation since **COP30 in Belém, Brazil (November 2025)**, with **COP31 scheduled for Antalya, Turkey (November 2026)**, where **Turkey holds the presidency** and **Australia leads negotiations**.
- India’s demand for **dedicated agenda space for **Article 9 of the **Paris Agreement** (climate finance obligations for developed nations)** stems from concerns over the **shrinking climate finance pool** and the **adaptation finance gap**, which currently stands at a **$210–387 billion annual shortfall** for developing countries by **2030** (per **UNEP Adaptation Gap Report 2023**).
- The **Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM)**, introduced by the **European Union (EU) in 2023**, is a **unilateral trade measure** under scrutiny at SB64, with India citing **Article 3.5 of the **UNFCCC** to challenge its **adverse effects on developing economies’ climate actions**.
- The **Sharm el-Sheikh Mitigation Work Programme (MWP)**, launched at **COP27 (2022)**, is set to conclude in **2026**, with negotiations ongoing on its **extension and scope**, including whether to introduce **new mitigation obligations** beyond the **Paris Agreement’s current mandates**.
- India’s stance on **Just Transition** is rooted in the principle of **Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC)**, as outlined in **Article 3.1 of the **UNFCCC**, which emphasizes that **developed countries must lead in emissions reductions**, including through **negative emissions technologies**.

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
- **Environmental & Political**:
  - **Adaptation vs. Mitigation**: India’s push for a **balanced Global Goal on Adaptation** highlights the **asymmetry in climate finance**, where **mitigation funds (e.g., Green Climate Fund)** disproportionately favor **developed nations’ tech transfers** over **adaptation needs** (e.g., **flood-resistant infrastructure in **Bihar**
### Syllabus Tag: General Studies

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Police officials patrol an area amid the ongoing violence in Pakistan-occupied Kashmir after violent clashes in Muzaffarabad, PoK on June 9, 2026.
                                          | Photo Credit: PTI
India on Tuesday (June 9, 2026) called on the global community to hold Pakistan accountable for the loss of lives in the police crackdown in Rawalkot, Pakistan Occupied Kashmir (PoK). Addressing the weekly press conference, Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal accused Pakistan of committing human rights abuses in PoK and said the police crackdown on Rawalkot protests that took place against the steep hike in prices of essential commodities, was “severe”.
“There are reports of severe police brutality in Pakistan Occupied Kashmir in which several protesters have been killed and many injured. We hope the international community will hold Pakistan accountable for its misdeeds and abuses,” said Mr. Jaiswal in the briefing. The statement came after media reports from the region informed that police attacked unarmed individuals who were protesting against rise in prices of essential commodities like flour. Protesters had also demanded that authorities should abolish the reservation of 12 seats for refugees in scheduled legislative election on July 27.
PoK has witnessed police crackdown on multiple occasions over the years including in 2025 when nine individuals were killed that included both protesters and police officials. The latest protest that started under the leadership of Jammu Kashmir Joint Awami Action Committee (JAAC) on June 5 however was triggered when local residents demanded subsidy on electricity  and flour and restoration of civil liberties in Muzaffarabad, Rawalkot, and Mirpur.
Mr. Jaiswal took note of a social media campaign that aims to tarnish the protesters and said, “We continue to see in this context, a pattern of fake news and videos emanating from Pakistan. It is a desperate attempt by Pakistan to cover-up its own fa
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**: **Police crackdown** in **Rawalkot, Pakistan-Occupied Kashmir (PoK)** on **June 5–9, 2026**, against protests over **price hikes** (flour, electricity) and **demands for civil liberties**, leading to **killed/injured protesters** and **internet blackout**.
- **Key actors**:
  - **India (MEA spokesperson **Randhir Jaiswal**)** accused **Pakistan** of **human rights abuses** and urged **global accountability**.
  - **Jammu Kashmir Joint Awami Action Committee (JAAC)** led protests in **Muzaffarabad, Rawalkot, Mirpur**.
  - **British MPs (All-Party Parliamentary Group on Kashmir, led by **Imran Hussain**)** demanded **UK government intervention** over crackdown’s impact on **British Kashmiris**.
- **Why it matters**:
  - **India’s stance** on **PoK’s autonomy vs Pakistan’s control** aligns with **UNSC resolutions** (e.g., **123** on Kashmir’s disputed status).
  - **Human rights violations** in PoK reflect **Pakistan’s governance failures** and **India’s diplomatic push** for **international scrutiny**.
  - **Protests’ demands** (subsidies, refugee quota abolition) highlight **economic distress** and **political disenfranchisement** in PoK.
- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for India’s use of diplomacy** to highlight **Pakistan’s abuses** (similar to **Balakot 2019** or **2022 PoK protests**).
  - **Internet blackout** raises **digital sovereignty** concerns under **IT Rules 2021** (India’s stance on **Article 19** vs **national security**).
  - **JAAC’s role** mirrors **2019–20 protests in J&K** (pre-**Article 370 abrogation**), showing **cross-LoC grievances**.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- **The **Ministry of External Affairs (MEA)** accused **Pakistan** of **human rights abuses** in **Rawalkot, PoK**, citing **police brutality** during protests against **price hikes** on **June 5–9, 2026**, as reported by **PTI**.
- **Protests in PoK** were led by the **Jammu Kashmir Joint Awami Action Committee (JAAC)**, demanding **subsidies on flour/electricity** and **abolition of 12 reserved seats for refugees** in the **July 27, 2026, PoK legislative elections**.
- **Internet blackout** in PoK has drawn **international concern**, with **British MPs (All-Party Parliamentary Group on Kashmir)** writing to **Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper** to address the crackdown’s impact on **UK citizens with PoK ties**.
- **PoK has seen repeated crackdowns**, including **2025 violence** where **9 people (protesters/police) died**, per **MEA statements** and **media reports**.
- **Pakistan’s response** includes a **social media campaign** to spread **fake news** about protesters, as noted by **MEA spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal**, reflecting a **pattern of disinformation** (similar to **2022 PoK protests**).

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
- **Political/Administrative**:
  - **PoK’s governance crisis**: Protests expose **Pakistan’s inability to address economic grievances** (flour/electricity subsidies) while **suppressing dissent** via **police crackdowns**. This mirrors **India’s challenges in J&K post-2019** (e.g., **Article 370 abrogation**, **public order laws**).
  - **Diplomatic leverage**: India’s **MEA statement** aligns with **UNSC Resolution 123 (1957)**, which calls for **Kashmir’s demilitarization and self-determination**—a stance Pakistan ignores. **UK’s role** (via MPs) shows **Western parliamentary interest** in **human rights in disputed territories** (e.g., **Hong Kong, Palestine**).

Jammu and Kashmir
on Tuesday (June 9, 2026), officials said.
A hand grenade exploded accidentally during routine handing over of equipment at a camp in Kamalkote in Uri, resulting in injuries to two jawans, the officials said.
They said the injured jawans were shifted to the Army's 92 Base Hospital where doctors declared them dead on arrival.
Inquest proceedings as required under law have been initiated, they added.
Srinagar-based Defence PRO said he would comment on the incident only after getting details from the concerned officials.

Of the 20 IITs, nine reported staff vacancies exceeding 35%.
                                          | Photo Credit: SUSHIL KUMAR VERMA
Over 15 lakh students appeared for the high-stakes Joint Entrance Examination this year to secure a seat in the country’s premier technical institutions. The competition has always been gruelling, particularly for the Indian Institutes of Technology (IITs), with roughly 80 students competing for every undergraduate seat in the 23 IITs.
However, data on vacant teaching positions in these institutes, obtained by
The Hindu
through the Right to Information (RTI) Act, raise questions on whether the students who surmount extraordinary challenges to get admitted receive the quality of education they deserve.
Published
- June 10, 2026 07:42 am IST
output:
### GK Gist:

  - **IITs (Indian Institutes of Technology)** – **23 institutes** under **Ministry of Education (MoE)**.
  - **RTI applicants** – **The Hindu** obtained data via **RTI Act, 2005**.
  - **Students** – **15 lakh+** appeared for **JEE 2026**, with **80:1 competition ratio** for undergraduate seats.
- **Why it matters**:
  - **Quality of education** in **NAAC-accredited** institutions under scrutiny.
  - **Brain drain risk** due to **faculty shortages** in **R&D-driven** institutions.
  - **Governance failure** in **public-funded** technical education.
- **Significance**:
  - **Human resource crisis** in **higher education** (linked to **NEP 2020**).
  - **Impact on India’s global competitiveness** in **STEM education**.
  - **Legal recourse** possible under **RTI Act** and **Right to Education (RTE) Act, 2009**.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- **The **Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005** was used by **The Hindu** to obtain data revealing that **9 out of 20 IITs** have **staff vacancies exceeding 35%**, with some exceeding **50%**.
- **The **Joint Entrance Examination (JEE) 2026** saw **over 15 lakh students** compete for **~25,000 undergraduate seats** across **23 IITs**, reflecting an **80:1 competition ratio**.
- **Under **NEP 2020**, **IITs** are classified as **"Institutions of Eminence"**, but **faculty shortages** threaten their **global rankings** (currently, **IIT Bombay** is ranked **#1 in India** and **#151 globally** by **QS 2025**).
- **The **Ministry of Education (MoE)** funds **IITs** under the **11th and 12th Five-Year Plans**, with **~₹50,000 crore** allocated for **higher education** in **2023-24**.
- **Section 12(1)(c) of the **RTE Act, 2009** mandates **quality education**, but **vacancies in teaching staff** may violate **Article 21 (Right to Life with Dignity)** if it leads to **substandard education**.

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
- **Social**:

- **Economic**:

  - **Industry-Academia Divide**: **NASSCOM** reports **60% of Indian IT firms** struggle to find **IIT-trained faculty** for **AI/ML roles**, despite **India being the 3rd largest startup hub** (after **US & UK**).

- **Political/Administrative**:
  - **Central vs. State Governance**: **IITs are centrally funded**, but **state governments** (e.g., **West Bengal, Tamil Nadu**) resist **faculty postings**, leading to **regional imbalances**.
  - **Bureaucratic Delays**: **Recruitment in IITs** is governed by **DST (Department of Science & Technology) norms**, but **clearance delays** (avg. **18-24 months**) exacerbate vacancies.
  - **NEP 2020 Implementation**: The policy’s **focus on "multidisciplinary education"** requires **more faculty**, but **budget constraints** (only **1.

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.

On Wednesday (June 10), the members of National Democratic Alliance (NDA) would meet in Delhi to mark the 12 years of the alliance being in power. Photo credit: PMO
Union Home Minister Amit Shah said
Prime Minister Narendra Modi
completed 4,398 days in office on Tuesday (June 9, 2026) and would surpass the record of former
Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru
on Wednesday (June 10).
He said Mr. Modi would have the distinction of serving the nation for the longest continuous period as an elected Prime Minister of India. He stated that during his tenure, Mr. Modi made public welfare the guiding principle of governance.
On Wednesday (June 10), the members of National Democratic Alliance (NDA) would meet in Delhi to mark the 12 years of the alliance being in power.
Mr. Shah launched the Land Port Management System (LPMS– ‘VINIMAY’) on Tuesday (June 9).
Addressing the programme, the Minister said that since 2014, moving beyond a security-centric approach, land ports were developed as the first line of defence for security, a means to facilitate trade, and a bridge for people-to-people connectivity. At the same time, land ports had played a significant role in the holistic development of border areas, promoting legitimate trade, and addressing challenges such as migration from border villages and districts. Easier movement of people across borders had strengthened mutual trust between the people of both countries and enhanced cultural exchange. He said that in a country like India, with its vast land borders, border management played a very important role.
Smart Borders
Mr. Shah said the Home Ministry was working on a four-pronged strategy to secure India’s borders under the concept of Smart Borders. The Land Port Management System had been designed keeping in mind the requirements of all stakeholders, while giving special emphasis to security. Together with the Smart Border initiative, this system would help to create an impregnable and secure border management framework. He
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**: **Prime Minister Narendra Modi** completed **4,398 days** in office on **June 9, 2026**, surpassing **Jawaharlal Nehru’s** record as the longest-serving **elected Prime Minister** of India.
  - **Key actors**: **Narendra Modi**, **Amit Shah**, **National Democratic Alliance (NDA)**.
  - **Why it matters**: Highlights **continuity in governance**, **public welfare policies**, and **NDA’s 12-year tenure**—key for **political history** and **governance analysis**.
  - **Significance**: Marks a **historical milestone** in Indian democracy, raising debates on **leadership longevity**, **policy consistency**, and **public trust**.

- **Event**: Launch of **Land Port Management System (LPMS– ‘VINIMAY’)** by **Union Home Minister Amit Shah** on **June 9, 2026**, under the **Smart Borders** initiative.
  - **Key actors**: **Amit Shah**, **Home Ministry**, **Border Security Force (BSF)**, **trade stakeholders**.
  - **Why it matters**: Part of **India’s border modernization**, linking **security**, **trade facilitation**, and **people-to-people connectivity**.
  - **Significance**: Aligns with **Act East Policy**, **Neighborhood First**, and **digital governance**—critical for **GS Paper 2 (Polity & Governance)** and **GS Paper 3 (Economy & Security)**.

---

### **Prelims Focus**
- **Prime Minister Narendra Modi** surpassed **Jawaharlal Nehru’s** record of **4,398 days** as the **longest-serving elected Prime Minister of India** on **June 10, 2026**, marking a historic milestone in **Indian political history**.
- The **Land Port Management System (LPMS– ‘VINIMAY’)**, launched by **Union Home Minister Amit Shah**, is a **digital platform** under the **Smart Borders** initiative to **eliminate 90% of paperwork** at land ports, reducing **truck waiting times by 40–60%** and **gate processing times by 22–35%**.
- **India’s land borders** play a **critical role in security, trade, and cultural exchange**, with **land ports** acting as the **"first line of defense"** while facilitating **legitimate trade and border area development**, as highlighted in the **Home Minister’s speech**.
- The **Smart Borders** strategy includes a **four-pronged approach** to **secure India’s borders**, integrating **cargo, passenger, and vehicle management** via **Single Electronic Window** and **Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR)** systems.
- The **National Democratic Alliance (NDA)** completed **12 years in power** in **2026**, with **Amit Shah** emphasizing **public welfare as the guiding principle** of governance under **Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s** tenure.

---

### **Mains Dimensions**
- **Political/Administrative**:
  - **Longevity in governance** raises questions about **democratic stability vs. leadership continuity**, especially in a **multi-party system**. Compare with **global examples** (e.g., **Singapore’s PAP**, **Japan’s LDP**) where **single-party dominance** has shaped policy consistency.
  - **Smart Borders** reflects **proactive border management**, but **implementation challenges** (e.g., **infrastructure gaps in remote areas**, **inter-agency coordination**) may hinder effectiveness. Link to **NITI Aayog’s border area development reports**.

- **Economic**:
  - **LPMS’s impact on trade**: Reduction in **logistics delays** (40–60% less waiting time) can **boost cross-border trade**, particularly with **neighboring countries like Nepal, Bangladesh, and Myanmar**. Aligns with **India’s Act East Policy** and **BBIN (Bangladesh-Bhutan-India-Nepal) motor vehicle agreement**.
  - **Tourism and cultural exchange**: Easier movement across borders can **enhance people-to-people connectivity**, but **security concerns** (e.g., **illegal migration, smuggling**) must be balanced. Compare with **EU’s Schengen Visa-free travel model**.

- **Security**:
  - **Shift from "security-centric" to "trade-and-connectivity" approach** at land ports reflects **India’s evolving border strategy**. However, **terrorism risks** (e.g., **cross-border infiltration**) persist, requiring **AI-driven surveillance** (e.g., **CBSE’s Smart Fencing**).

Indians have lost around ₹52,976 crore to cyber-enabled frauds over the past six years, with nearly 8% of the losses linked to “digital arrest” scams, the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) said on Tuesday (June 10, 2026).
The commission further warns that the frauds are causing not only financial devastation but also severe psychological trauma and violations of human rights.
Also Read :
Inside cyber-scam factories trapping Indians in Southeast Asia
According to a communique, an open house discussion on “Safeguarding Human Rights Against Digital Arrest Scams” was chaired by NHRC Chairperson Justice (retd) V. Ramasubramanian who said fraudsters were exploiting public fear of law enforcement agencies to extort money, while victims often found the recovery process more painful than the original loss.
NHRC Secretary General Bharat Lal said elderly citizens, including retired government officials, professionals, industrialists and bankers, were among the primary targets of such scams. Referring to information placed before the Supreme Court, he said more than 3,000 digital arrest frauds targeting senior citizens were reported last year alone.
“Cybercriminals are increasingly exploiting personal data obtained through data leaks and other sources to target victims, raising serious concerns about data privacy and protection. Evolving digital governance and financial systems enable them to commit fraud and move money faster uncaught,” said Mr Lal. He also urged strengthening safeguards to protect vulnerable groups, particularly senior citizens and ensuring effective support and remedies for victims of cyber fraud.

  - **Cross-border cybercrime** exposing gaps in **India’s digital governance** and **international cooperation**.
- **Significance**:
  - **Policy gap**: No **dedicated legal framework** for digital arrest scams; existing laws (e.g., **IT Act, 2000**) are inadequate.
  - **Vulnerable groups**: **Elderly (retired officials, professionals, industrialists)** are primary targets, reflecting **digital divide** and **trust deficit** in institutions.

Ramasubramanian**, reported that Indians lost **₹52,976 crore** to cyber-enabled frauds in the past six years (2020–2026), with **8%** attributed to **"digital arrest" scams**—a fraud tactic where victims are tricked into paying to avoid fake arrests.**

- **Over **3,000 digital arrest fraud cases** targeting **senior citizens** were reported in **2025**, as per data presented to the **Supreme Court**, exposing vulnerabilities in **digital literacy** and **financial protection mechanisms** for the elderly.**
- **Experts warned that **artificial intelligence (AI)**, **deepfakes**, and **synthetic identities** are poised to make cyber frauds **more convincing and harder to detect**, requiring **proactive cybersecurity policies** under the **Digital India** initiative.**

---

### **Mains Dimensions**

Bharatiya Mazdoor Sangh (BMS), labour wing of Rashtriya Swayamsewak Sangh (RSS) urged countries to ensure that artificial intelligence complements workers rather than replaces them, while highlighting India’s social security coverage and protections for gig workers at the International Labour Conference.
Speaking at the 114
th
international labour conference in Geneva on Tuesday (June 9), BMS delegate Bojji Surendran claimed India had received international recognition for achieving 64% social security coverage for its workforce, describing it as the highest in the world. He said the award was conferred by the International Social Security Association at the World Social Security Summit in Kuala Lumpur.
“At a time when global unemployment is projected to remain stable at 4.9%, the persistence of global job gaps affecting more than 400 million people serves as a stark reminder that employment alone is not enough,” the transcript of his speech shared by the organisation stated.
He urged that governments and international institutions must focus on decent work that provides fair wages, social protection, occupational safety, and opportunities for skills development.
He also expressed concern over
tensions in West Asia
, saying conflicts affect labour mobility, overseas employment, remittance flows, supply chains, and the sustainability of small businesses. Trade unions, he added, have a role in promoting dialogue and building confidence during periods of conflict.
Referring to the ILO Director-General’s report on artificial intelligence, Mr. Surendran said new technologies should be designed to assist humans rather than displace them. He welcomed recent efforts by the Union and some State governments to extend social security protections to gig and platform workers, calling them important steps toward inclusion in the digital economy.
Mr. Surendran also criticised the use of the term “labour market”, arguing that labour should be viewed as human capital rather than a c
output:

- **Event**: **Bharatiya Mazdoor Sangh (BMS)**, the labour wing of **Rashtriya Swayamsewak Sangh (RSS)**, addressed the **114th International Labour Conference (ILC)** in **Geneva (June 9, 2026)** to advocate for **AI-driven job augmentation**, **social security expansion**, and **decent work** globally.
- **Key actors**:

Chief Justice Sushrut Arvind Dharmadhikari and Justice G. Arul Murugan grant a fortnight’s time for the government to spell out the procedure. File.
                                          | Photo Credit: K. Pichumani
The Madras High Court has granted two weeks’ time for Tamil Nadu government to spell out the procedure it intends to follow for making appointments to the posts of Director, Deputy Directors and Assistant Directors of Prosecution across the State.
First Division Bench of Chief Justice Sushrut Arvind Dharmadhikari and Justice G. Arul Murugan accepted a request made by State Public Prosecutor R. John Sathyan to grant him a fortnight’s time to obtain necessary instructions from the State government.
The time was granted following a writ petition filed jointly by a group of five Additional and Assistant Public Prosecutors including Iden Isan, M. Santhiya, S. Sasireka, J.R. Hercules and M. Barath Rathna who had been appointed through the Tamil Nadu Public Service Commission (TNPSC).
The petitioners had urged the court to declare Section 20 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita (BNSS) as ultra vires the Constitution since it permits appointment of lawyers, who had not served as public prosecutors in trial courts, as Director, Deputy Director or Assistant Director of Prosecution.
The litigants insisted that only the prosecutors, who had been appointed through TNPSC recruitment process, must be promoted to the top posts in the directorate of prosecution and that the Director and Deputy Directors should not be appointed without the concurrence of the Chief Justice of the High Court.
Further, the petitioners had sought a direction to the the State government to frame statutory rules governing the appointments in the directorate of prosecution and not to make any appointment to the top posts in the interregnum from among candidates outside the regular cadre of prosecuting officers.
Though Mr. Isan had also filed another petition urging the High Court to iss
