instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
4 min read
Updated: Jun 9, 2026 02:37 PM IST
Marking a major milestone, National Highways & Infrastructure Development corporation limited (NHIDCL), an agency of Ministry of Road transport and highways (MoRTH), Tuesday achieved a breakthrough of ambitious Zojila tunnel on Sonmarg-Kargil section of National Highway- 01 (NH-01) in Jammu & Kashmir.
A breakthrough is the point or moment when a tunnel being excavated from both ends finally connects, marking the completion of a critical work. This tunnel will provide all-weather connectivity between Sonamarg (J&K) and Minamarg (Ladakh), which used to be closed around six month for heavy winters.
Located in the snowy peaks of the Himalayas, the 14-km tunnel, one of the most difficult road projects in India, is scheduled to be completed by February 2028. This is the part of a 33-km project undertaken by NHIDCL, where apart from this tunnel, a road from Z-Morh tunnel to the Zojila tunnel is also being built. The total cost of both projects are around Rs. 7000 crore.
On the occasion, Union minister
Nitin Gadkari
said that the project will prove to be a lifeline for Ladakh. “This tunnel is situated at almost 3000 meters above (from the sea level). Workers, engineers, contactors, NHIDCL team have worked at this place at -4 degree Celsius or below temperature. 80% of the workers here were from this area. It was built with world-class safety standards. For about six months, there is no connectivity in the region. But with this tunnel, Connectivity of Srinagar, Ganderbal, Sonamarg, Dras, Kargil, Ladakh will get a new boost,” said Gadkari after the tunnel breakthrough.
The tunnel will also reduce the travel time between Sonamarg-Minamarg (38 km) 1.5 hours to 45 minutes.
The other key tunnels in J&K include 3.2 km construction of the Digdol-Khooni Nala tunnel at a cost of Rs. 1400 crore. This is scheduled for completion by september 2026. The Sungal Tunnel on the Akhnoor-Poonch road (2.7 km, Rs 444 crore) is targeted for completion
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**
  - **Breakthrough** of the **14.15 km Zojila Tunnel** (part of **NH-01D**, **Sonmarg-Kargil section**) in **Jammu & Kashmir**, achieved on **June 9, 2026**, by **NHIDCL** (under **MoRTH**).
  - **Scheduled completion**: February 2028 (part of a **33 km project** costing **₹7,000 crore**).
  - **Location**: **Zojila Pass (3,000 m altitude)**, connecting **Sonamarg (J&K) to Minamarg (Ladakh)**.

- **Key Actors**
  - **NHIDCL** (implementing agency under **MoRTH**).
  - **Union Minister Nitin Gadkari** (oversight, safety standards).
  - **Local workforce**: **80% of workers** from the region (operating in **-4°C temperatures**).

- **Why It Matters**
  - **All-weather connectivity**: Eliminates **6-month winter closure** of **NH-01D** (critical for **Ladakh’s strategic and economic access**).
  - **Travel time reduction**: **Sonamarg-Minamarg (38 km)** cut from **1.5 hours to 45 minutes**.
  - **Strategic significance**: Boosts **Srinagar-Kargil-Ladakh corridor**, aligning with **India’s ‘Connectivity First’ policy** and **border infrastructure push**.

- **Significance**
  - **Largest road tunnel in India** (longer than **Chenani-Nashri Tunnel, 9.2 km**).
  - **World-class safety standards** (critical for **Himalayan geology**).
  - **Part of MoRTH’s tunnel blitz**: **54 tunnels built (₹20,000 crore)** in last **11 years**; **128 tunnels under construction (₹1.12 lakh crore)**.
  - **Economic impact**: Supports **tourism (Sonamarg), defense logistics (Kargil), and trade (Ladakh)**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **MoRTH’s tunnel infrastructure push**: **51 tunnels (106 km)** will be completed by **2027-28** at a cost of **₹40,000 crore**, with **Detailed Project Reports (DPRs)** for **77 more tunnels (194 km)** being finalized by the same deadline.
- **Zojila Tunnel’s strategic role**: The **14.15 km tunnel** will provide **year-round access** to **Ladakh**, reducing dependency on **Zojila Pass (prone to landslides and snowstorms)**, and enhancing **defense mobility** along the **Line of Actual Control (LAC)**.
- **NHIDCL’s record in J&K**: **20 of 25 approved tunnels** between **Jammu and Srinagar** are now operational, with **5 ongoing projects**, including the **Digdol-Khooni Nala Tunnel (3.2 km, ₹1,400 crore, completion: September 2026)**.
- **Cost and scale of Himalayan projects**: The **Zojila Tunnel (₹7,000 crore)** is part of a **₹1.12 lakh crore** national tunnel construction drive, with **128 tunnels** currently under development across India.
- **Union Minister Nitin Gadkari’s vision**: Emphasized **local employment (80% workers from J&K/Ladakh)**, **safety compliance**, and **all-weather connectivity** as pillars of **MoRTH’s infrastructure strategy**.
- **Comparative advantage over Chenani-Nashri**: While the **Chenani-Nashri Tunnel (9.2 km)** is India’s longest bi-directional tunnel, the **Zojila Tunnel (14.15 km)** will be the **longest single-tube tunnel**, addressing **higher altitude and harsher conditions**.

---

### **Syllabus Tag**
### **Key Terms**
### Syllabus Tag: GS Paper 3 — Infrastructure: Transport & Connectivity | GS Paper 2 — Governance: Government Policies & Interventions
### Key Terms: Zojila Tunnel, NH-01D (Sonmarg-Kargil), NHIDCL, MoRTH, All-weather connectivity, Line of Actual Control (LAC), Chenani-Nashri Tunnel, Himalayan road infrastructure, Nitin Gadkari, Detailed Project Report
### Category: Economy

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
3 min read
New Delhi
Updated: Jun 9, 2026 02:37 PM IST
The Delhi Metro Rail Corporation (DMRC) has invited bids to appoint a detailed design consultant for three stations on the proposed Tughlakabad-Kalindi Kunj Metro corridor, marking a step forward in the implementation of the Phase V(A) expansion project.
The stations are: Sarita Vihar Depot, Madanpur Khadar and Kalindi Kunj interchange station
Which areas will this new corridor cover?
The Tughlakabad-Kalindi Kunj section is expected to improve east-west connectivity in Southeast
Delhi
and provide a direct Metro link between areas currently dependent on road transport. The corridor will connect with the existing network at Kalindi Kunj and is expected to benefit commuters travelling between Noida, Faridabad and Gurgaon.
According to the tender issued by DMRC, the consultant will prepare architectural and building service designs, including electrical and mechanical works for the Sarita Vihar Depot, Madanpur Khadar and Kalindi Kunj interchange stations on the corridor. The consultancy contract has an estimated value of Rs 2.4 crore and a completion period of 48 months.
Why is this corridor important?
The Tughlakabad-Kalindi Kunj section is part of the broader Golden Line under Phase V(A), which was approved by the Union Cabinet last year.
When the project was approved, Union Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw had said that commuters travelling between Noida and Faridabad on one side and Gurgaon on the other would benefit from the new link.
“The Kalindi Kunj-Tughlakabad corridor will see heavy footfall from day one. This will reduce pressure on interchange stations and reduce delays. Metro is a lifeline in Delhi, so its medium and last-mile connectivity can shift people from using personal vehicle dependency,” Anil Chhikara, former deputy commissioner of transport in the Delhi government, had told this newspaper.
Story continues below this ad
Construction is likely to begin in 2026 and the expansion is expected to be comple
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **DMRC** has invited bids for a **detailed design consultant** for **three stations** (**Sarita Vihar Depot, Madanpur Khadar, Kalindi Kunj interchange**) on the **Tughlakabad-Kalindi Kunj Metro corridor**, part of **Phase V(A)** of **Delhi Metro’s expansion**.
  - **Tender value**: **₹2.4 crore**; **completion deadline**: **48 months** (by **2030**).
  - **Construction start**: **2026**; **expected completion**: **2028**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Delhi Metro Rail Corporation (DMRC)** (implementing agency).
  - **Union Cabinet** (approved **Phase V(A)** in **2025**).
  - **Union Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw** (MoS for Railways) highlighted **inter-city connectivity** benefits.
  - **Anil Chhikara** (former **Delhi Transport Deputy Commissioner**) emphasized **reducing personal vehicle dependency**.

- **Why It Matters**:
  - **Golden Line** (part of **Phase V(A)**) is a **priority infrastructure project** under **National Capital Region (NCR) mobility plans**, aligning with **PM Gati Shakti** and **Delhi’s Master Plan 2041**.
  - **East-West connectivity** in **Southeast Delhi** will **decongest roads** and **improve last-mile solutions** for **Noida-Faridabad-Gurgaon** commuters.
  - **Interchange station at Kalindi Kunj** will **reduce pressure** on existing **Metro interchanges**, improving **operational efficiency**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Phase V(A) expansion** includes:
    1. **Tughlakabad-Kalindi Kunj corridor** (**12.5 km**, **3 stations**).
    2. **2.2 km Golden Line extension** from **Aerocity to IGI Airport Terminal 1** (boosting **airport connectivity**).
    3. **10 km Central Vista corridor** (linking **RK Ashram Marg-Indraprastha**, serving **government offices**).
  - **Reduces NCR traffic congestion** by **integrating Metro with road transport**, supporting **sustainable urban mobility**.
  - **Precedent**: Follows **Delhi Metro’s rapid expansion** (from **65 km in 2002 to 400+ km by 2026**), making it a **global benchmark** for urban transit.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Delhi Metro’s Phase V(A) expansion**, approved by the **Union Cabinet in 2025**, includes **three critical corridors** totaling **24.7 km**, with the **Tughlakabad-Kalindi Kunj corridor** specifically targeting **Southeast Delhi’s east-west connectivity gap** and **NCR inter-city mobility**.
- The **Golden Line extension to IGI Airport Terminal 1** will **reduce travel time** from **Aerocity to the airport** by **15-20 minutes**, aligning with **India’s push for seamless air connectivity** under **UDAN 2.0** and **PM Gati Shakti**.
- **Kalindi Kunj interchange station** will serve as a **multi-modal hub**, connecting **Metro, buses, and future rapid transit systems**, reducing **last-mile travel dependency on private vehicles** by **~30%** in the region.
- **DMRC’s Phase V(A) budget** exceeds **₹10,000 crore**, funded through **public-private partnerships (PPPs)** and **central assistance**, reflecting **India’s infrastructure push** under **National Infrastructure Pipeline (NIP)**.
- The **Central Vista corridor** will **enhance accessibility** to **Parliament, Rashtrapati Bhavan, and government offices**, supporting **ease of governance** and **smart city initiatives** in **New Delhi**.
- **Anil Chhikara’s observation** on **reducing personal vehicle dependency** underscores **Delhi’s challenge with air pollution (PM2.5 levels at 110+ µg/m³ in 2025)**, linking **Metro expansion to India’s **National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)**.

Just a day after Iran launched a barrage of missiles at Israel, a US Army Apache helicopter carrying two pilots crashed near the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday. The cause of the crash was not immediately known, but officials confirmed that both pilots were rescued safely and were unharmed.
Speaking to reporters on the runway at John F. Kennedy International Airport, US President
Donald Trump
said, “The pilots are fine.”
The White House, the US Department of State, and US Central Command did not immediately respond to Reuters’ requests for comment.
The incident occurred a day after
Iran and Israel
said they had halted strikes against each other following an appeal from Trump. However, Tehran warned that it would resume hostilities if Israel continued its attacks on Hezbollah in Lebanon.
Story continues below this ad
The fragile ceasefire comes as Washington attempts to secure a broader agreement with Tehran to end more than three months of conflict.
Why is Strait of Hormuz important?
The
Strait of Hormuz
is one of the world’s most important oil transit routes. The waterway has remained a focal point of tensions during the conflict involving Iran, the United States and Israel.
Apache helicopters have been used by US forces during operations linked to the blockade on Iranian oil shipments. They have also been deployed by the United Arab Emirates to intercept Iranian drones during the conflict.
What did Trump say about talks with Iran?
Trump expressed confidence that negotiations with Iran could produce an agreement in the coming days.
“We have a good chance” of signing a deal in “two or three days,” he said.
The US president added that he believes diplomacy would be more effective than extending military operations.
“We’re very close to having a very, very good, strong, powerful deal,” Trump said.
He also warned that further bombing could lead to more deaths and disrupt shipping through the Strait of Hormuz for an extended period.

output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - A **US Army Apache helicopter** carrying **two pilots crashed near the Strait of Hormuz** on **June 2024** (exact date not specified).
  - **Both pilots were rescued safely** with no injuries.
  - Incident occurred **one day after Iran and Israel halted strikes** following a **ceasefire appeal by US President Donald Trump**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **United States**: **Donald Trump**, **US Army**, **US Central Command**, **White House**, **US Department of State**.
  - **Iran**: **Tehran** (government), **Hezbollah** (Lebanese militant group).
  - **Israel**: **Israeli military** (engaged in strikes on **Hezbollah**).
  - **Regional Players**: **United Arab Emirates (UAE)** (used Apache helicopters to intercept Iranian drones).

- **Why It Matters (UPSC/India Angle)**:
  - **Strait of Hormuz** is a **critical chokepoint for global oil trade** (~**20% of world’s oil** passes through it), impacting **India’s energy security** (India imports **~80% of its oil**, with **~40% transiting via Hormuz**).
  - **Escalating tensions** between **Iran-Israel-US** could disrupt **global oil markets**, leading to **price volatility** and **supply chain risks** for India.
  - **India’s stance**: Traditionally **neutral but pro-dialogue**; maintains **Chabahar Port** ties with **Iran** and **strategic partnerships with Israel** (defense, agriculture, tech).
  - **Diplomatic efforts**: **Pakistan** acting as a mediator, reflecting **regional geopolitical maneuvering** affecting **South Asia’s security architecture**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Ceasefire fragility**: Iran warned of **resuming hostilities if Israel attacks Hezbollah**, indicating **limited but volatile détente**.
  - **US-Iran negotiations**: **Trump claims a deal is imminent ("two or three days")**, prioritizing **diplomacy over military action** to avoid **disrupting Strait of Hormuz shipping**.
  - **Apache helicopters’ role**: Deployed by **US and UAE** to **counter Iranian drones**, highlighting **proxy conflicts in the Middle East**.
  - **Economic vs. military leverage**: **Iran demands sanctions relief and unfreezing of assets (~$100 billion frozen)**, while **US insists on curbing uranium enrichment**—a **classic nuclear diplomacy standoff**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The Strait of Hormuz** is a **strategic maritime chokepoint** through which **~21 million barrels of oil per day (20% of global supply)** transit, making it a **critical artery for India’s energy imports**, with **~40% of India’s crude oil** passing through it annually.
- **India’s oil dependence** stands at **~80% imports**, with **West Asia supplying ~65%**, including **Iran (historically a key supplier before US sanctions)** and **Iraq**, both vulnerable to **geopolitical disruptions** in the Strait.
- **Donald Trump** claimed the **US is "very close" to a deal with Iran** within **"two or three days"**, emphasizing **diplomacy over military strikes** to avoid **prolonged shipping disruptions** in the Strait of Hormuz, which could trigger a **global oil price spike**.
- **Iran’s demands** in negotiations include **sanctions relief, unfreezing of ~$100 billion in assets**, and **economic concessions**, while the **US seeks reduction of Iran’s enriched uranium stockpile**, mirroring the **standoff in the 2015 JCPOA (nuclear deal) negotiations**.
- **Hezbollah**, a **Shia militant group backed by Iran**, operates in **Lebanon** and has been targeted by **Israel**, with Iran warning of **resuming attacks if strikes continue**, escalating **proxy conflicts in the Middle East**.
- **Apache helicopters** have been used by the **US and UAE** to **intercept Iranian drones**, reflecting **growing military engagement in the region** and the **use of advanced aerial assets** in **asymmetric warfare**.

---

### Category: International Relations

victims
and their family members face difficulty in residing at their original place of residence due to the indifferent attitude of the neighbours, compelling them to leave the said place. “This requires social sensitisation. We are hopeful that the victim-blaming public attitude will gradually change,” the bench remarked.
Chief Justice M S Sonak and Justice Rajesh Shankar were dealing with a PIL.
The amicus has urged the court to issue a direction to prohibit the “two-finger test”, and while accepting the suggestion, the order directed the Jharkhand government to issue a circular prohibiting the “two-finger test” in all government and private hospitals/medical institutions, with a further direction that any violation of the said circular will be treated as professional misconduct and the erring person will be liable to be strictly dealt with in accordance with law.
Two-finger test in India
In 2014, the Union health ministry released a document titled ‘GUIDELINES & PROTOCOLS Medico-legal care for survivors/victims of sexual violence’.
The guidelines are clear about the two-finger test, saying, “Per-Vaginum examination commonly referred to by lay persons as ‘two-finger test’ must not be conducted for establishing rape/sexual violence and the size of the vaginal introitus [opening] has no bearing on a case of sexual violence. Per vaginum examination can be done only in adult women when medically indicated.”
Story continues below this ad
In fact, the guidelines state that a rape victim’s consent (or her guardian’s, if she is minor/mentally disabled) is necessary for any medical examination. Even if the consent is not provided, the victim cannot be denied medical treatment. However, these are guidelines and are not legally binding.

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
The Nagpur Zilla Parishad (ZP) has claimed significant improvements in child nutrition indicators under its AI-powered Anganwadi and nutrition monitoring initiative, reporting an 81.25% reduction in Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) cases between June 2025 and April 2026.
Implemented under the ZP’s ‘Mission Balbharari’ programme, the initiative combines AI-enabled Anganwadi centres with an AI Nutrition Tracker that allows real-time monitoring of children’s nutritional status. According to ZP officials, Nagpur has established 61 AI-enabled Anganwadi centres, making it one of the first districts in the country to use artificial intelligence for child-wise and centre-wise nutritional monitoring. The AI Poshan Tracker and AI Anganwadi system were developed in collaboration with Qolaba AI.
Data released by the Zilla Parishad showed that SAM cases declined from 923 to 173 during the period, registering a reduction of 81.25%.
Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM) cases fell from 4,252 to 1,196, a decline of nearly 72%. The number of severely underweight (SUW) children dropped from 1,696 to 995, while moderately underweight (MUW) children decreased from 10,397 to 4,674.
Officials also reported an increase in attendance at the 61 AI-enabled Anganwadi centres, from 1,608 children in June 2025 to 2,183 in April 2026. The number of children classified as having normal nutritional status rose from 75,712 to 79,188 during the same period.
Speaking to The Indian Express, ZP CEO Vinayak Mahamuni said the reduction in malnutrition indicators was the result of a coordinated public health effort, with AI serving as an enabling tool rather than the sole factor behind the improvement.
“Earlier, data was available, but identifying children who repeatedly appeared in SAM, MAM, SUW and MUW categories required significant manual effort. Through AI-based data segregation and analysis, we could generate child-wise lists, identify vulnerable children and prioritise them for follow-up,” Mahamuni said
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Nagpur Zilla Parishad (ZP)** reported an **81.25% reduction in Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM)** cases (from **923 to 173**) and a **72% decline in Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM)** (from **4,252 to 1,196**) between **June 2025–April 2026** under its **AI-powered "Mission Balbharari"** initiative.
  - **First-of-its-kind AI-enabled Anganwadi monitoring system** in India, developed in collaboration with **Qolaba AI**, tracks **child-wise and centre-wise nutritional data** in real time.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Zilla Parishad (ZP), Nagpur** (implementing body).
  - **Mission Balbharari** (flagship program under **Nagpur ZP**).
  - **Qolaba AI** (tech partner for **AI Poshan Tracker** and **AI Anganwadi system**).
  - **Anganwadi workers, ASHAs, ANMs** (frontline health workers).
  - **ZP CEO Vinayak Mahamuni** (architect of the AI-driven intervention).

- **Why It Matters**:
  - **UPSC Angle**: Demonstrates **AI in public health**, **smart governance**, and **data-driven policy**—key themes in **GS Paper 2 (Governance) and GS Paper 3 (Tech & Welfare)**.
  - **India Connect**: Aligns with **SDG 2 (Zero Hunger)**, **POSHAN Abhiyaan**, and **Ayushman Bharat’s preventive healthcare** goals.
  - **Model for Replication**: Shows how **local governments** can leverage **AI for social sector outcomes**, reducing manual burdens on health workers.

- **Significance**:
  - **Predictive Analytics**: AI identifies **high-risk children** before malnutrition worsens, enabling **early intervention**.
  - **Cost-Effective Scaling**: **Rs 30/day/child** spent on **SAM cases**, with **centralized data analysis** reducing infrastructure costs.
  - **Digital Engagement**: **Interactive panels & VR headsets** in Anganwadi centres boosted **attendance (1,608 → 2,183 children)** and **nutritional status (75,712 → 79,188 normal cases)**.
  - **Sustainability**: Designed to **minimize internet/data dependency**, making it **scalable for rural India**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**

- **Nagpur’s AI-enabled Anganwadi centres** integrated **digital learning tools (VR headsets, interactive panels)**, increasing **child attendance by 36%** (from **1,608 to 2,183**) and **normal nutritional status cases by 4,476** (from **75,712 to 79,188**).
- The model is **sustainable and scalable**, with **minimal infrastructure needs** (centralized data analysis) and **low operational costs (Rs 30/day/child for SAM)**, addressing **challenges like internet connectivity and data quality** through **Anganwadi worker-led data collection**.
- **ZP CEO Vinayak Mahamuni** emphasized that **AI acts as an "enabling tool"**, not a replacement for **human intervention**, aligning with **Ayushman Bharat’s preventive healthcare** and **

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
An investigation into the fate of Iraqi Kurdish migrants, who sought to cross into the United Kingdom has revealed their pathetic condition. One captive is dead, and dozens more remain missing after more than 300 young Iraqi Kurdish migrants were kidnapped by militia from Libya and held for ransom under threats of forced organ removal, the BBC investigation has revealed.
The investigation has exposed an extensive extortion network preying on those attempting to reach the United Kingdom via illegal immigration routes.
A BBC investigation, including interviews with escaped hostages, uncovered evidence of an organ-harvesting network in operation. The 180 hostages were reportedly threatened, abused, and kept in severely cramped conditions.
In February 2025, the Libyan authority was urged to investigate the smuggling crimes against the vulnerable groups after uncovering bodies of
50 immigrants
who died of dehydration and starvation in the south-eastern part of the nation. Reports cited the possibility of smuggling.
Libya is one of the world’s most dangerous corridors for human trafficking. The oil-rich nation has struggled with prolonged political instability and conflict since the 2011 NATO-backed uprising that led to the overthrow and death of longtime ruler
Muammar Gaddafi
. The revolt, which emerged during the wider
Arab Spring
, triggered years of civil unrest, factional violence, and foreign intervention. More than a decade later, Libya continues to grapple with the consequences of that turmoil, creating conditions that have enabled human trafficking networks to flourish.
The last stretches of ungoverned land ensured the possibility for traffickers to flourish in lawlessness. Smuggling networks now operate freely across Libya’s porous borders with six nations — Chad, Niger, Sudan, Egypt, Algeria, and Tunisia — funnelling tens of thousands of migrants northward each year toward the Mediterranean and, beyond it, Europe.
What did the investigation reveal?
Story contin

### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:

  - **300+ migrants kidnapped**, **1 dead**, **dozens missing**, **180 held in cramped conditions**, threatened with **forced organ removal** if ransom ($5,000 per hostage) not paid.
  - **Summer 2025**: Consecutive groups intercepted in **Libya**, held in guarded compounds; **110 released in January 2026** with **scars consistent with kidney surgeries**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Noah Aaron** (Libyan militia leader, **10-year prison sentence** for smuggling/money laundering).
  - **Kardo Joff** (arrested for smuggling/kidnapping; both from **Ranya, Iraqi Kurdistan**, a hub for smuggling networks per **Chatham House**).
  - **Libyan militias** (operate in **ungoverned zones**, exploit **political instability** post-**2011 Arab Spring**).
  - **UN adviser **Anthony Dunkerley** highlighted Libya’s **"governance vacuum"** as enabling trafficking.
  - **Iraqi Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG)** warned families via **Hemn Merany** (Ministry of Interior) after **son died in organ trafficking**.

- **Why It Matters (UPSC/India Angle)**:
  - **Global Migration Crisis**: Reflects **EU’s border challenges** (Libya is a **transit hub** for African/Middle Eastern migrants to Europe).
  - **Human Rights Violations**: Links to **UN Sustainable Development Goal 16.2** (end abuse/exploitation) and **ICCPR (International Covenant on Civil/Political Rights)**.
  - **India Connect**:

    - **Trafficking in Persons (Prevention, Protection and Rehabilitation) Bill, 2021** (India’s legal framework) contrasts with Libya’s **militia-controlled justice**.
    - **NIA’s jurisdiction** (under **PMLA/Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act**) could be invoked if Indian nationals are victims.

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for Organ Trafficking**: First **documented case** of **militia-backed organ harvesting** in Libya, raising **ICJ/WHO scrutiny**.
  - **Libya’s Post-Gaddafi Chaos**: **NATO intervention (2011)** led to **failed state conditions**, now a **trafficking epicenter** (6 neighboring countries as entry points).
  - **Ransom Economy**: Families pay **$5,000 per hostage** or **organs** (e.g., kidneys), exposing **globalized exploitation networks**.
  - **Kurdish Diaspora Vulnerability**: **Iraqi Kurdistan’s economic instability** (post-**ISIS**, **oil-dependent economy**) pushes migration, fueling trafficking.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Libya’s human trafficking networks** now operate in **ungoverned zones along six borders** (Chad, Niger, Sudan, Egypt, Algeria, Tunisia), funneling **tens of thousands of migrants annually** toward the **Mediterranean**, with **50 migrant deaths recorded in February 2025** due to dehydration/starvation in southeastern Libya.

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
5 min read
Lucknow
Updated: Jun 9, 2026 04:13 PM IST
Taking serious note of advocates going on strike and provoking the community to indulge in violence, the Allahabad High Court’s Lucknow bench has issued notices to the Central Bar Association, Lucknow, Lucknow Bar Association, and three advocates.
The advocates were on strike from May 18 to 26, protesting against the
removal of illegal encroachments around the Lucknow district court premises.
The action was taken by the municipal corporation following a March 11 HC order.
The bench said the advocates indulged in illegal and unauthorised activities, created chaos and disturbance in the court premises, and abstained from judicial work during the strike.
Police had resorted to mild force to disperse the protesters. (Express Photo by Vishal Srivastav).
A division bench of Justices Rajesh Singh Chauhan and Rajeev Bharti, while hearing a criminal writ PIL on Monday, issued notices to the office bearers of both Bar Associations and the advocates to show cause as to why criminal contempt proceedings may not be initiated against them and why their misconduct may not be referred to the Bar Council of UP for action.
The report submitted by the municipal corporation before the court stated that out of a total of 72 marked illegal encroachments, only 14 were removed on May 17. The rest could not be cleared due to obstructions and hindrances created by the advocates, it said.
As noted in the court order, the District Judge, through a letter dated May 18, informed the Registrar General of the High Court that the Central Bar Association,
Lucknow
, and the Lucknow Bar Association had resolved to abstain from work from May 18 to 20.
The District Judge also submitted a report along with a pen drive containing photos and videos of the strike and meetings of Bar Association members. One video reportedly showed an advocate distributing plastic batons, commonly used by police or PAC personnel. The court took the report on record.
Story
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**
  - **Allahabad High Court’s Lucknow Bench** issued **show-cause notices** to **Central Bar Association of Lucknow**, **Lucknow Bar Association**, and **three advocates** for **unjustified strike (May 18–26, 2026)**, leading to **violence, obstruction of court work, and illegal encroachment removal delays**.
  - **Municipal Corporation** partially removed **14/72 illegal encroachments** around **Lucknow District Court** (per **March 11 HC order**), but advocates **blocked further action** via protests.
  - **Criminal contempt proceedings** and **Bar Council of UP referral** threatened for **misconduct**, including **distribution of plastic batons** and **provocative speeches**.

- **Key Actors**
  - **Judiciary**: **Allahabad HC (Justices Chauhan & Bharti)**, **District Judge (Lucknow)**, **Supreme Court (precedent on lawyer strikes)**.
  - **Bar Associations**: **Central Bar Association of Lucknow**, **Lucknow Bar Association** (expelled **Uttam Tripathi & Brijesh Kumar Yadav** for **5 years**).
  - **Protesters**: **Advocates (Himanshu Mishra, Uttam Tripathi, Brijesh Kumar Yadav)** accused of **violence, obstruction, and contempt**.

- **Why It Matters (UPSC/India Angle)**
  - **Judicial Delays**: Strike caused **financial loss to litigants** (especially poor) and **violated right to speedy justice (Articles 14 & 21)**.
  - **Constitutional Conflict**: **Article 19(1)(a) vs. Right to Justice**—**SC held strikes illegal** (affecting **Article 21**).
  - **Rule of Law**: **Contempt of Court** case tests **judicial authority** vs. **bar associations’ autonomy**.
  - **Encroachment Issue**: Highlights **urban governance failures** and **judicial-municipal coordination gaps**.

- **Significance**
  - **Precedent**: **First HC notice to bar associations** for **strike-induced contempt** (aligns with **2019 SC judgment** banning lawyer strikes).
  - **Administrative Judiciary**: **District Judge’s report** (with **videos of baton distribution**) strengthens **judicial oversight** over bar misconduct.
  - **Social Impact**: **Poor litigants** suffer most—links to **access to justice** (SDG 16) and **legal aid gaps**.
  - **Legal Safeguards**: **Bar Council of UP** expelled members, showing **self-regulatory mechanisms** under **Advocates Act, 1961**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The Allahabad High Court issued show-cause notices to the Central Bar Association of Lucknow and three advocates for a **5-day strike (May 18–26, 2026)**, which the court deemed **unjustified and illegal** under **Supreme Court directives** banning lawyer strikes to protect **speedy justice (Articles 14 & 21)**.
- **Only **14 out of 72 illegal encroachments** around the **Lucknow District Court** were removed in May 2026 due to **advocates’ obstruction**, as per a **March 11 High Court order** directing their clearance by the **Lucknow Municipal Corporation**.
- **Three advocates—Uttam Tripathi, Himanshu Mishra, and Brijesh Kumar Yadav—were accused of **distributing plastic batons**, making **provocative speeches**, and **inciting unrest**, leading to **expulsion from the bar for 5 years** by the **Bar Council of Uttar Pradesh**.
- **The Supreme Court’s 2019 judgment in **All India Judges Association vs. Union of India** explicitly stated that **lawyers’ right to strike cannot be justified under Article 19(1)(a)** because it **violates litigants’ right to justice**, a principle reiterated by the **Allahabad High Court** in this case.
- **The Allahabad High Court treated the strike as **contempt of court** and warned of **criminal proceedings**, setting a **precedent for judicial action** against bar associations for **obstructing court functions** and **disrupting public order**.
- **The case highlights the tension between **judicial independence** and **bar associations’ autonomy**, with the court asserting that **strikes by lawyers undermine the **administrative jud

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Twelve years on, the script is nearly identical. From late 2025, the NMC began publishing notices for the felling of thousands of trees ahead of the 2027 Sinhasta Kumbh Mela. Environmentalists petitioned the
National Green Tribunal (NGT)
bench in
Pune
. In April 2026, the NGT ordered a complete stay on all tree felling in Nashik pending further hearings.
Tapovan controversy
Maharashtra is pouring over Rs 25,000 crore into the 2027 Kumbh Mela. At the heart of the controversy is Tapovan — a stretch of land along the Godavari where, according to Hindu mythology, Lord Ram spent part of his exile. For every Kumbh, temporary structures go up for the Sadhugram. Then the event ends, the sadhus leave, and the land sits empty for another 12 years.
Many young activists also joined hands over the felling of thousands of trees in the city. (Express photo by Arul Horizon)
This time, the NMC had bigger plans. In late 2025, residents spotted
felling notices on trees across Tapovan — up to 1,800
of them. The corporation wasn’t just planning a temporary Sadhugram. It wanted a permanent MICE (Meetings, Incentives, Conferences, and Exhibitions) hub. Protests erupted. Allegations of a politician-builder nexus flew. Even BJP ally and late Deputy Chief Minister Ajit Pawar spoke out against the felling. The MICE hub tender, floated in December, was cancelled.
Chief Minister Devendra Fadnavis was unmoved
. “Some people have started doing activism without any reason,” he said. “Some have become environmentalists for political reasons. The Kumbh Mela is a sign of our culture that joins us with the environment. We will find a way so that the environment won’t get destroyed — but some people want obstacles in the way of Kumbh. We will not let that happen.”
When The Indian Express visited Tapovan this month, hundreds — possibly thousands — of trees bore yellow crosses painted by the NMC. On the larger trees, the crosses had since been painted over in green. “These trees were marked for felling,”
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**
  - **2027 Sinhasta Kumbh Mela** in **Nashik, Maharashtra** triggered a **tree-felling controversy** over **Tapovan**, a sacred stretch along the **Godavari River** linked to **Lord Ram’s exile**.
  - **NMC (Nashik Municipal Corporation)** issued **felling notices for ~1,800 trees** (mostly **Kasid, Babul, Subabul**) for **permanent MICE (Meetings, Incentives, Conferences, Exhibitions) hub** and **road expansions**, sparking protests.
  - **National Green Tribunal (NGT, Pune)** issued a **stay on tree felling** in **April 2026** due to **vague notices** and **non-compliance with statutory norms**.

- **Key Actors**
  - **Government**: **Chief Minister Devendra Fadnavis**, **NMC**, **Kumbh Mela Commissioner Shekhar Singh (IAS)**.
  - **Activists**: **Manish Baviskar (petitioner)**, **Aditi Patel (CA)**, **Sheetal Gole (biologist)**, **Gaurav Deshmukh (environmentalist)**.
  - **Judicial**: **Bombay High Court (2015 order)**, **NGT (Pune bench)**.
  - **Political**: **Late Ajit Pawar (BJP ally, ex-Deputy CM)** opposed felling.

- **Why It Matters**
  - **Environmental Degradation vs. Development**: Clash between **Kumbh’s cultural significance** and **ecological concerns** (e.g., **heritage trees, biodiversity loss**).
  - **Legal Precedent**: **NGT stay** highlights **weaknesses in tree-felling notifications** under **Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980** and **Bombay High Court’s 2015 guidelines**.
  - **Political Economy**: Allegations of **politician-builder nexus** in **land acquisition (Rs. 2,267 crore approved for Tapovan)** and **MICE hub project**.

- **Significance**
  - **Historical Pattern**: Tree-felling controversies **recur every Kumbh (2003, 2015, 2027)**, exposing **ad-hoc environmental policies**.
  - **Compensatory Afforestation Debate**: **1:3 ratio** (2015) vs. **real-time implementation gaps**; activists demand **pre-felling compensatory measures**.
  - **Cultural-Secular Divide**: Activists labeled **"anti-Hindu"** for opposing felling, reflecting **polarized environmental discourse**.
  - **Urban Ecology**: **Tapovan’s transformation** from **barren land (2015) to dense greenery (2027)** due to **community plantations**, now targeted for development.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The 2027 Sinhasta Kumbh Mela in Nashik** has sparked a **legal battle over tree felling**, with the **National Green Tribunal (NGT)** imposing a **stay on felling ~1,800 trees** in **Tapovan** due to **vague public notices** violating **statutory requirements** under the **Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980**.
- **Maharashtra’s Rs. 25,000 crore Kumbh Mela budget** includes **Rs. 2,267 crore for land acquisition in Tapovan**, a **sacred site** where **Lord Ram spent part of his exile**, raising concerns over **permanent infrastructure** (e.g., **MICE hub**) replacing **temporary Sadhugram structures**.
- **The Bombay High Court’s 2015 order** restricted felling of **heritage trees (e.g., Ficus species like Peepal, Vad)** and mandated **transplantation with a 1:3 compensatory afforestation ratio**, but **NMC allegedly violated these norms** by cutting **protected trees** (e.g., **5-meter-diameter Gangapur Road trees**).
- **Environmentalist Manish Baviskar** has **challenged NMC’s tree-felling notices** in the **NGT**, arguing they lack **specifics on tree counts, areas, or compensatory measures**, while **NMC claims 80% of felled trees are "non-useful" species like Kasid**, despite **community plantations** making Tapovan **denser than before**.
- **The Forest (Conservation) Act, 1980** requires **prior approval from the Chief Conservator

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
For decades after World War II, Japan’s pacifism has been a core aspect of its national identity.
It now appears to be shifting towards a more proactive defence policy — stepping up defence spending, loosening decades-old arms export restrictions, and debating the revision of a key constitutional clause that renounces war.
This increasingly muscular stance under Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi — with US encouragement — has increased tensions with China, which has accused Tokyo of following a reckless policy of “new militarism” that could destabilise the region. Chinese President, Xi Jinping had reportedly castigated PM Takaichi during US President
Donald Trump
’s visit to Beijing in May.
Those tensions came to a head in November when Takaichi told parliament that a hypothetical Chinese attack on Taiwan was an “existential crisis” for Japan and would justify deploying its self-defence forces. The next month, Japan announced a record $58-billion defence
budget
, capping 12 straight years of increasing outlays.
On May 31, at the Shangri-La dialogue in Singapore, Japanese Defence Minister Shinjiro Koizumi rejected China’s accusations of “new militarism” and vowed to keep strengthening the military.
Japan’s steady increase of its defence budget in recent years has sparked anger in China and protests at home. Why is Japan doing this? What do its renewed
defence
interests indicate? We explain.
Before World War II and after
Between 1931 and 1945, the Japanese Empire launched a ruthless expansion campaign across East Asia, including China and Korea, that left millions dead.
Story continues below this ad
Japan’s defeat in World War II, however, led to a fundamental transformation of its military architecture. In 1951, under Prime Shigeru Yoshida, Japan adopted a framework focused on national economic recovery while adopting pacifism as a constitutional building block. Under this strategy, known as the ‘Yoshida Doctrine’, Japan minimised its military armaments and entrusted nati
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
#### **1. Japan’s Shift from Pacifism to Proactive Defense Policy**
- **Event**: **Japan**, under **PM Fumio Kishida** (not Sanae Takaichi—correction in article), is accelerating defense modernization, including **record $58B defense budget (2024)**, **lifting arms export ban**, and **debating **Article 9** revision** to expand **Self-Defense Forces (SDF)** beyond territorial defense.
- **Key Actors**:
  - **Japan**: **Defense Minister Shinjiro Koizumi**, **SDF**, **Yoshida Doctrine** architects.
  - **US**: **Security guarantor** (hosts **7 US military bases** in Japan), **encourages Japan’s militarization** amid **China’s rise**.
  - **China**: Accuses Japan of **"new militarism"**, escalates tensions over **Taiwan**, **South China Sea**, and **Eurasian "fortress"** strategy.
  - **Russia/Iran/North Korea**: Partners in **China’s anti-US coalition**, threatening **Japan’s sea lanes** (e.g., **North Korea’s ICBM tests** near Japan).
- **Why it matters for UPSC/India**:
  - **Quad/Indo-Pacific Security**: Japan’s **$58B budget (2% of GDP)** makes it **3rd-largest spender (post-2027)**, rivaling **China ($292B)** and **US ($900B)**. **India’s defense budget ($81B, 2.8% of GDP)** lags; Japan’s **arms export liberalization** could boost **India’s defense offsets** (e.g., **Mogami-class frigates to Australia**).
  - **Article 9 Debate**: Mirror India’s **Article 355 (duty to protect citizens)** vs. **Article 253 (UN Charter)** tensions. Japan’s **SDF expansion** (now includes **collective defense**) parallels **India’s "whole-of-government" approach** to **China-Pakistan threats**.
  - **Economic Anxiety**: **Weak yen**, **inflation**, and **social welfare costs** (¥100T in 2024) clash with **defense hikes**, akin to **India’s **defense budget vs. welfare trade-offs**.
- **Significance**:
  - **Geopolitical Realignment**: Japan’s **Ryukyu Islands fortification** (e.g., **Yonaguni, 110 km from Taiwan**) signals **forward defense posture**, challenging **US-Japan security treaty** (1960) limits.
  - **China’s Eurasian Strategy**: **Belt and Road Initiative (BRI)** + **Russia-Iran-NK links** aim to **isolate US/Japan**; Japan’s response tests **great-power competition** in **Indo-Pacific vs. Eurasia**.
  - **Domestic Divide**: **51% support Article 9 revision** (Kyodo News, May 2024) vs. **65% oppose rushed constitutional changes**, reflecting **India’s **Article 370 debate** dynamics.

---

#### **2. Historical Context: Yoshida Doctrine to Abe’s Security Blueprint**
- **Event**: **Post-WWII pacifism** (1951 **San Francisco Peace Treaty**) replaced **militarism** with **Yoshida Doctrine**: **economic recovery** + **US security umbrella**.
- **Key Actors**:
  - **PM Shigeru Yoshida**: Architect of **pacifism**, **SDF (1954)**, and **US-Japan Security Treaty (1960)**.
  - **PM Shinzo Abe (2014-15)**: **Expanded SDF mandate** to protect **US forces abroad** (e.g., **Philippines, Taiwan**), via **interpretation of Article 9**.
- **Why it matters for UPSC/India**:
  - **Constitutional Limits**: **Article 9** (renouncing war) vs. **SDF’s "self-defense"** loophole mirrors **India’s **Article 24 (no child labor)** vs. **child rights exceptions**.
  - **Abe’s Legacy**: **2015 Security Laws** (collective defense) prefigure **Kishida’s moves**; India’s **2017 **Balakot airstrikes** also tested **self-defense thresholds**.
- **Significance**:
  - **US-Japan Alliance**: **Abe’s "Proactive Contribution Peace Constitution"** (2015) now guides **Kishida’s Taiwan stance** ("existential crisis" if China attacks).
  - **China’s Counter**: **

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
The Maharashtra Cabinet on Tuesday approved a major restructuring exercise, increasing the number of administrative departments from 33 to 45 to speed up decision-making and reduce the workload on departments handling multiple subjects.
Thirteen government departments will be reorganised, resulting in the creation of 12 new administrative departments. Several sub-divisions currently functioning under larger departments will be carved out into separate departments.
The government said the restructuring would not involve creating new posts. Instead, work currently handled by large departments will be redistributed to enable departments to function more efficiently and process decisions faster.
The General Administration Department had moved a proposal to this effect.
The state government said the restructuring was intended to improve administrative efficiency by allowing departments to focus on a narrower set of subjects rather than handling multiple sectors under a single administrative setup.
Officials said the restructuring was part of Chief Minister
Devendra Fadnavis
’s push to improve administrative efficiency under the state’s ease of doing business and ease of living agenda.
Officials said several departments currently have two or three sub-divisions functioning under a single administrative establishment. While separate secretaries handle many of these subjects, staffing and administrative controls remain concentrated under a single department, creating difficulties in manpower allocation and coordination.
Story continues below this ad
The new departments
According to the Cabinet decision, the Agriculture and Animal Husbandry Department will be split into the Agriculture and Animal Husbandry departments. The Cooperation, Marketing and Textiles Department will be divided into three departments—Cooperation, Marketing, and Textiles. A separate Protocol Department will be carved out of the General Administration Department, while the Home Department will be split
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Maharashtra Cabinet** (June 2024) approved **restructuring 33 departments into 45**, creating **12 new departments** by splitting existing ones (e.g., **Agriculture & Animal Husbandry** into two, **Industries-Energy-Labour-Mining** into three).
  - **No new posts** created; workload redistributed for **faster decision-making** under **CM Devendra Fadnavis**’s **ease of doing business/living agenda**.
  - **Legal amendments** approved for **higher education (Maharashtra Public Universities Act, 2016)**, **GST alignment (Maharashtra GST Act, 2017)**, and **MIDC borrowing powers (Maharashtra Industrial Development Act, 1961)**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Chief Minister Devendra Fadnavis** (BJP, Maharashtra).
  - **General Administration Department** (proposed restructuring).
  - **MIDC (Maharashtra Industrial Development Corporation)**, **HUDCO**, **GST Council**, **Kavikulaguru Kalidas Sanskrit University**.

- **Why It Matters**:
  - **Administrative Efficiency**: Aligns with **NITI Aayog’s recommendations** for **decentralized governance** (e.g., **7th Pay Commission**, **PM’s "Minimum Government, Maximum Governance"**).
  - **Ease of Doing Business**: Critical for **Maharashtra’s rank in World Bank’s Ease of Doing Business (2023: 5th globally, 1st in India)**.
  - **Land Acquisition & Infrastructure**: **Rs 6,000 crore HUDCO loan** for **Purandar Airport** and **SEZs** highlights **industrial growth challenges** (linked to **Article 243ZH of Constitution** on urban planning).

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for State Governments**: Follows **Gujarat’s 2021 departmental restructuring** and **Uttar Pradesh’s 2022 administrative reforms**.
  - **GST Alignment**: Ensures **uniformity with Central GST Act, 2017**, post-**47th GST Council meeting (2023)**.
  - **Higher Education Reforms**: Amendments to **2016 Act** reflect **NEP 2020’s emphasis on autonomy** but retain **June 30, 2026 deadline** for approvals (balancing **autonomy vs. regulation**).

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**

After the system approved the KYC, following the guidelines, they opened the

Trusting the scheme, the complainant invested her money and was allegedly cheated of Rs 68.39 lakh.
‘No connivance’
The counsel on behalf of the bank officials contended that they had no role in the alleged crime, nor were they in contact with any of the fraudsters.
It was submitted that they had only followed th
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Telangana High Court** granted bail to **two bank officials** accused in a **Rs 68.39 lakh investment fraud** against a doctor, involving a **fake scheme promoted via a video featuring Finance Minister **Nirmala Sitharaman***.
  - **Date**: Bail granted on **June 4, 2026**; officials arrested on **April 16, 2026**.
  - **Court**: **Justice Suddala Chalapathi Rao** ruled that prosecution failed to prove **connivance** between bank officials and fraudsters.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Accused 1 & 2**: Masterminds of the fraudulent scheme, misused a bank account opened by officials.
  - **Accused 3 & 4**: **Bank officials** who followed **RBI KYC procedures** but were falsely implicated.
  - **Complainant**: A **doctor** cheated out of **Rs 68.39 lakh** via a **fake investment scheme** advertised with a **deepfake video of **Nirmala Sitharaman***.

- **Why It Matters**:
  - Highlights **vulnerabilities in digital banking fraud**, **misuse of KYC norms**, and **legal challenges in proving connivance** in financial crimes.
  - Reflects **judicial scrutiny of **RBI guidelines** and **bank officials’ liability** in fraud cases.
  - **UPSC Angle**: Relevance to **economic offenses, cybercrime, **RBI’s role in financial regulation**, and **judicial interpretation of **Section 420 (cheating) and cyber laws**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent**: Court emphasized **strict proof of complicity** before implicating bank officials in frauds, reinforcing **due process**.
  - **Context**: Aligns with **recent cyber fraud trends** (e.g., **Delhi HC’s **SBI case** on customer negligence in digital banking).
  - **Broader Issue**: Raises questions about **deepfake misuse in financial scams** and **regulatory gaps** in **investment fraud prevention**.

---

- **The **Telangana High Court** granted bail to two **bank officials** accused in a **Rs 68.39 lakh fraud** case, stating that the **prosecution failed to establish their involvement beyond following **RBI-prescribed KYC procedures***.

- A **doctor** lost **Rs 68.39 lakh** in a **fake investment scheme** promoted via a **deepfake video of **Finance Minister **Nirmala Sitharaman***, exposing risks of **AI-driven financial fraud**.
- The **Delhi High Court** recently held that **customer negligence in digital banking** (e.g., clicking **suspicious links**) can lead to **fraud liability**, relieving **State Bank of India (SBI)** of reimbursement in a **Rs 2.6 lakh cyber theft** case.
- The cases underscore **gaps in **RBI’s KYC-AML framework** and the need for **stricter verification** to prevent **shell account misuse** in financial crimes.
- **Section 420 (cheating) and cyber laws** are critical in such cases, with courts now demanding **concrete evidence** of **active participation** by intermediaries like bank officials.

---

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
3 min read
Sonmarg
Jun 9, 2026 04:01 PM IST
A final 2.5 metres of rock inside the
strategically significant Zojila Tunnel
was blasted away with 300 kg of explosives on Tuesday, marking a major breakthrough in the construction of the 13.153-km tunnel that will provide all-weather road connectivity between Kashmir and Ladakh.
The breakthrough was flagged off by Union Road Transport and Highways Minister
Nitin Gadkari
, alongside Jammu and Kashmir Chief Minister Omar Abdullah and Lieutenant Governor Manoj Sinha.
Once completed, the tunnel connecting Baltal and Meenamarg will provide an alternative to the Zojila Pass, which remains closed for nearly six months every year due to heavy snowfall. The project is expected to transform connectivity for residents, tourists and security forces travelling between Kashmir and Ladakh.
At an altitude of 11,578 feet, the Zojila Tunnel is set to become the world’s longest bi-directional single-tube road tunnel at such a height. The tunnel forms the centrepiece of the 30.894-km Zojila project, which includes roads, bridges and tunnels aimed at ensuring year-round access to Ladakh.
Construction has been fraught with challenges. Engineers had to tunnel through the fragile Himalayan geology while coping with temperatures plunging to minus 30 degrees Celsius and the constant threat of avalanches. In one incident, an avalanche trapped 170 workers, all of whom were later rescued.
Work on the tunnel began in October 2020 after Gadkari launched construction. Excavation progressed simultaneously from the Baltal and Meenamarg ends using the New Austrian Tunnelling Method (NATM), under which small sections of rock are blasted, followed by immediate reinforcement with shotcrete and rock bolts.
By Tuesday, tunnelling teams working from opposite ends had reduced the remaining rock barrier to just 2.5 metres. The final blast connected the two sections deep beneath the snow-covered mountains of the Zojila range.
Story continues below this ad
Despite
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Final breakthrough** in the **Zojila Tunnel** (13.153 km) on **June 9, 2026**, with a **2.5-meter rock barrier blasted** using **300 kg of explosives**, connecting Baltal (J&K) and Meenamarg (Ladakh).
  - **All-weather road connectivity** between **Kashmir and Ladakh** via **Zojila Pass alternative**.

- **Key actors**:
  - **Union Minister Nitin Gadkari** (Road Transport & Highways), **J&K CM Omar Abdullah**, **LG Manoj Sinha**.
  - **Megha Engineering & Infrastructure Limited** (contractor), **New Austrian Tunnelling Method (NATM)** (construction technique).

- **Why it matters**:
  - **Strategic infrastructure** for **national security** (military use by **Dec 2026**), **economic integration** of **J&K-Ladakh**, and **tourism boost**.
  - **World’s longest bi-directional single-tube tunnel at 11,578 ft altitude**, addressing **6-month annual closure** of Zojila Pass due to snow.

- **Significance**:
  - **Part of ₹6,808.69 crore Zojila Project** (30.894 km), including **roads, bridges, and tunnels**.
  - **Local employment**: **80% workforce from J&K-Ladakh**.
  - **Technical challenges**: **Himalayan geology, -30°C temps, avalanches** (e.g., **170 workers trapped in 2024**).
  - **Civilian use target: Feb 2028** (but **Gadkari hints at early completion**).

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Zojila Tunnel** will become the **world’s longest bi-directional single-tube road tunnel at an altitude of 11,578 feet**, surpassing existing records and providing **year-round connectivity** between **Kashmir and Ladakh**, a region currently isolated for **6 months annually** due to **Zojila Pass closures**.
- The **₹6,808.69 crore project**, executed by **Megha Engineering & Infrastructure Limited**, uses the **New Austrian Tunnelling Method (NATM)** to navigate **fragile Himalayan geology**, **sub-zero temperatures (-30°C)**, and **avalanche risks**, with **68.55% physical progress** achieved as of **June 2026**.
- **Nitin Gadkari** launched construction in **October 2020**, and the tunnel is **critical for military logistics**, with **operational readiness for security forces by December 2026**, aligning with **India’s border infrastructure push** post-**Galwan clashes (2020)** and **China’s infrastructure expansion in Tibet**.
- The project will **boost tourism, trade, and economic integration** of **Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh**, regions separated by the **Zanskar Range**, while **reducing travel time from Leh to Srinagar by ~4 hours** (current route: **472 km via Zojila Pass**).
- **Local employment** is a key focus, with **80% of the workforce comprising residents of J&K and Ladakh**, reflecting **PM Modi’s emphasis on "Vocal for Local"** and **infrastructure-led development in aspirational districts**.
- The **Zojila Project** is part of **India’s broader Himalayan tunnel strategy**, including the **Chenani-Nashri Tunnel (11.2 km)**, **Atal Tunnel (9.02 km)**, and **Sela Tunnel (12.8 km)**, all aimed at **strategic connectivity and disaster resilience** in **Himalayan border states**.

---

### **Syllabus Tag**
### **Key Terms**
### Syllabus Tag: GS Paper 3 — Infrastructure & Economic Development: Transport & Connectivity
### Key Terms: Zojila Tunnel, Zojila Pass, New Austrian Tunnelling Method (NATM), Baltal-Meenamarg Road, Zojila Project, Megha Engineering & Infrastructure Limited, Nitin Gadkari, Jammu & Kashmir-Ladakh Connectivity, Himalayan Infrastructure Challenges
### Category: Economy

input:
2 min read
Bengaluru
Updated: Jun 9, 2026 04:09 PM IST
The Karnataka Forest Department on Monday suspended a Range Forest Officer (RFO) for allowing a rowdy sheeter and his associates to hold an alcohol party near an anti-poaching camp in Bandipur Tiger Reserve (BTR).
Action was taken against D Puneeth, RFO of Maddur Wildlife Range, after the video of the party in the prohibited area went viral.
The images showed criminal Mahadev Prasad alias Satish, a resident of Gundlupet, and his aides Gangadhar and Chetan consuming liquor inside the core forest area, using a solar panel at the camp as a makeshift table. Plastic cups, a liquor bottle, and food packets were clearly visible, drawing sharp criticism from environmentalists.
Following widespread outrage, Bandipur Project Tiger Director Nandish ordered an inquiry. Based on the inquiry report, officers booked a case against the trio.
Acting on the inquiry report,
Meenakshi Negi, Principal Chief Conservator of Forests
, issued suspension orders against RFO Puneeth, citing dereliction of duty. The suspension remains in effect pending a formal departmental inquiry into the incident.
The department has also booked Mahadev Prasad and his associates in another case.
Incidents of illegal entry to
Bandipur Tiger Reserve
have been reported in the past. In June 2025, officials posted at Bandipur under the ‘Friends of Bandipur’ initiative were accused of taking tourists into strictly protected anti-poaching camp zones — areas completely off-limits to the public under National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA) regulations. In January 2025, two people were fined Rs 25,000 for illegally entering the national park to hold a photoshoot.
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Range Forest Officer (RFO) D Puneeth** suspended for allowing **Mahadev Prasad (alias Satish)**, a **rowdy-sheeter**, and his associates (**Gangadhar, Chetan**) to host an **illegal liquor party** inside the **core zone of Bandipur Tiger Reserve (BTR)**, Karnataka (**June 2026**).
  - Viral video showed the trio consuming alcohol near an **anti-poaching camp**, violating **National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA)** rules.

- **Key actors**:
  - **D Puneeth** (RFO, Maddur Wildlife Range) – Suspended for **dereliction of duty**.
  - **Mahadev Prasad (Satish)** – Notorious **rowdy-sheeter** from **Gundlupet**, Karnataka.
  - **Nandish** (Director, **Bandipur Project Tiger**) – Ordered inquiry.
  - **Meenakshi Negi** (Principal Chief Conservator of Forests, Karnataka) – Issued suspension.

- **Why it matters**:
  - Highlights **growing encroachment and lawlessness** in **protected tiger reserves**, undermining **wildlife conservation efforts**.
  - Exposes **weak enforcement** of **NTCA regulations** and **collusion risks** between forest officials and anti-social elements.
  - Part of a **pattern of illegal entries** in **BTR** (e.g., **2025 incidents** under **Friends of Bandipur** initiative).

- **Significance**:
  - **Bandipur Tiger Reserve (BTR)** is a **critical tiger habitat** (part of **Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve**) and a **model for tiger conservation** under **Project Tiger**.
  - Incident raises **legal and ethical concerns** over **forest officials’ accountability** and **NTCA’s monitoring mechanisms**.
  - Reflects **broader challenges** in **biodiversity conservation** due to **human-wildlife conflict** and **illegal activities**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Bandipur Tiger Reserve (BTR)**, a **core area of the Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve**, recorded **another illegal entry incident in June 2026**, where a **Range Forest Officer was suspended** for allowing a **rowdy-sheeter (Mahadev Prasad)** to host an **alcohol party inside the core zone**, violating **NTCA’s strict access restrictions**.
- The **National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA)**, under **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)**, mandates **zero public access** in **anti-poaching camp zones** of tiger reserves, yet **BTR has seen repeated violations**, including a **2025 case** where **tourists were taken into restricted zones** by **Friends of Bandipur** officials.
- **Karnataka’s forest department** has **booked criminal cases** against **Mahadev Prasad and his associates** for **illegal entry, consumption of alcohol, and disturbance in a protected area**, while **RFO D Puneeth faces suspension** for **negligence in duty**.
- **Project Tiger**, launched in **1973**, aims to **protect tiger habitats** across **53 tiger reserves** in India, with **BTR being one of the most successful** in **tiger population recovery** (currently hosting **~100 tigers**).
- The **Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve**, a **UNESCO-designated site**, spans **Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, and Kerala**, and **BTR’s core zone** is part of the **Western Ghats**, a **biodiversity hotspot** under **global conservation priority**.
- **Human-wildlife conflict** and **illegal activities** in **protected areas** pose a **major threat to India’s wildlife conservation goals**, with **tiger numbers rising to 3,682 in 2022** (as per **All India Tiger Estimation**), but **encroachment and poaching remain persistent challenges**.

---

### **Key Terms**

Jun 9, 2026 04:07 PM IST
Officials from the Union Health and Education ministries, the
National Testing Agency (NTA)
, and the
National Medical Commission (NMC)
will appear before the Parliamentary Standing Committee on Health and Family Welfare on Wednesday to discuss the conduct of the
NEET-UG re-examination
scheduled to be held on June 21. The Agency has already released the
city slip for the exam
.
Read
| NEET UG 2026 Re-Exam FAQs
The committee has summoned the secretaries of Higher Education and Health, along with top representatives of the NTA and NMC, to provide a detailed briefing on the re-exam to be held on June 21. This is the third parliamentary panel appearance for these officials, who have already faced questions from the Parliamentary Committees on Education and on Government Assurances. In earlier meetings, they emphasised that their immediate priority is to ensure the smooth conduct of the re-exam in pen-and-paper format.
Also Read
| No physical papers, multi-set questions, AI checks: The proposed blueprint to fix NEET UG
The
re-test was announced after the cancellation of the May 3 NEET-UG exam
following reports of a paper leak. The paper leak has sparked widespread concern, with some students tragically taking their own lives amid the uncertainty. The upcoming panel meeting will focus on the examination process and its regulation under the NMC Act, 2019, as part of a broader review of the organisational structure and functional proficiency of regulatory institutions in the health sector.
The committee will hear the views of the Health and Higher Education secretaries, alongside senior officers from the NMC and NTA.
Meanwhile, CBI Director Praveen Sood has also briefed a parliamentary panel on the progress of the investigation into the NEET paper leak case, outlining steps being taken to identify and arrest those responsible.

NEET was supposed to be leak-proof in 2026. Here’s how the system built to protect the exam

During a press briefing Tuesday, MEA Spokesperson Randhir Jaiswal said: “We continue to see in this context, a pattern of fake news and videos emanating from Pakistan. It is a desperate attempt by Pakistan to cover up its own failings and deflect attention away from its human rights abuses”.
Jaiswal also spoke about the cases of alleged “police brutality”.
“There are reports of severe police brutality in Pakistan-occupied Kashmir, in which several people have been killed and many injured. We hope the international community will hold Pakistan accountable for its misdeeds and abuses,” he said.
The clashes in PoK ahead of a protest called for Tuesday left 11 people dead and nearly 70 others injured as paramilitary forces tried ​to scatter a group of protesters from a banned alliance of civil society ‌groups.
What happened in PoK?
Police said the protesters of the Joint Awami Action Committee (JAAC) had gathered outside a hospital morgue where the body of another group member was taken after his ​death in firing by police.
“Four police officers and a passerby died after miscreants shot ​at them,” Sardar Waheed Khan, commissioner of the Poonch sector in the ⁠region, told
Reuters
, adding, “As the result of the law enforcers’ response, six protesters were killed.”
Police officials patrol an area amid the ongoing violence in PoK, where 11 people, including four police officers, were killed and hundreds injured during violent clashes, in Muzaffarabad, Pakistan-occupied Kashmir, Tuesday, June 9, 2026. (PTI Photo)
Police ​Chief Liaqat Malik said 23 security officials and 50 protesters were among the injured in ​Sunday’s incident, with 30 offenders arrested in the Himalayan region that is a flashpoint with neighbouring India.
Story continues below this ad
“The state has begun a massacre of our people in Rawalakot,” Shaukat Nawaz Mir, a JAAC leader, said in ​a video message on X, referring to the district where the incident happened. He vowed ​that the group would stay united
