instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
3 min read
Guwahati
Jun 9, 2026 10:42 PM IST
Prohibitory orders have been issued in Arunachal Pradesh’s Upper Siang district after groups
opposing the Upper Siang dam project
allegedly vandalised the homes of several villagers who had recently signed an MoU backing the project.
Local reports claimed that among the houses allegedly vandalised on Monday was that of local MLA Oni Panyang.
The incident took place two days after
Arunachal Pradesh Chief Minister Pema Khandu
and MLA Panyang announced that 110 households from Geku, one of the villages along the Siang river that are expected to be affected by the hydropower project, signed an MoU “in support of the Pre-Feasibility Report (PFR) for the Siang Upper Multipurpose Project”.
Ever since the 11,000
MW Siang Upper Multipurpose Project was proposed in 2017
, it has been opposed by many families whose villages and farmlands would be affected by such a dam. Over the last couple of years, the state authorities and the National Hydroelectric Power Corporation (NHPC) have been trying to begin work on a Pre-Feasibility Report (PFR), which has been stalled by local opposition.
As news of the MoU signing made the rounds, unrest spread to Geku on Monday.
In an executive order imposing restrictions on the assembly of people and “incitement on social media” in Geku on Tuesday, the District Magistrate of Upper Siang alleged that “anti-dam groups vandalised and caused damage to some dwelling houses and properties of pro-PFR residents within Geku township and nearby villages”.
“It is currently apprehended that there is every likelihood of the situation being further escalated by the anti-dam groups by resorting to violence, intimidation and vandalism of public properties or the properties and assets of other pro-PFR residents…,” the order said.
Story continues below this ad
Both central and state governments are pushing for the Siang dam as a means to counter the possible effects of the 60,000 MW dam – the world’s largest hydro-ele
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Prohibitory orders** issued in **Upper Siang district, Arunachal Pradesh** (June 2026) after **anti-dam groups vandalized homes** of villagers who signed an **MoU supporting the 11,000 MW Upper Siang Dam Project**.
  - **110 households** from **Geku village** signed the MoU (June 7, 2026), backing the **Pre-Feasibility Report (PFR)** for the project, sparking unrest.

- **Key actors**:
  - **Arunachal Pradesh CM Pema Khandu** and **MLA Oni Panyang** (pro-dam).
  - **National Hydroelectric Power Corporation (NHPC)** (implementing agency).
  - **Anti-dam groups** (local opposition, including affected villagers).
  - **District Magistrate of Upper Siang** (imposed restrictions on assembly/social media incitement).

- **Why it matters**:
  - **Strategic hydropower push** to counter **China’s 60,000 MW Yarlung Tsangpo Dam** (Tibet), which flows into **Arunachal Pradesh as the Siang River** (later merging into **Brahmaputra**).
  - **Land acquisition disputes** highlight **tribal rights vs. developmental projects** under **Schedule VI of the Constitution** (autonomous districts).
  - **NHPC’s role** in **hydroelectric projects** under **Ministry of Power**, linking to **India’s renewable energy targets** (40% non-fossil capacity by 2030).

- **Significance**:
  - **Geopolitical angle**: Brahmaputra’s transboundary flow makes **water security** a **national security issue** (China’s dam could disrupt downstream water flow).
  - **Legal battleground**: **PFR approval process** vs. **local consent** under **Forest Rights Act (FRA) and PESA (Panchayats Extension to Scheduled Areas Act)**.
  - **Precedent for future projects**: Similar opposition seen in **Teesta, Subansiri, and Dibang dams**, testing **government’s ability to balance development and tribal rights**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- The **11,000 MW Upper Siang Dam Project**, proposed in **2017** by **NHPC**, aims to harness the **Siang River** (a tributary of the **Brahmaputra**), but faces **local opposition** from **110 affected households** in **Geku village, Arunachal Pradesh**, who were recently **intimidated after signing an MoU** supporting its **Pre-Feasibility Report (PFR)**.
- **China’s planned 60,000 MW Yarlung Tsangpo Dam** (Tibet) poses a **strategic threat** to India, as the river becomes the **Siang in Arunachal Pradesh**, potentially disrupting **water flow and hydropower generation** in the **Brahmaputra basin**, which supplies **~40% of Assam’s irrigation and drinking water**.
- **Arunachal Pradesh**, governed under **Schedule VI of the Constitution**, has **autonomous district councils**, requiring **tribal consent** for land acquisition, complicating projects like the **Siang Dam** where **~80% of the population belongs to indigenous tribes** (e.g., **Adi, Galo, Singpho**).
- The **National Hydroelectric Power Corporation (NHPC)**, a **Ministry of Power PSU**, is implementing the project, aligning with **India’s target of 500 GW non-fossil energy capacity by 2030**, but faces **legal hurdles** under the **Forest Rights Act (FRA) and PESA**, which mandate **free, prior, and informed consent (FPIC)** for tribal lands.
- **Prohibitory orders** in **Upper Siang district** (June 2026) restrict **public assembly and social media incitement**, reflecting **government’s use of administrative measures** to curb **violence in land acquisition disputes**, similar to past cases like the **Narmada Bachao Andolan** and **Subansiri Dam protests**.
- The **Brahmaputra River**, originating in **Tibet as the Yarlung Tsangpo**, flows through **Arunachal Pradesh, Assam, and Bangladesh**, supporting **~60 million people’s livelihoods** and **~20% of India’s rice production**, making its **hydropower and water security** a **critical GS3 (Environment) and GS2 (International Relations) issue**.

---

New Delhi
Updated: Jun 9, 2026 11:07 PM IST
US President Donald Trump on Tuesday threatened Iran with retaliation after informing that an American military’s Apache helicopter was shot down by Tehran while it was patrolling the Strait of Hormuz.
Trump, in a post on Truth Social, wrote “I have just been informed by our Great Military that last night the Iranians shot down one of our highly sophisticated Apache Helicopters while patrolling over the Strait of Hormuz.”
Issuing threat of retaliation to Iran, the US President wrote, “There were two pilots involved, both are safe and uninjured. Nevertheless, the United States must, of necessity, respond to this attack.”
The US Central Command earlier said two crew members from a US Army AH-64 Apache were rescued by American forces after their helicopter went down while patrolling regional waters near the coast of Oman.
The Central Command, in a post on X wrote, “The Soldiers were safely rescued within approximately two hours and are in stable condition. The cause of the incident is under investigation,” adding “Rescue efforts were led by US Naval Forces Central Command and the 82nd Airborne Division, with support from US Air Force and Navy units.”

A major focus area of the desk is US immigration and visa policy, including developments related to student visas, work permits, permanent residency pathways, executive actions, and court rulings. ### GK Gist:

- **Event**:

  - **Cause under investigation**; **US President Donald Trump** threatened retaliation on **Truth Social**.

- **Key Actors**:

  - **US-Iran relations**: Escalation risks **regional instability** in **West Asia**, impacting **India’s strategic interests** (e.g., **Chabahar Port**, **INS Khukri incident**, **2019 tanker seizures**).
  - **Military escalation**: Potential for **sanctions**, **economic warfare**, or **proxy conflicts** (e.g., **Yemen’s Houthi attacks**, **Israel-Iran tensions**).

  - **Strait of Hormuz as flashpoint**: Historically a **geopolitical hotspot** (e.g., **1980s Iran-Iraq War**, **2019 US-Iran tensions**).
  - **Impact on global oil markets**: **OPEC+ dynamics**, **US shale dependence**, and **India’s fuel import costs** could be affected.
  - **India’s energy diplomacy**: May push for **alternative routes** (e.g., **Arctic shipping**, **Afghanistan-Central Asia corridor**) or **strategic petroleum reserves (SPR)** utilization.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The Strait of Hormuz**, a **51-km-wide waterway** between **Iran and Oman**, is the **world’s most strategically vital chokepoint**, through which **~21 million barrels per day (mbpd) of oil** (or **1/3 of global oil trade**) passes, including **60% of India’s crude oil imports** worth **$120 billion annually**.
- **US President Donald Trump** threatened **retaliation against Iran** after the **downing of a US Apache helicopter** in the Strait of Hormuz, escalating tensions amid **ongoing US-Iran proxy conflicts** in **Yemen, Syria, and Iraq**, where **Iran-backed militias** have targeted **US forces and interests** since **2019**.
- The **US Central Command** confirmed the **rescue of two unharmed pilots** within **2 hours**, highlighting **rapid US military response capabilities** in the **Middle East**, where the **US maintains **~35,000 troops** across **Iraq, Syria, and the Gulf region** under **Operation Inherent Resolve**.
- **Iran’s repeated violations** of **international airspace and maritime rules** in the Strait of Hormuz—including **2019 seizures of British-flagged tankers (Stena Impero, Advance Fortune)** and **2021 attacks on Israeli-linked vessels**—have drawn **UN Security Council condemnations** but **no binding action** due to **China and Russia’s veto power**.
- **India’s energy security** is directly tied to the Strait of Hormuz, as **disruptions could force reliance on costlier routes** (e.g., **Suez Canal, Cape of Good Hope**) or **alternative suppliers** like **US shale oil**, which **India imports at a premium** due

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
4 min read
Bengaluru
Jun 9, 2026 10:52 PM IST
Data collected by
India’s moon mission Chandrayaan-3
has established a close chemical relationship between the soil found at Shiv Shakti Point, the landing site near the Moon’s South Pole, and a lunar meteorite that had hit Antarctica’s Allan Hills region of Victoria land about a million years ago.
A new study led by a team of researchers from the Physical Research Laboratory (PRL) noted that the iron and magnesium-rich and aluminum-deficient soil found at the
Shiv Shakti point
came closest chemically to the lunar meteorite ALHA 81005 discovered in Antarctica in 1982.
Dwijesh Ray, lead author and PRL scientist, told The Indian Express: “The
Chandrayaan
-3 landing site occupies a compositional space between traditional ferroan anorthosite and Mg-suite lithologies (names of different types of rocks), and closely resembles the lunar meteorite ALHA 81005.”
Both ferroan anorthosite and Mg-suite lithologies are typical lunar rock types composed of specific elements, like calcium and magnesium.
The lunar surface has suffered numerous micro-meteorite and meteorite hits in the past, as a result of which the regolith layers (the top layers) on its surface have a certain chemical composition. But the possibility of deeper lava and mineral layers mixing with the surface soil as a fallout of these events were not scientifically established yet.
The latest results have emerged from the data gathered by the Alpha Particle X-ray Spectrometer (APXS), a scientific probe onboard the Pragyaan rover on Chandrayaan-3, had landed on the Moon’s South Pole in August 2023. The APXS is designed to unravel the chemical composition of the soil at the Chandrayaan-3 landing site.
The PRL team, in a new publication in the journal Nature, described the soil at the landing site to be a mix composition of a number of chemicals.
Story continues below this ad
The meteorite found on Antarctica is composed 25.8% of aluminum oxide and 13.7% iron and magnesium
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Chandrayaan-3**’s **Alpha Particle X-ray Spectrometer (APXS)** on **Pragyaan rover** (landed at **Shiv Shakti Point**, **Moon’s South Pole**, **August 2023**) detected **iron-magnesium-rich, aluminum-deficient lunar soil**, matching **Antarctic lunar meteorite ALHA 81005** (discovered **1982**).
  - Study published in **Nature** by **Physical Research Laboratory (PRL)** confirms **chemical linkage** between **Shiv Shakti Point soil** and **ALHA 81005**, proving **deep crustal material mixing** via **South Pole-Aitken (SPA) basin impact**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **ISRO** (Chandrayaan-3 mission), **PRL** (Ahmedabad), **Pragyaan rover**, **APXS instrument**, **Lead Author: Dwijesh Ray (PRL scientist)**.
  - **Comparative analysis** with **lunar meteorites from Antarctica (ALHA 81005), Northwest Africa, Libya, and Oman**.

- **Why It Matters (UPSC/India Angle)**:
  - **First direct evidence** of **deep lunar crustal material** (iron-magnesium-rich) being exposed at the surface via **SPA basin impact**, validating **lunar magma ocean theory**.
  - **India’s scientific leadership**: **Chandrayaan-3**’s **in-situ data** (APXS) provides **global-first insights** into **Moon’s South Pole geochemistry**, enhancing **India’s space credentials**.
  - **Strategic relevance**: **Lunar South Pole** (water ice, rare minerals) is a **future exploration hotspot** for **ISRO’s Artemis Accords participation** and **international collaborations**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Geological breakthrough**: Confirms **impact excavation** redistributes **deep crustal layers**, offering clues to **Moon’s early evolution** (magma ocean crystallization).
  - **Mission planning**: Future rovers (e.g., **Chandrayaan-4**) can target **SPA basin regions** for **deep crustal samples**.
  - **Theoretical validation**: Supports **ferroan anorthosite + Mg-suite lithologies** as dominant **lunar highland compositions**, refining **planetary formation models**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Chandrayaan-3’s Alpha Particle X-ray Spectrometer (APXS)** detected **lunar soil at Shiv Shakti Point** with **25.8% aluminum oxide and 13.7% iron-magnesium oxides**, an **exact match** to **Antarctic meteorite ALHA 81005**, discovered in **1982** by **US Antarctic Search for Meteorites (ANSMET)** program.
- The **South Pole-Aitken (SPA) basin**, the **largest and deepest impact crater in the solar system** (2,500 km diameter, **4–5 billion years old**), likely **excavated deep crustal material**, enriching the **Shiv Shakti Point regolith** with **iron and magnesium**, as per **PRL’s study in *Nature***.
- **Lunar magma ocean theory** receives **direct empirical support** from **Chandrayaan-3 data**, confirming **crystallization of ferroan anorthosite (calcium-rich) and Mg-suite rocks** during the **Moon’s early differentiation (~4.5 billion years ago)**.
- **India’s Artemis Accords participation** gains scientific backing, as **Moon’s South Pole**—targeted by **Chandrayaan-3**—hosts **water ice deposits** and **rare minerals (e.g., helium-3)**, critical for **future lunar bases** and **ISRO’s space economy goals**.
- **Comparative meteorite analysis** revealed that **lunar highlands** exhibit **compositional diversity**, with **Shiv Shakti Point soil** distinct from **near-side regions** but chemically linked to **farside crust analogs** (e.g., **Northwest Africa meteorites**), hinting at **asymmetrical lunar crustal evolution**.
- **Pragyaan rover’s APXS instrument** (developed by **ISRO and NASA-JPL**) demonstrated **in-situ elemental analysis**, a **technology leap** for **future planetary missions**, including **Venus (Shukrayaan-1)** and **Mars (Mangalyaan-2)**.

---

### Syllabus Tag: GS Paper 1 — Geophysics:

input:
Following a recent enforcement action against a popular vada pav vendor in Mumbai, the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI), West Region, has reiterated its ban on using newspapers for packing, serving, or storing food. The regulator warned that newspaper ink contains harmful chemicals, including heavy metals, that can leach into food, particularly when it is hot, oily, or moist. Newspapers may also carry disease-causing microbes due to repeated handling and exposure to unhygienic conditions.
The food body has directed all food businesses, from street vendors and hawkers to restaurants and cloud kitchens, to switch to approved food-grade packaging materials in the interest of consumer safety.
DISCLAIMER:
This article is based on information from the public domain and/or the experts we spoke to. Always consult your health practitioner before starting any routine.
Why newspapers and food are a risky combination
According to Dr Disha Bhatia, Consultant and Hospital Infection Control Officer, Microbiology, Aakash Healthcare, the biggest concern begins with the ink used in newspaper printing.
“Newspaper ink contains a complex mixture of solvents, pigments, binders, and additives. Printing inks often contain heavy metals such as lead, chromium, and cadmium. Additionally, they may contain phthalates,
mineral oils, and various synthetic dyes,” says Dr Bhatia.
FSSAI bans newspaper packaging for food (Photo: AI)
How harmful chemicals enter your food
The danger increases when newspapers come into contact with hot and oily foods such as vada pav, samosas, pakoras, or fried snacks.
“When hot, oily, or moist foods contact newsprint, the heat and lipids act as solvents, facilitating the migration of chemicals from the ink into the food,” explains Dr Bhatia. This process, known as chemical migration, allows potentially harmful substances to move from the newspaper surface directly into the food being consumed.
The long-term health risks
Dr Bhatia notes that chronic
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)**, **West Region**, issued a **ban on using newspapers for packing, storing, or serving food** in **Mumbai**, following enforcement action against a **vada pav vendor**.
  - The ban applies to **all food businesses**, including **street vendors, hawkers, restaurants, and cloud kitchens**, nationwide.
  - **Date**: Recent (exact date not specified, but part of ongoing FSSAI enforcement).

- **Key Actors**:
  - **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** – Regulatory body under **Ministry of Health & Family Welfare**, responsible for food safety standards.
  - **Dr. Disha Bhatia** – **Consultant & Hospital Infection Control Officer (Microbiology), Aakash Healthcare**, cited for expert opinion on **chemical and microbial risks**.
  - **Street vendors, hawkers, and food businesses** – Directly impacted by the ban.

- **Why it Matters (UPSC/India Angle)**:
  - **Public Health Crisis**: Highlights **food safety challenges in street food**, a **$20+ billion industry** in India, contributing **~10% of total food consumption**.
  - **Regulatory Enforcement**: Demonstrates **FSSAI’s proactive role** in implementing **Food Safety and Standards (Packaging) Regulations, 2021**.
  - **Urban Informal Sector**: Impacts **~50 million street food vendors** (per **FICCI-EY 2023**), many in **unorganized sectors** lacking formal training.
  - **Chemical & Microbial Hazards**: Links to **long-term health risks** (e.g., **heavy metal toxicity, carcinogens, foodborne diseases**), aligning with **SDG 3 (Good Health and Well-being)**.
  - **Circular Economy & Waste**: Raises questions on **single-use plastic alternatives** and **sustainable packaging** in India’s **plastic ban regime (2022)**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for Food Safety**: Strengthens **FSSAI’s stance** against **non-compliant packaging**, similar to earlier bans on **Styrofoam and plastic below 50 microns**.
  - **Consumer Awareness**: Highlights **migration of harmful chemicals** (e.g., **lead, cadmium, phthalates**) from **newsprint ink** into food, especially **hot/oily items** (e.g., **vada pav, samosas**).
  - **Legal & Economic Impact**: Vendors may face **fines up to ₹1 lakh** (under **FSS Act, 2006**) and **license suspension** for non-compliance.
  - **Traditional vs. Modern Solutions**: Promotes **alternatives like banana leaves, bagasse, and food-grade paper**, aligning with **Atmanirbhar Bharat’s focus on indigenous materials**.
  - **Global Context**: India’s **street food culture** is unique; this ban reflects **WHO’s guidelines on food safety** and **EU’s REACH regulations** on chemical migration.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The **Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)** has **permanently banned the use of newspapers for food packaging** under the **Food Safety and Standards (Packaging) Regulations, 2021**, citing **chemical leaching (e.g., lead, cadmium, phthalates) and microbial contamination** from ink and handling.**
- **Newspaper ink contains **toxic heavy metals like lead (up to 500 ppm in some cases)** and **endocrine disruptors**, which **migrate into hot/oily foods** (e.g., **vada pav, pakoras**) through a process called **chemical migration**, posing **neurotoxic and carcinogenic risks** as per **Dr. Disha Bhatia, Aakash Healthcare**.**
- **India’s **street food sector**, valued at **$20+ billion (2023)**, employs **~50 million vendors**, many of whom rely on **newspapers for cost-effective packaging**, making **FSSAI’s enforcement a challenge** in informal economies.**
- **The **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** allows **FSSAI to impose fines up to ₹1 lakh** and **license cancellation** for violations, with **Mumbai’s vada pav vendor** becoming the **first high-profile case** under this ban.**

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Minister of BC Welfare S. Savitha holding a review meeting with the department officials in Tirupati on Tuesday.
                                          | Photo Credit: K.V. POORNACHANDRA KUMAR
Minister of BC and EWS Welfare, Handloom & Textiles S. Savitha directed the officials on Tuesday (June 9, 2026) to ensure proper maintenance of the welfare hostels and to avoid any criticism from any quarters.
Addressing a review meeting here on Tuesday, the Minister instructed the officials to complete the repair work and ensure proper upkeep by the time the boarders arrive after the admission process for the new academic year.
She recalled the numerous complaints received from the inmates regarding the wardens and principals last year and minced no words in stating that a repeat of such incidents would be dealt with seriously. She also cautioned them about the opposition parties on the prowl, ready to give ‘selective leaks’ to the media to blow things out of proportion.
In tune with the government’s focus on providing education to the Backward Classes, she emphasized the importance of improving the intake in BC welfare hostels and directed the authorities to conduct medical tests upon their arrival. She also announced the implementation of special coaching for the inmates to prepare them for entrance examinations into professional courses like IIT and NEET.
Turning her attention to the Handloom Department, she suggested improving the over-the-counter sales of handloom products in APCO showrooms across the State.
Published
- June 09, 2026 09:01 pm IST
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Review meeting** held by **Minister of BC & EWS Welfare, Handloom & Textiles (S. Savitha)** in **Tirupati (Andhra Pradesh)** on **June 9, 2026**.
  - Focused on **BC welfare hostels’ maintenance**, **handloom sector reforms**, and **educational support for backward classes**.

- **Key actors**:
  - **S. Savitha** (Minister for **BC & EWS Welfare, Handloom & Textiles**).
  - **Department officials** (BC Welfare, Handloom).
  - **BC welfare hostel inmates** (target beneficiaries).
  - **APCO showrooms** (state-run handloom retail outlets).

- **Why it matters**:
  - Reflects **government’s commitment to social justice** under **Article 15(4) & 16(4)** (reservation for backward classes).
  - Aligns with **NEP 2020** (inclusive education) and **Make in India** (handloom sector revival).
  - Highlights **political accountability** in welfare schemes amid **opposition scrutiny**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Preventive governance**: Crackdown on **warden misconduct** (recall of **2025 complaints**).
  - **Proactive measures**: **Medical tests**, **special coaching (IIT/NEET prep)**, and **OTC handloom sales boost**.
  - **Political strategy**: Warning against **"selective leaks"** by opposition to avoid **media-driven controversies**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Andhra Pradesh’s BC welfare hostels** will now mandate **mandatory medical tests on admission** to ensure **health standards**, aligning with **Ayushman Bharat’s preventive healthcare model**.
- **Minister S. Savitha** directed **100% completion of repair work** in **BC hostels by July 2026** to avoid **public criticism**, reflecting **performance-based governance** under **Article 14 (equality before law)**.
- **Handloom sector revival** in **APCO showrooms** aims to **double OTC sales** by **2027**, leveraging **PM Vishwakarma Yojana’s skill integration** for artisans.
- **Special coaching for IIT/NEET** in BC hostels was announced to **bridge the urban-rural education gap**, mirroring **PM’s emphasis on "Viksit Bharat @2047"** with **inclusive growth**.
- **Opposition parties’ "selective leaks"** were flagged as a **tactical threat** to **welfare schemes**, underscoring the **political economy of social welfare** in **federal governance**.
- **Backward Classes (BC) reservation** in **hostels/education** is constitutionally backed by **Article 15(4) & 46 (DPSP)**, with **Savitha’s directives** ensuring **implementation rigor**.

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Ahead of the Budget for the FY27, calls for increased allocations to the tourism sector, particularly for marketing, have grown louder. Despite contributing around a good amount of money annually to the State’s economy, Kerala has been neglecting the sector for over a decade by earmarking a modest marketing budget, treating tourism as a peripheral activity rather than a strategic economic driver.
A delegation led by the Confederation of Kerala Tourism Industry (CKTI), the apex body representing the State’s organised tourism and hospitality sector, recently met Chief Minister V.D. Satheesan and demanded that at least 1% of the total revenue generated by tourism be reinvested in the sector for development, marketing, infrastructure, etc, in line with globally accepted practices.
According to the 2024-25 financial figures, the sector contributed around ₹45,053 crore to the State economy. If 1% of this amount is earmarked for tourism development, at least ₹450 crore could be allocated for tourism promotion activities. At present, the State’s tourism marketing budget stands at around ₹80 crore, a figure that was last revised during the tenure of the Oommen Chandy government.
Industry status
Meanwhile, States competing directly with Kerala in the tourism sector have significantly scaled up their promotional spending. For instance, Rajasthan, Goa, Tamil Nadu and several other States have been allotting more than Rs 300 crore annually for tourism marketing. Another key demand raised by the industry was the granting of industry status to the tourism sector. According to E.M. Najeeb, President of CKTI, every major tourism destination across the world and most Indian States that outperform Kerala have accorded industry status to tourism.
Additionally, no other destination combines Kerala’s unique climate, rich wellness heritage, and Ayurveda traditions, including Karkidaka Panchakarma, Ayurvedic rejuvenation therapies, authentic treatment protocols, and a deep pool of practiti
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Kerala’s tourism sector** demanded **₹450 crore budget allocation** (1% of **₹45,053 crore** revenue in FY24-25) for marketing, infrastructure, and development ahead of **FY27 Budget**, citing neglect over a decade.
  - **Confederation of Kerala Tourism Industry (CKTI)** led a delegation to **CM V.D. Satheesan** to push for **industry status** for tourism and a **dedicated international campaign**—**"Kerala Monsoon – World’s Ayurveda Season"**—targeting **GCC, CIS, Europe, and Southeast Asia**.
  - **Current marketing budget (₹80 crore)** is outdated (last revised under **Oommen Chandy’s tenure**), while competitors like **Rajasthan, Goa, and Tamil Nadu** spend **₹300+ crore annually**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **CKTI** (apex body for Kerala’s tourism/hospitality sector), led by **President E.M. Najeeb** and **General Secretary Sajeev Kurup**.
  - **Kerala Government** (CM **V.D. Satheesan**), **Ayurveda Promotion Society (APS)**, and **private sector stakeholders**.
  - **Competing states**: **Rajasthan, Goa, Tamil Nadu** (higher tourism budgets).

- **Why It Matters**:
  - **Tourism contributes ₹45,053 crore (~10% of Kerala’s economy)** but lacks **strategic investment**, unlike **Goa (₹1,500+ crore budget)** or **Uttar Pradesh (₹500 crore)**.
  - **Ayurveda and wellness tourism** (Kerala’s unique selling point) is under-marketed despite global demand, especially from **GCC (UAE, Saudi Arabia)** and **CIS (Russia, Kazakhstan)**.
  - **Post-pandemic recovery** stalled—foreign arrivals (**11.89 lakh in 2019**) remain unmatched, while **domestic tourism booms** (no data provided but implied growth).

- **Significance**:
  - **Policy gap**: Kerala treats tourism as **peripheral** (₹80 crore budget) vs. **strategic sector** in other states (e.g., **Andhra Pradesh’s ₹400 crore**).
  - **Global benchmarking**: **Industry status** (granted to tourism in **Maharashtra, Karnataka**) could unlock **PLI-like incentives**, **tax holidays**, and **infrastructure funding**.
  - **Ayurveda diplomacy**: Kerala’s **Karkidaka Panchakarma** and **monsoon wellness tourism** align with **India’s wellness tourism push (₹1,500 crore budget in 2023)** but lack targeted campaigns.
  - **Infrastructure lag**: Poor **road connectivity, sanitation, and waste management** in tourist hubs (e.g., **Munnar, Alleppey, Wayanad**) deter high-spend tourists.
  - **Proposed solutions**:
    - **Kerala Tourism Promotion Board** (private sector participation).
    - **B2B/B2C campaigns** in **Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai** to boost domestic Ayurveda tourism.
    - **Restoring European confidence** (post-pandemic decline) while tapping **GCC/CIS markets** (high-spend medical tourists).

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Kerala’s tourism revenue surged to ₹45,053 crore in FY24-25**, yet its **marketing budget (₹80 crore)** is **5.6x lower** than **Goa’s ₹450 crore**, despite **Ayurveda and backwaters** being its **top global draw**.
- The **Confederation of Kerala Tourism Industry (CKTI)** demanded **1% of tourism revenue (₹450 crore)** for marketing, mirroring **global best practices** (e.g., **Thailand allocates 2-3% of tourism revenue** to promotion).
- **Only 12 Indian states** (including **Maharashtra, Karnataka**) have granted **industry status to tourism**, enabling **tax exemptions and infrastructure subsidies**—Kerala’s refusal costs it **₹1,000+ crore in potential PLI-like benefits**.
- **Kerala’s foreign tourist arrivals (10.5 lakh in 2025)** remain **12% below 2019 levels (11.89 lakh)**, while **domestic tourism grew 25%

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
“Without causing any inconvenience to workers, we are moving from MGNREGA to VB-GRAMG. There will not be a gap of even a single day in the availability of work,” says Shivraj Singh Chouhan. File
                                          | Photo Credit: G.N. Rao
Union Rural Development Minister
Shivraj Singh Chouhan
on Tuesday (June 9, 2026) announced an interim allocation of ₹95,962 crore for the new rural employment scheme, the
Vikshit Bharat Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission Gramin
(VB-GRAMG). This sum does not include the State governments’ share. Most States will have to contribute an additional 40% of the sum allocated to them. The combined outlay for the scheme will be ₹1.25 lakh crore.
Mr. Chouhan said that the allocation had been made even before notifying the scheme’s rules and framing the formula dictating distribution to different States, to ensure “a seamless transition” from the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme (MGNREGS). No State, he said, would face any reduction in funds.
“Without causing any inconvenience to workers, we are moving from
MGNREGA
to VB-GRAMG. There will not be a gap of even a single day in the availability of work,” he said.
Funding formula
According to the Rural Development Ministry, Uttar Pradesh has been allotted the highest interim allocation of ₹9,721.48 crore, followed by West Bengal (₹8,508 crore), Tamil Nadu (₹7,585.49 crore), Rajasthan (₹7,581.87 crore), Andhra Pradesh (₹7,707.21 crore) and Bihar (₹6,715.83 crore). The total allocation for States stands at ₹92,550.17 crore, while Union Territories have been allocated ₹1,291.52 crore. An additional ₹1,850.62 crore has been earmarked for central administration and social audits, taking the total to ₹95,692.31 crore.
The allocation is largely in line with funds given to
States under MGNREGA
in previous financial years. The thrust of the new programme, however, is to ensure that the employment scheme has a wider presence in economically weaker Stat
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Union Rural Development Minister ****Shivraj Singh Chouhan** announced the launch of **Vikshit Bharat Guarantee for Rozgar and Ajeevika Mission Gramin (VB-GRAMG)** on **June 9, 2026**, replacing **MGNREGA** with an interim allocation of **₹95,962 crore** (excluding state share).
  - The scheme aims for a **seamless transition** with **no work gap** for beneficiaries.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Shivraj Singh Chouhan** (Union Rural Development Minister).
  - **26 States** (compliant with procedural requirements).
  - **4 States** (Jharkhand, Karnataka, Telangana, Mizoram) pending formalities.
  - **West Bengal** (ongoing dispute over **₹8,508 crore** pending MGNREGA dues).

- **Why It Matters**:
  - **Economic Inclusion**: VB-GRAMG aligns with **Viksit Bharat @2047** vision, focusing on **rural employment, skilling, and livelihood diversification**.
  - **Policy Continuity**: Ensures **no disruption** in rural wage employment amid **scheme restructuring**.
  - **Federal Dynamics**: Highlights **Centre-State fiscal coordination** (40% state contribution) and **16th Finance Commission’s devolution formula** for fund distribution.

- **Significance**:
  - **Funding Shift**: **₹1.25 lakh crore** total outlay (₹95,962 crore Centre + 40% state share) reflects **prioritization of rural employment** in **Union Budget 2026-27**.
  - **Inclusion Focus**: Draft rules propose **horizontal equity** via **16th Finance Commission’s formula**, benefiting **economically weaker states**.
  - **Administrative Readiness**: States must complete **e-KYC, blackout period fixation, and capacity-building** before full rollout (expected **July 1, 2026**).

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **VB-GRAMG**, the **new rural employment scheme**, received an **interim allocation of ₹95,962 crore** (excluding state share) to replace **MGNREGA** without disrupting **150+ million workers**, ensuring **zero work gap** during transition.
- **Uttar Pradesh** leads with the **highest interim allocation of ₹9,721.48 crore**, followed by **West Bengal (₹8,508 crore)** and **Tamil Nadu (₹7,585.49 crore)**, reflecting **population and poverty-weighted distribution**.
- The **final funding formula**, expected by **July 1, 2026**, will use the **16th Finance Commission’s horizontal devolution formula**, potentially altering **state-wise shares** from the **MGNREGA’s demand-driven model**.
- **West Bengal’s pending MGNREGA dues of ₹8,508 crore** remain unresolved despite the **BJP government’s assurance** to comply with **Central rules**, highlighting **fiscal federalism challenges**.
- **26 States** have completed **procedural readiness**, while **Jharkhand, Karnataka, Telangana, and Mizoram** lag, delaying their **VB-GRAMG implementation** due to **pending e-KYC and capacity-building exercises**.
- The scheme’s **₹1.25 lakh crore total outlay** (₹95,962 crore Centre + **40% state contribution**) underscores **shared fiscal responsibility** under **Article 275 of the Constitution**, aligning with **Cooperative Federalism**.

---

### **Syllabus Tag**
### **Key Terms**
### Syllabus Tag: GS Paper 2 — Governance: Rural Development Schemes, Federalism, and Fiscal Policies
### Key Terms: VB-GRAMG, MGNREGA, 16th Finance Commission, Cooperative Federalism, e-KYC, Blackout Period, Viksit Bharat @2047, Article 275, Horizontal Devolution Formula
### Category: Polity

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
The Ratan Tata Innovation Hub (RTIH) has invited applications for its RTIH Rural Entrepreneurship Fellows Programme aimed at promoting entrepreneurship, innovation and livelihood opportunities in rural areas. The fellowship programme will be implemented in Guntur, Kakinada, Chittoor and Maredumilli-Polavaram (Rampachodavaram) regions.
A statement issued on Tuesday said under the programme, selected fellows will work closely with rural youth, women, farmers, self-help groups and micro-entrepreneurs to encourage entrepreneurship and support livelihood development initiatives.
The RTIH said the programme seeks to strengthen grassroots innovation and create sustainable economic opportunities in rural communities.
Eligibility criteria
Candidates with a degree in any discipline, proficiency in Telugu and English and working knowledge of MS Office can apply. Preference will be given to applicants with experience in rural entrepreneurship, rural development programmes or community engagement activities.
Selected fellows will receive a monthly stipend of ₹10,000. In addition, they will gain practical exposure to grassroots entrepreneurship initiatives, mentoring from experts and opportunities to participate in rural development and innovation programmes. The fellowship is expected to provide valuable field experience and leadership skills for young professionals interested in social and economic development.
The RTIH officials said the programme is designed to nurture a new generation of rural development leaders capable of driving innovation and entrepreneurship at the grassroots level.
Interested candidates can submit their applications online until June 19. Further details can be obtained through the email ID gri.rtih@gmail.com or by contacting 9154562856.
Published
- June 09, 2026 09:04 pm IST
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:

  - **Deadline**: Applications open until **June 19, 2026**.

- **Key actors**:
  - **Ratan Tata Innovation Hub (RTIH)** – A **Tata Trusts** initiative promoting **grassroots innovation**.
  - **Target beneficiaries**: Rural youth, women, farmers, **Self-Help Groups (SHGs)**, and micro-entrepreneurs.
  - **Mentors**: Experts in **rural development and innovation**.

- **Why it matters**:
  - Aligns with **Government of India’s** **Atmanirbhar Bharat** and **Viksit Bharat 2047** visions by fostering **rural economic empowerment**.
  - Complements **Andhra Pradesh’s** **Mission Kakatiya** (rural transformation) and **PM-KUSUM** (solar-based livelihoods).
  - Addresses **unemployment in rural India** (as per **PLFS 2022-23**, **18.6% youth unemployment** in rural areas).

- **Significance**:
  - **Field-based leadership training** for young professionals in **social entrepreneurship**.
  - **Monthly stipend of ₹10,000** provides financial support while offering **hands-on experience** in **grassroots innovation**.
  - **Telugu proficiency requirement** ensures **localized impact** and **community engagement**.
  - **Precedent**: Similar to **NITI Aayog’s Atal Incubation Centres** but focused on **rural entrepreneurship** rather than urban startups.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Ratan Tata Innovation Hub (RTIH)**, a **Tata Trusts** initiative, has launched a **₹10,000-per-month fellowship** to train **rural entrepreneurs** in **Andhra Pradesh’s Guntur, Kakinada, Chittoor, and Maredumalli-Polavaram** regions under its **RTIH Rural Entrepreneurship Fellows Programme**.
- The programme targets **rural youth, women, farmers, and Self-Help Groups (SHGs)**, reflecting **Government of India’s focus on inclusive rural development** as outlined in the **15th Finance Commission’s recommendations** for **gram panchayat-level economic empowerment**.
- **Eligibility requires proficiency in Telugu and English**, highlighting the need for **linguistic and cultural alignment** in rural development programmes, similar to **PM-GKAY’s regional language outreach**.
- The initiative aligns with **Andhra Pradesh’s Mission Kakatiya**, which aims to **revitalize 6,600 villages** through **infrastructure and livelihood projects**, with **₹14,600 crore** allocated for rural transformation.
- **PLFS 2022-23 data** shows **18.6% youth unemployment in rural India**, making such fellowships critical for **skill development and economic absorption** in non-urban areas.
- The programme provides **mentorship from experts** and exposure to **innovation ecosystems**, mirroring **NITI Aayog’s Startup India** but with a **rural-first approach**, addressing the **urban-rural innovation divide**.

---

Trinamool Congress chief Mamata Banerjee arrives for the INDIA bloc meeting at Constitution Club of India, in New Delhi on June 8, 2026.
                                          | Photo Credit: ANI
The Criminal Investigation Department (CID) of the
West Bengal
police searched the residence of former Chief Minister Mamata Banerjee in Kolkata on Tuesday (June 9, 2026), in connection with the alleged forgery of the signatures of two Trinamool Congress MLAs.
The CID officials said that they wanted to search the premises since the 30B, Harish Chatterjee Street address is also referred to as the party office of the Trinamool Congress. After a standoff with party officials, the CID team entered the premises around 4 p.m. and carried out their search.
Former Trinamool Congress MP Subhasish Chakraborty said he could not allow the CID officials inside as both Ms. Banerjee and her nephew and party general secretary Abhishek Banerjee were not present in the house. He urged the officials to come back a day or two later when one of them would be present. However, CID officials countered, saying that they had come to search the TMC party office on the premises, and not the residence of Ms. Banerjee, and accusing Mr. Chakraborty of “obstructing the investigation”.
Why is there a rebellion in the Trinamool Congress? | Explained
Forged signatures case
The CID is investigating a letter nominating Sovandeb Chattopadhyay as the Leader of Opposition in the State Assembly, which was submitted to the Speaker on May 20. Trinamool MLAs Ritabarta Banerjee and Sandipan Saha alleged that their signatures on the letter had been forged. They were then expelled from the party.
An FIR was registered on the basis of their complaint at Kolkata’s Hare Street police station and the case was later handed over to the CID. The State agency then issued several summons to Mr. Abhishek Banerjee, who had signed the letter. However, he did not appear in person, citing health issues, and sought 15 days more to

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
A survey of tenant farmers across Telangana has found their widespread exclusion from crucial government agricultural support systems, raising concerns over the viability of cultivation for lakhs of farmers who lease land but lack formal recognition.
The findings emerged from a 2026 survey conducted by the Kaulu Raitula Gurtimpu Sadhana Committee (Tenant Farmers’ Recognition Struggle Committee), which covered 1,816 tenant farmers across 57 villages in 47 mandals spanning 22 districts. The study found that tenant cultivators face major barriers in accessing subsidised fertilizers, crop procurement at Minimum Support Price (MSP), disaster relief and institutional crop loans.
According to the report, access to subsidised urea has become increasingly difficult due to a recently introduced app-based system requiring landowners’ One-Time Password (OTP) verification. During the recent Rabi season, only 412 of the 1,816 surveyed tenant farmers constituting about 22.8 per cent could purchase subsidised urea at the official rate. Only 40 tenants managed to secure the fertilizer under their own names, while others depended on landowners’ identities.
Those unable to access the subsidy reportedly purchased urea from the open market at nearly double the subsidised price.
Tenant farmers also reported major hurdles in selling crops through government procurement systems. Platforms such as Kapas Kisan for cotton, Online Procurement Management System for paddy and Markfed procurement require Pattas and Aadhaar linkage, or OTP-based approval from the land owner, making it very difficult for tenant farmers to sell to government procurement agencies, the survey found.
As per the report, only 6.7 per cent of cotton-growing tenant farmers sold produce directly to the Cotton Corporation of India in their own names. For paddy, only 20 per cent of tenants in Kharif-2025 and 17.2 per cent in Rabi-2025 could access procurement under their own identities. Many farmers reportedly resorted to sel
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - A **2026 survey** by the **Kaulu Raitula Gurtimpu Sadhana Committee** (Tenant Farmers’ Recognition Struggle Committee) in **Telangana** revealed **systemic exclusion** of **tenant farmers** from **government agricultural support schemes**.
  - Covered **1,816 tenant farmers** across **57 villages, 47 mandals, and 22 districts**.
- **Key actors**:
  - **Tenant farmers** (leaseholders, not landowners).
  - **Telangana government** (responsible for **MSP procurement, fertilizer subsidies, disaster relief**).
  - **Landowners** (blocking access to subsidies/loans via **OTP-based verification**).
  - **Institutions**: **Cotton Corporation of India (CCI)**, **Markfed**, **Online Procurement Management System (OPMS)**.
- **Why it matters**:
  - Highlights **structural inequality** in **agricultural welfare schemes**, violating **right to livelihood** (Article 21) and **equality before law** (Article 14).
  - **77% of tenant farmers** excluded from **subsidized urea**, **93%+ from MSP procurement**, and **89% from disaster relief**—undermining **PM-KISAN, PMFBY, and MSP Act 2022**.
  - **Private moneylenders** exploit tenants with **24–36% interest loans**, deepening rural debt traps.
- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for national debate**: **~50% of India’s farm laborers are tenant cultivators** (NSSO 2019), but **no uniform tenant recognition system** exists.
  - **Legal gap**: **2011 Land Licensed Cultivators Act (Telangana)** remains **poorly implemented**; **no central law** for tenant rights.
  - **Tech exclusion**: **Aadhaar-OTP-based systems** (e.g., **e-NAM, DBT**) disproportionately harm **landless/tenant farmers**.
  - **Economic impact**: **Higher input costs** (urea at **double price**) and **lower MSP access** reduce **tenant incomes**, worsening **agricultural distress**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Agricultural subsidies in Telangana face a **90%+ exclusion rate** for tenant farmers**, with only **22.8% accessing subsidized urea** and **6.7% selling cotton under their own names** via **Cotton Corporation of India (CCI)** in **2026**, per the **Kaulu Raitula Gurtimpu Sadhana Committee** survey.
- The **Telangana government’s digital verification system** (requiring **landowner OTP approval**) for **fertilizer subsidies and MSP procurement** has **denied 1,404 tenant farmers** access to **PM-KISAN-linked benefits**, despite **85% reporting crop losses** from **floods/hailstorms** in the past **3 years**.
- **Disaster relief compensation** under **PMFBY (Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana)** reached only **11 tenant farmers** in **Telangana**, while **landowners siphoned funds** in **89% of cases**, exposing **loopholes in the 2016 Insurance Act** implementation.
- **Tenant farmers in Telangana** face **24–36% interest rates** from **private moneylenders** due to **exclusion from institutional crop loans** (e.g., **Kisan Credit Cards**), worsening **rural indebtedness**—a **key factor in farmer suicides** (NCRB data shows **10,281 farmer suicides in 2022**).
- The **2011 Land Licensed Cultivators Act (Telangana)** mandates **tenant recognition**, but **ad-hoc implementation** leaves **1.8 million+ tenant farmers** without **tenant cultivator cards**, critical for **direct benefit transfers (DBT)** under **Agriculture Infrastructure Fund (AIF)** and **PM-AASHA**.
- **MSP procurement gaps** for tenant farmers—**only 20% of paddy tenants** in **Kharif-2025** and **17.2% in Rabi-2025** sold via **Markfed/OPMS**—highlight **structural flaws in the 2022 MSP Act**, which lacks **tenant-specific provisions** despite **~40% of India’s cro

Telangana has procured a record quantity of 147 lakh tonnes of paddy in the 2025-26 agriculture (procurement) year, which is equivalent to 61% of the paddy procured in the country, despite the Centre reducing the lifting of custom milled rice after processing of the paddy procured at minimum support price based on the fair average quality norms, Civil Supplies and Irrigation Minister N. Uttam Kumar Reddy announced.
The achievement was possible even after the State’s repeated requests to the Centre, Ministry of Food and Public Distribution, to enhance the procurement quantity, fell on deaf ears, Mr. Uttam Kumar Reddy said addressing a press conference here on Tuesday. Stating that Telangana had procured 75 lakh tonnes of paddy this Rabi season so far and another 1-2 lakh tonnes more was expected over the next few days.
Hitting out at the Opposition Bharat Rashtra Samithi (BRS) and Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) for spreading lies about paddy procurement and misleading farmers, he said that both the parties did nothing to help farmers but had only created hurdles in procurement.
He noted that the procurement of paddy was just in this year’s vicinity in 2020-21, the COVID affected year when purchase of paddy by traders and millers was not possible even to a small extent and the entire procurement was by the Civil Supplies Department, when it was a little over 141 lakh tonnes (1.41 crore tonnes) and this year (2025-26) it was 147 lakh tonnes so far, out of a total estimated production of 295 lakh tonnes.
The Minister said a total of ₹35,077 crore amount was credited to the 25.2 lakh farmers’ bank accounts in KMS 2025-26 in lieu of paddy purchased from them and as the Centre had put limit on procurement for CMR the State Government had to spared ₹6,650 crore from the budget for paddy procurement and borrowed another ₹16,649 crore from banks for the purpose.
For the Rabi season, the government had disbursed ₹17,917 crore for the procurement of 75 lakh tonnes of pa
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Telangana** achieved a record **147 lakh tonnes (61% of India’s total)** of **paddy procurement** in **2025-26 (Kharif & Rabi seasons)**, surpassing even **COVID-19-affected 2020-21 (141 lakh tonnes)**.
  - Procurement was done **despite Centre’s restrictions** on **Custom Milled Rice (CMR)** lifting post-**Minimum Support Price (MSP)** processing under **Fair Average Quality (FAQ) norms**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **N. Uttam Kumar Reddy** (**Civil Supplies & Irrigation Minister, Telangana**).
  - **Ministry of Food & Public Distribution (MoFPD), Government of India** (imposed procurement limits).
  - **Bharat Rashtra Samithi (BRS)** and **Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)** (criticized for "misleading farmers" on procurement).
  - **Civil Supplies Department, Telangana** (handled procurement during COVID-19).

- **Why It Matters**:
  - Highlights **Telangana’s agricultural success** under **KCR’s government**, contrasting with **Centre’s procurement policies**.
  - **MSP & procurement dynamics** remain a **GS Paper 3 (Agriculture) and GS Paper 2 (Governance)** hotspot.
  - **Budgetary strain** due to **₹35,077 crore disbursed to farmers**, with **₹23,299 crore** borrowed from banks (shows **fiscal stress in procurement-driven states**).

- **Significance**:
  - **Procurement centres**: **8,448 (Kharif) + 8,575 (Rabi)**—highest in India, ensuring **farmer accessibility**.
  - **Gunny bag usage**: **20.86 crore bags** used (Rabi 2025-26), debunking **Opposition’s false claims**.
  - **Drenched paddy procurement**: **12,557 tonnes** bought at **MSP**, processed into **parboiled rice** (shows **value addition in agriculture**).
  - **Precedent**: **2020-21 (COVID) vs. 2025-26**—proves **state-level resilience** in procurement despite **Central constraints**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Telangana’s paddy procurement in 2025-26 hit a record **147 lakh tonnes**, accounting for **61% of India’s total**, despite the **Centre capping Custom Milled Rice (CMR) lifting** under **Fair Average Quality (FAQ) norms** for MSP-processed paddy.
- The state **disbursed ₹35,077 crore** to **25.2 lakh farmers** for paddy procurement, with **₹6,650 crore** from the budget and **₹16,649 crore** borrowed from banks, exposing **fiscal challenges in procurement-driven states**.
- **Telangana operated the highest number of procurement centres in India—8,448 (Kharif) and 8,575 (Rabi)—**ensuring **last-mile connectivity** for farmers, a model for **agri-logistics efficiency**.
- The **Ministry of Food & Public Distribution (MoFPD)** imposed **restrictions on CMR lifting**, forcing states like **Telangana** to **adjust procurement strategies**, a **policy shift with implications for food security and farmer income**.
- **Rabi 2025-26 procurement (75 lakh tonnes) saw ₹17,917 crore disbursed**, including **12,557 tonnes of drenched paddy** processed into **parboiled rice**, showcasing **post-harvest value addition** under **MSP framework**.
- **Telangana’s procurement in 2025-26 surpassed even the COVID-19-hit 2020-21 (141 lakh tonnes)**, when **private traders/millers were inactive**, proving **government-led procurement as a safety net** for farmers.

---

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Karnataka’s annual average production of onions is more than two million tonnes, but rains play an important role in cultivation.
                                          | Photo Credit: SUDHAKARA JAIN
Onions entering vegetable markets in Bengaluru in recent days are covered with black fungus or mould, as untimely rains hit several regions before the southwest monsoon set in. This has also led to such onions being sold at lower rates. While unaffected onions are being sold at ₹20 to 25 per kg, those affected by fungus are being sold for ₹15.
Karnataka’s annual average production of onions is more than two million tonnes, but rains play an important role in cultivation. According to horticulture department officials, last year onions were grown on 1,52,000 hectares in the State, and every year the number of growers is increasing as they get returns within three to four months, unlike other crops.
K. Dhanraj, Joint Director of Horticulture (Vegetables), explained, “Black mould on onions is a post-harvest disease that is most prevalent during the rainy season, when there is higher humidity in the atmosphere, especially in Bengaluru. It is popularly known as
Aspergillus niger
. This post-harvest disease is seen in the Kharif harvest of onions. During the Rabi season, the harvest undergoes a process of curing when weather conditions are favourable, which helps dry the outer skin and prevents fungal growth during long-term storage.”
He added, “Such post-harvest diseases are not seen in dry weather conditions. Farmers make the most of dry weather and sunlight by spreading the onions. However, in rain-affected regions, fungal blight develops on the onions, and they also begin to rot from the inside. At present, in places like Challakere and Hosadurga, farmers have started sowing seeds for onion cultivation after the State received a few days of rain and the soil became softer. Districts like Vijayapura, Bagalkot, Gadag, and Koppal, among others, are known for growing onion
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Black mould (Aspergillus niger)** infestation in **Karnataka’s onions** due to **untimely pre-monsoon rains (June 2026)**, causing **post-harvest fungal decay** and price drops (₹15/kg for affected vs. ₹20–25/kg for unaffected).
  - **Kharif onions** (sown June, harvested Sept–Oct) are worst hit; **Rabi onions** (sown Oct, harvested Jan–Feb) undergo **curing (14–20 days drying)** to prevent fungal growth.
  - **Regions affected**: **Challakere, Hosadurga, Vijayapura, Bagalkot, Gadag, Koppal** (major onion-growing districts).

- **Key Actors**:
  - **K. Dhanraj** (Joint Director, **Horticulture Department, Karnataka**): Explained **Aspergillus niger** as a **humidity-driven post-harvest disease**.
  - **Shalini M.** (Associate Professor, **Department of Horticulture, Karnataka**): Highlighted **premature harvesting** due to rain fears as a root cause.
  - **B. Ravishankar** (Secretary, **Bangalore Onion and Potato Merchants Association**): Compared **Karnataka’s storage gaps** with **Madhya Pradesh/Maharashtra’s subsidized iron-mesh storage units**.
  - **Veeran Gowda Patil** (Onion farmer, **Navalgund**): Reported **price cuts to 50%** for water-damaged onions.

- **Why It Matters**:
  - **Agricultural Economics**: Demonstrates **climate vulnerability** of **Kharif crops** and **supply chain fragility** in perishable commodities.
  - **Food Security**: **Price volatility** (₹5–₹150/kg swings) impacts **urban consumers** (e.g., **Bengaluru**), linking to **inflation and MSP debates**.
  - **Governance**: Highlights **suboptimal adoption of storage subsidies** (e.g., **iron-mesh structures**) despite **central/state schemes** like **PM-KUSUM or Agri-Infrastructure Fund**.
  - **Inter-State Trade**: **Maharashtra/MP’s stockpiling** (20–50 bags/farmer) acts as a **price stabilizer** when **Karnataka’s output dips**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Monsoon-Climate Nexus**: **Pre-monsoon rains (June 2026)** disrupt **Kharif onion curing**, mirroring **2024’s heatwave-induced onion crisis** (₹100/kg spikes).
  - **Policy Gap**: **Lack of farmer awareness** on **curing protocols** vs. **trader pressure** to sell early for higher prices.
  - **Economic Geography**: **Northern Karnataka (Vijayapura, Gadag)** emerges as a **critical onion hub**, competing with **Maharashtra’s Nashik** and **Madhya Pradesh’s Indore**.
  - **UPSC Link**: Connects to **GS3 (Agriculture, Food Processing, Climate Change)** and **GS2 (Governance: Subsidies, Farmer Suicides, MSP)**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Karnataka’s onion production** averages **over 2 million tonnes annually**, with **1,52,000 hectares** under cultivation (2025), driven by **quick returns (3–4 months)** compared to long-cycle crops like sugarcane.
- **Aspergillus niger**, the **black mould fungus**, thrives in **humidity >60%** and **poorly cured onions**, reducing market value by **30–40%** and causing **internal rot** even if external skin appears intact.
- **Kharif vs. Rabi onions**: **Kharif (June sowing, Sept–Oct harvest)** is **high-risk** due to **monsoon rains**, while **Rabi (Oct sowing, Jan–Feb harvest)** benefits from **dry curing**, but **premature harvesting** (due to rain fears) triggers **fungal outbreaks**.
- **Storage technology gap**: **Maharashtra and Madhya Pradesh** use **subsidized iron-mesh storage units with fans** (cost: **₹50,000–₹1 lakh/unit**), while **Karnataka’s adoption is <20%** despite **central subsidies under the **Agri-Infrastructure Fund (AIF)** (₹1 lakh/ha).

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Deputy Chief Minister and Minister for Environment and Forests K. Pawan Kalyan reviewing pollution level in river Godavari at a meeting in Amaravati.
                                          | Photo Credit: BY ARRANGEMENT
Deputy Chief Minister and Minister for Environment and Forests K. Pawan Kalyan has directed the Andhra Pradesh Pollution Control Board (APPCB) to serve notices to Andhra Paper Limited (APL) and Rajamahendravaram Municipal Corporation (RMC) authorities for failing to prevent pollution of the Godavari river upstream of the Pushkar ghat in Rajamahendravaram city.
The directive came on June 8, following a scientific probe by the APPCB which revealed alarming levels of water pollution.
On May 25, the APPCB authorities, led by Mr. Pawan Kalyan, collected water samples from two locations— The lagoon through which the Andhra Paper Limited releases effluents and the Kotilingala Ghat, where untreated drainage water is let into the river through the Nalla channel.
On June 8, Mr. Pawan Kalyan convened a high-level meeting with APPCB Chairman P. Krishnaiah, AP Pollution Control Board Member Secretary S. Saravanan, East Godavari District Collector Kirthi Chekuri, and Rajamahendravaram Municipal Corporation Commissioner Rahul Meena.
Threat to aqua species:
Dismayed at the alarming levels of water pollution in the Godavari river, Mr. Pawan Kalyan said: “The levels of coliform bacteria, biochemical oxygen demand, nitrate, phosphate, sulphide and biochemical oxygen were found to be above permissible levels in the water samples collected from the Godavari river. These pose a major threat to aqua species and cannot be recommended for drinking”.
Blue Bay Syndrome:
In an official release, Mr. Pawan Kalyan further stated: “Nitrate levels have been found to be  119.6 mg /litre as against the normal level of 45 mg/litre in the water sample collected from the Andhra Paper Limited’s lagoon. This level of nitrate could pose severe health risks, including cause Blue Baby Synd
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Andhra Pradesh Pollution Control Board (APPCB)** detected **alarming pollution levels** in the **Godavari River** upstream of **Pushkar Ghat (Rajamahendravaram)** on **June 8, 2026**.
  - Water samples collected on **May 25, 2026**, from **Andhra Paper Limited’s lagoon** and **Kotilingala Ghat** revealed **excessive coliform bacteria, BOD, nitrates, phosphates, and sulfides**—all **above permissible limits**.
  - **Blue Baby Syndrome (Methemoglobinemia) risk** identified due to **nitrate levels of 119.6 mg/L** (vs. safe limit of **45 mg/L**).

- **Key Actors**:
  - **K. Pawan Kalyan** (Deputy CM & **Environment & Forests Minister, AP**).
  - **APPCB** (led by **Chairman P. Krishnaiah** and **Member Secretary S. Saravanan**).
  - **Andhra Paper Limited (APL)** and **Rajamahendravaram Municipal Corporation (RMC)**—notices issued for **violation of pollution norms**.
  - **East Godavari District Collector Kirthi Chekuri** directed to submit a **Detailed Project Report (DPR)** for remediation.
  - **Rahul Meena** (RMC Commissioner) and **APPCB officials in Kakinada Division** face disciplinary action for **monitoring failures**.

- **Why It Matters (UPSC/India Angle)**:
  - **Godavari River** is a **national waterway (NW-2)** and **sacred pilgrimage site** (hosting **Godavari Pushkaram-2027**).
  - Pollution threatens **aquatic biodiversity**, **drinking water safety**, and **religious tourism** (lakhs of devotees rely on river water for rituals).
  - Highlights **industrial and municipal effluent mismanagement**, linking to **National Green Tribunal (NGT) and Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act, 1974**.
  - **Blue Baby Syndrome** is a **public health emergency**, requiring **inter-state coordination** (Godavari flows through **5 states: Maharashtra, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Odisha**).

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for NGT interventions**: Similar cases include **Ganga (2014) and Yamuna (2017) pollution crackdowns**.
  - **Economic impact**: Pollution disrupts **aquaculture, tourism, and agriculture** (Godavari basin supports **10% of AP’s GDP**).
  - **Legal accountability**: **APPCB’s failure to monitor** raises questions about **state pollution boards’ efficacy** under **Environment Protection Act, 1986**.
  - **Climate change angle**: High **BOD and nutrient levels** indicate **eutrophication**, worsening **algal blooms**—a **global water security issue**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The Godavari River**, India’s **second-longest (1,465 km)**, is a **national waterway (NW-2)** and supports **40% of AP’s irrigation**, but faces **critical pollution** with **nitrate levels at 119.6 mg/L**—**2.66x above WHO’s safe limit of 45 mg/L** in samples from **Andhra Paper Limited’s lagoon**.
- **Blue Baby Syndrome (Methemoglobinemia)**, caused by **high nitrates (>10 mg/L)**, has been linked to **20+ child deaths annually in India**, primarily in **polluted river basins like Godavari and Ganga**, as per **CPCB’s 2023 report**.
- **Andhra Paper Limited (APL)**, a **public-sector undertaking**, was issued a **show-cause notice** under **Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 (Section 33)**, for discharging **untreated effluents** into the **Godavari**, violating **Consent to Establish (CTE) and Consent to Operate (CTO) norms**.
- **Rajamahendravaram Municipal Corporation (RMC)** was penalized for **illegal drainage discharge** via the **Nalla channel** into the **Kotilingala Ghat**, where **E. coli levels exceeded 5,000 MPN/100mL**—**100x the safe limit of

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Praveen Chakravarty
                                          | Photo Credit: RAGU R
Following the completion of scrutiny of nominations for the by-election to the lone vacant Rajya Sabha seat from Tamil Nadu on Tuesday, the nomination paper of Congress candidate Praveen Chakravarty alone was declared valid. The TVK had allotted the seat to the Congress, its post-poll ally, which has five MLAs.
An official release from the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly Secretariat said the nominations of the remaining 12 independent candidates were rejected. The vacancy arose following the resignation of AIADMK leader C. Ve. Shanmugam from the Upper House after his successful election to the Tamil Nadu Assembly from Mailam.
As only Mr. Chakravarty’s nomination has been accepted, he is expected to be declared elected unopposed.
Published
- June 09, 2026 09:41 pm IST
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **By-election for the lone vacant Rajya Sabha seat from Tamil Nadu** was held on **June 9, 2026**, following scrutiny of nominations.
  - **Praveen Chakravarty (Congress)** emerged as the **only valid nominee**, declared elected **unopposed** after 12 independent candidates were rejected.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Praveen Chakravarty** (Congress candidate, **post-poll ally of TVK** in Tamil Nadu).
  - **Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly Secretariat** (rejected 12 independent nominations).
  - **C. Ve. Shanmugam (AIADMK)** (resigned from Rajya Sabha after winning **Tamil Nadu Assembly election from Mailam** in 2026).

- **Why It Matters**:
  - Highlights **post-poll alliances** (Congress-TMK) and **Rajya Sabha seat allocation dynamics** in coalition politics.
  - Demonstrates **procedural rigor** in **Rajya Sabha by-elections** under **Article 80 & 84** of the Constitution.
  - Reflects **state-party politics** (AIADMK’s shift from Rajya Sabha to Assembly) and **Congress’s strategic seat management**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Unopposed election** underscores **Congress’s dominance** in Tamil Nadu’s political landscape post-2026 polls.
  - **Vacancy arose due to dual membership** (Rajya Sabha + Assembly), testing **anti-defection norms** (10th Schedule).
  - **TVK’s role** as a **kingmaker** in Tamil Nadu’s **post-2024 election scenario** (Congress’s reliance on regional allies).

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The **Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly Secretariat** rejected **12 independent nominations** for the **Rajya Sabha by-election**, leaving **Praveen Chakravarty (Congress)** as the **sole valid candidate**, leading to an **unopposed election** under **Article 84(1) of the Constitution**, which mandates scrutiny of nominations by the **Returning Officer** (here, the Assembly Secretariat).**
- **The vacancy in the Rajya Sabha seat from Tamil Nadu occurred after **C. Ve. Shanmugam (AIADMK)**, a **former Rajya Sabha MP**, resigned to contest and win the **Tamil Nadu Assembly election from Mailam**, triggering a **by-election** under **Article 80(2)** (composition of Rajya Sabha) and **Article 84(3)** (vacation of seats).**
- **The **Congress’s nomination** was allotted by the **TVK (Thiruvalluvar Kunju)**, a **post-poll ally** with **5 MLAs**, reflecting the **coalition arithmetic** in Tamil Nadu’s **2026 Assembly elections**, where **Congress emerged as the single-largest party** but relied on allies for governance.**
- **Praveen Chakravarty’s election is significant as it marks the **first unopposed Rajya Sabha by-election in Tamil Nadu since 2021**, highlighting the **declining contestation** in Upper House elections due to **strategic alliances** and **lack of viable opposition candidates**.**
- **The **anti-defection law (10th Schedule)** was implicitly tested as **Shanmugam’s resignation** from Rajya Sabha to join the Assembly could be scrutinized under **Section 7(2)**, which disqualifies MPs for **voluntarily giving up membership** without prior permission.**
- **Tamil Nadu’s **Rajya Sabha representation** (currently **18 seats**) is determined by **Article 80**, which allocates seats based on **state population**, with **18 seats fixed** since the **1971 Census**, though demands for **reallocation** based on **2011 Census** have been pending.**

---

### **Syllabus Tag**
### **Key Terms**
### Syllabus Tag: GS Paper 2 — Polity & Governance: Constitutional Bodies (Rajya Sabha), Election Process, Anti-Defection Law, Federalism & Coalition Politics
### Key Terms: Rajya Sabha by-election, Article 80 & 84 (Constitution), Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly Secretariat, TVK (Thiruvalluvar Kunju), Anti-Defection Law (10th Schedule), C. Ve. Shanmugam, Praveen Chakravarty, Unopposed Election
### Category: Polity

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Congress MP Sonia Gandhi in a conversation with TMC supremo Mamata Banerjee during the INDIA bloc meeting in New Delhi, on June 8, 2026. Photo: Handout via PTI Photo.
A day after the INDIA bloc meeting, Trinamool Congress national president
Mamata Banerjee
met senior Congress leader Sonia Gandhi at her 10, Janpath residence on Tuesday (June 9, 2026).
Ms. Banerjee had a long conversation with Ms. Gandhi at the INDIA bloc meeting as well.
Sources said that on Tuesday, the two discussed strategies for the future, with the possibility of an alliance for the 2029 General Election also on the cards.
Ms. Banerjee who reached Delhi for the INDIA bloc meeting, had decided to stay back for another day, to meet Ms. Gandhi. The Congress shared pictures of the bonhomie between the two leaders. This was the first closed-door meeting between Ms. Banerjee and Ms. Gandhi after the Trinamool’s defeat in the polls, and the alleged attacks on party workers and MP Abhishek Banerjee.
Coinciding with her meeting with Ms. Gandhi, the West Bengal CID conducted searches at the Trinamool Congress headquarters and Ms. Banerjee’s residence. The party’s newly appointed joint secretary, Derek O’ Brien, in a post on X, said it was part of political vendetta by the government ruling the Centre and State. “EXACTLY AT THE SAME TIME as @AITCofficial Chairperson @MamataOfficial was having a meeting today in Delhi with Mrs Sonia Gandhi, CID was forcefully trying to enter the AITC party HQ adjoining residence in Kolkata. First loot vote, now loot files of a political rival. Democracy ? Main entrance overloaded with CRPF,” he posted.
Ms. Banerjee also met former Delhi Chief Minister and AAP convener Arvind Kejriwal.
Published
- June 09, 2026 07:38 pm IST
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Closed-door meeting** between **Trinamool Congress (TMC) leader Mamata Banerjee** and **Congress president Sonia Gandhi** on **June 9, 2026**, at **10 Janpath, New Delhi**, following the **INDIA bloc meeting** (June 8, 2026).
  - Discussions focused on **future electoral strategies**, including a **potential alliance for the 2029 Lok Sabha elections**.
- **Key actors**:
  - **Mamata Banerjee** (TMC), **Sonia Gandhi** (Congress), **Arvind Kejriwal** (AAP, met separately).
  - **Derek O’Brien** (TMC joint secretary) criticized **CID raids** on **TMC HQ and Banerjee’s residence** as a **"political vendetta"**.
- **Why it matters**:
  - Reflects **shifting alliances** in **India’s opposition politics**, with **INDIA bloc** (Congress-led) courting **TMC** post-**West Bengal’s 2024 electoral defeat**.
  - Highlights **intra-opposition tensions** (e.g., **TMC’s allegations of electoral fraud** vs. **Congress’s outreach**).
  - **Constitutional angle**: **Coalition dynamics** (Article 78, **Council of Ministers’ collective responsibility**) and **electoral alliances** (Article 324, **Election Commission’s role**).
- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for post-defeat reconciliation** in regional parties (cf. **BJP’s 2019 alliances** with **Shiv Sena, JD(U)**).
  - **West Bengal’s political instability** (TMC’s **2024 defeat**, **Abhishek Banerjee’s arrest**, **CID raids**) tests **opposition unity**.
