Bengaluru Development Minister Krishna Byre Gowda.
                                          | Photo Credit: file photo
The discontentment of Krishna Byre Gowda over being given a truncated Bengaluru Development portfolio — without the Bengaluru Development Authority (BDA) and the Bengaluru Metropolitan Region Development Authority (BMRDA), which are held by Chief Minister D.K. Shivakumar — seems to have reached the doors of the Congress Central leadership.
The stand-off comes at a crucial time when the monsoon has set in over the State. There are also no elected councillors in Bengaluru since elections to five corporations under the Greater Bengaluru Authority (GBA) are yet to be held.
Mr. Shivakumar will be in Delhi on Wednesday to meet senior party leaders including Sonia Gandhi and Leader of Opposition in Lok Sabha Rahul Gandhi. Mr. Gowda, who is yet to take charge of the portfolio, is also in Delhi.
Sources close to Mr. Gowda said that the Minister had gone to Delhi along with some legislators, who are seeking Cabinet berths. It is learnt that he has informed both the Chief Minister and Congress general secretary in charge of Karnataka Randeep Singh Surjewala that he will not take charge of the portfolio unless the issue is resolved, and that he is ready to take any other portfolio.
The Chief Minister is expected to explain to the leaders on Wednesday why he is keen to keep the BDA and the BMRDA. Sources close to him said: “The BDA and the BMRDA have been kept by the Chief Minister since he has worked on projects that are under the two agencies. By the time the new Minister will be able to understand its intricacies, the term would be coming to an end and projects could be derailed.”
The Chief Minister will be in Delhi to attend the Niti Ayog meeting on Thursday, and has also invited IAS officers for a dinner meeting.
Among the projects that were taken up when Mr. Shivakumar held the Bengaluru Development portfolio include the ambitious tunnel projects, the Per
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Karnataka Congress Minister **Krishna Byre Gowda** refused to take charge of the truncated **Bengaluru Development portfolio** (excluding **BDA** and **BMRDA**), sparking a leadership conflict with **CM **D.K. Shivakumar** and **Congress Central**.
  - **When**: June 2026 (post-monsoon season, pre-**Greater Bengaluru Authority (GBA)** elections).
  - **Where**: **Delhi** (meetings with **Sonia Gandhi**, **Rahul Gandhi**, and **Randeep Surjewala**).

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Krishna Byre Gowda** (Minister-to-be, demanding full portfolio or alternative assignment).
  - **D.K. Shivakumar** (CM, retaining **BDA/BMRDA** for project continuity).
  - **Congress Central Leadership** (mediating the dispute).
  - **Randeep Surjewala** (**Congress General Secretary** for Karnataka).

- **Why It Matters**:
  - Highlights **inter-departmental coordination failures** in **urban governance**, a **GS Paper 2** (Polity) and **GS Paper 3** (Infrastructure) concern.
  - Reflects **power struggles in coalition governments** (Karnataka’s **Congress-JD(S)** alliance) and **accountability gaps** in **statutory urban bodies**.
  - **Bengaluru’s development** hinges on **BDA/BMRDA**, with **critical projects** (tunnels, **Peripheral Ring Road**, **Bidadi Township**) at stake.

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for portfolio fragmentation**: Risks **project delays**, **cost escalation**, and **public accountability issues** (e.g., flooding, lake management).
  - **Monsoon season vulnerability**: **Bengaluru’s urban flooding** (linked to **BDA’s lake management**) exacerbates governance challenges.
  - **Electoral vacuum**: **No elected councillors** in **GBA** corporations, weakening **local governance**.
  - **Central intervention**: **Congress High Command’s role** in state portfolio allocations underscores **party discipline vs. state autonomy** tensions.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Bengaluru’s **Bengaluru Development Authority (BDA)** and **Bengaluru Metropolitan Region Development Authority (BMRDA)** oversee **12 major projects**, including **₹12,000 crore tunnel projects**, the **₹6,500 crore Peripheral Ring Road**, and the **₹3,000 crore Bidadi Integrated Township**, critical for **Karnataka’s **₹1.5 lakh crore urban infrastructure push**.
- **D.K. Shivakumar**, **Karnataka’s Chief Minister**, retained **BDA/BMRDA** to prevent **"project derailment"** during his tenure, citing **lack of time for new ministers to grasp intricacies**, a concern echoed in **PM Modi’s **₹10 lakh crore National Infrastructure Pipeline (NIP)** where **bureaucratic continuity** is prioritized.
- **Krishna Byre Gowda’s refusal** stems from **accountability risks** in a **split portfolio**, where **BDA’s Outer Ring Road delays** or **lake flooding incidents** (e.g., **2025’s **₹800 crore damage** from **Hesaraghatta Lake overflow**) could lead to **political blame games**, mirroring **Mumbai’s **Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC)** governance debates.
- **Greater Bengaluru Authority (GBA)**, covering **5 corporations**, has **no elected councillors** since **2024 elections were postponed**, leaving **₹2,000 crore annual urban funds** without **local democratic oversight**, akin to **Delhi’s **MCD elections** controversies.
- **Monsoon 2026** brought **30% excess rainfall** to **Karnataka**, testing **BDA’s drainage systems**, with **₹1,500 crore** allocated for **flood mitigation**—highlighting **climate-adaptive urban planning** gaps in **Smart Cities Mission** implementations.
- **Congress Central’s intervention** in **portfolio allocations** reflects **party-centric governance**, where **state leaders** (e.g., **Surjewala**) mediate **ministerial grievances**, similar to **UPA-era conflicts** between **state and central Congress units**.

---

Call for cooperation: If the Governor functions as per his whim, it will affect governance, says Higher Education Minister P. Viswanathan. 
                                          | Photo Credit: N. Sai Charan
Higher Education Minister P. Viswanathan on Tuesday (June 9, 2026) said that neither he nor the State government sought any confrontation with the Governor. However, the government would not compromise on the State’s autonomy in the appointment of Vice-Chancellors of State universities.
“I can only think of [lyricist] Kannadasan’s lines:
Vandi oda chakkarangal irandu mattum vendum; antha irandil onru siriyathendraal entha vandi odum
? (A vehicle needs two wheels of equal size; if one is smaller, how can it move?). The Governor and the Chief Minister should work in harmony for the smooth functioning of our universities,” he said in an interview to
The Hindu
.
‘CM doesn’t want spat’
Mr. Viswanathan said the previous government may have adopted a confrontational approach towards the Governor. “But our Chief Minister does not want any confrontation. As a Minister, I, too, do not want any confrontation. The matter [of appointment of Vice-Chancellors] is pending before the Supreme Court, and we hope the outcome will be favourable,” he said, expressing confidence that the new Governor would protect the State’s rights.
Contending that the appointment of Vice-Chancellors was directly linked to the State’s autonomy, he said no State government would surrender its rights. “We cannot allow the Governor to encroach upon or usurp the powers of the State,” he said.
Pointing out that the dispute over the appointment of Vice-Chancellors stemmed from the UGC’s mandate to include its nominee in the selection panel, besides the nominees of the syndicate, the State government, and the Governor, Mr. Viswanathan said the Centre was trying to bring higher education under its control through the UGC nominee.
He accused former Governor R.N. Ravi of having issued a notification consti
output:

  - The dispute stems from the **Governor’s inclusion of a **UGC nominee** in VC selection panels**, despite **Supreme Court rulings** and **State autonomy** concerns.

- **Key actors**:

  - **TN State government** (BJP-led) vs. **Governor’s office** (appointed by **Centre**).

- **Why it matters**:
  - **Federalism vs. Centralization**: Tests **State autonomy** in **higher education** governance under **Article 163 (Counsels of Governor)** and **Article 309 (State services)**.
  - **Constitutional Crisis**: Highlights **Governor’s role** as a **nominee of the Centre** vs. **State government’s elected mandate**.
  - **Judicial Overlap**: **UGC’s mandate** (via **National Education Policy 2020**) clashes with **State laws** (e.g., **TN State Universities Act**).

  - **Legal Battle**: **Supreme Court’s pending verdict** could redefine **VC selection norms**, impacting **1,000+ State universities** nationwide.
  - **Higher Ed Reforms**: Reflects **NEP 2020’s** push for **centralized oversight** vs. **State rights** under **7th Schedule (Entry 25: Education)**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**

- **Tamil Nadu’s 13 State universities** have faced **administrative paralysis** since **2021**, with **12/13 lacking Vice-Chancellors** due to **Governor-State government conflicts**, delaying **academic appointments** and **policy decisions**.
- **Former Governor **R.N. Ravi** issued a **controversial notification** (challenged in court) to include a **UGC nominee** in VC search panels, despite **earlier Supreme Court judgments** favoring **State governments’ primacy** in such appointments.

Viswanathan** invoked **Kannadasan’s lyric** ("*Vandi oda chakkarangal irandu mattum vendum*") to emphasize the need for **harmonious federalism**, warning that **Governor’s unilateral actions** could **disrupt governance** under **Article 163 (Counsels of Governor)**.
- The **UGC’s push** to include its nominee in **State university selection panels** aligns with **NEP 2020’s** goal of **centralized quality control**, but **opposition-ruled States** like **Tamil Nadu** argue it **encroaches on **Entry 25 (Education) of the **7th Schedule**.
- **Temporary lecturers’ crisis** in **TN universities** was flagged by the Minister, linking it to **VC appointment delays**, which affect **higher education quality** and **student admissions**—a **GS Paper 2 (Governance) and GS Paper 4 (Ethics) issue**.

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen speaks to the press on the 21st sanctions package against Russia in Brussels, Belgium, on June 9, 2026.
                                          | Photo Credit: Reuters
The European Commission has proposed its 21st package of
sanctions on Russia
which also includes Indian entities. The sanctions need to be approved by member states before they are finalised.
The proposed package focuses on energy, crypto and financial services and trade, including fisheries, European Commission President Ursula Von der Leyen said. The sanctions would also ban the entry of former Russian combatants into the EU.
“Brick by brick, we are collapsing the foundations of Russia’s war economy,” the EU’s top diplomat Kaja Kallas said on social media site X.
Also Read |
Three Indian firms among 45 entities sanctioned by EU for links with Russia
“The new listings will cover more than 30 designations in the drones manufacturing as well as new export control measures on 50 companies, including entities based in China, Türkiye, Kyrgyzstan, Kazakhstan, UAE and India,” Ms. Kallas wrote.
This is
not the first time Indian entities have found themselves on a EU sanctions list
. However, the news comes as Brussels and New Delhi are in the implementation phase of a trade agreement that was announced in February.
The sanctions add 30 vessels to the existing 632 sanctioned ships in the so called “shadow fleet” of Russia — vessels transporting Russian oil and petroleum products and circumventing Western sanctions. The package also expands sanctions to cover 31 more Russian banks, 20 banks, crypto-related entities and oil traders in third countries. It also introduces an export ban on equipment used to support drones and metals and alloys used in defence and aerospace.
Published
- June 09, 2026 10:10 pm IST
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **European Union (EU)** proposed its **21st sanctions package against Russia** on **June 9, 2026**, targeting **energy, crypto, financial services, trade (including fisheries), and drone manufacturing**.
  - **Key focus areas**: Expansion of **"shadow fleet" sanctions** (vessels transporting Russian oil), **export controls on drone-related equipment**, and **bans on Russian combatants entering the EU**.

- **Key actors**:
  - **Ursula von der Leyen** (**President, European Commission**) announced the package.
  - **Kaja Kallas** (**EU Foreign Affairs Chief**) highlighted **30+ new designations**, including **Indian entities**, and **50 companies from China, Türkiye, Kyrgyzstan, Kazakhstan, UAE**.
  - **Indian firms**: **Three Indian entities** sanctioned for **links with Russia**, amid **EU-India Free Trade Agreement (FTA) negotiations** (announced **February 2026**).

- **Why it matters (UPSC/India angle)**:
  - **Geopolitical tensions**: India’s **strategic autonomy** tested as it balances **Russia ties** (defense, energy) with **Western economic partnerships**.
  - **Trade-offs**: Sanctions on Indian firms could **impact bilateral trade talks**, especially in **defense tech, pharmaceuticals, and energy sectors**.
  - **Shadow fleet expansion**: **662 sanctioned vessels** now target Russian oil exports, forcing India to navigate **G7 price cap compliance** while importing Russian crude.

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for secondary sanctions**: First time **Indian firms explicitly named** in EU’s Russia-related sanctions, signaling **broader enforcement of third-country compliance**.
  - **Drone tech controls**: Ban on **export of drone components** (e.g., metals/alloys) may **disrupt India’s defense imports** (e.g., Russian drones used in Ukraine war).
  - **Crypto crackdown**: Sanctions on **20 crypto entities** in third countries could **affect India’s digital rupee and crypto regulations** under **Finance Ministry’s 2026 draft laws**.
  - **FTA negotiations**: Sanctions may **delay or complicate** **EU-India trade deal**, particularly **pharma, IT, and agriculture chapters**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The European Union’s 21st sanctions package against Russia, announced on June 9, 2026, expands its "shadow fleet" to **662 vessels**, targeting **Russian oil exports** that circumvent Western sanctions, with **30 new vessels added** in this round.**
- **Three Indian firms were included in the EU’s sanctions list for the first time**, joining **45 other entities** from **China, Türkiye, UAE, and Central Asia**, highlighting **India’s exposure to secondary sanctions** amid its **Russia trade ties**.
- **The sanctions impose an export ban on **equipment used for drones and metals/alloys critical for defense and aerospace**, which could impact India’s imports of **Russian military hardware**, including **drones and missile components** used in conflicts like Ukraine.**
- **The EU also sanctioned **31 Russian banks, 20 foreign banks, and crypto-related entities**, escalating pressure on **Russia’s financial system** and potentially affecting **India’s crypto regulatory framework** under the **Finance Ministry’s proposed **Digital Rupee and Crypto Asset Laws (2026)**.
- **India’s **Free Trade Agreement (FTA) negotiations with the EU**, announced in **February 2026**, face **geopolitical hurdles** due to sanctions on Indian entities, particularly in **pharmaceuticals, IT services, and defense tech sectors**, which are key negotiation areas.**
- **The ban on **former Russian combatants entering the EU** reflects the bloc’s **hardening stance** on **military personnel linked to the Ukraine war**, which may indirectly influence **India’s stance on mercenaries and private military contractors** operating in conflict zones.**

---

### **Syllabus Tag**
### **Key Terms**

14 Kuki persons were formally released and the individuals were handed over to police in Senapati district, Manipur.
                                          | Photo Credit: Special Arrangement
Nearly a month after they were held hostage by Naga armed groups in Manipur’s Senapati district, 14 Kukis were released on Tuesday (June 9, 2026) following an intervention by church bodies and other civil society groups, officials said.
Manipur Director General of Police Mukesh Singh told
The Hindu
that 14 Kukis had been released and reached their homes at Taphou village in Senapati.
Following the killing of three church leaders belonging to the Thadou tribe who were ambushed by armed groups on May 13, at least 44 civilians were held hostage by Kuki and Naga groups in Kangpokpi and Senapati districts of Manipur. No arrests have been made for the murder of the the three church leaders yet.
While others were released, 14 Kukis remained in captivity till Tuesday and six Naga men are still missing.
Heeding sentiments
The United Naga Council (UNC), the apex body of the Nagas in Manipur, had earlier announced that the 14 men will be released on June 1 but it later said that considering the “prevailing sentiments of the Naga public”, the release is cancelled.
On Tuesday, UNC President Ng Lorho said that the “detainees” were released considering the commitment made by Union Home Minister Amit Shah through Nagaland Chief Minister Neiphiu Rio assuring that the government will put in all efforts to establish the status of the six missing Nagas. He thanked various church and tribal bodies and Meghalaya Chief Minister Conrad K. Sangma.
“Today, the UNC in consultation with all stakeholders facilitated the release of all 14 Kuki detainees and handed them over to the local administration,” Mr. Lorho said.
The Chief Ministers of Nagaland and Meghalaya had earlier appealed for the release of the abducted individuals.
Nagaland CM welcomes move
On Tuesday, Mr. Rio said that he welcomes the safe
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**
  - **14 Kuki civilians** were **released by Naga armed groups** in **Senapati district, Manipur**, on **June 9, 2026**, after **30 days in captivity**, following mediation by **church bodies** and **civil society**.
  - Release came after **Union Home Minister **Amit Shah**’s assurance** (via **Nagaland CM **Neiphiu Rio****) to investigate the **abduction of 6 Naga men** still missing.

- **Key actors**
  - **United Naga Council (UNC)**: Apex Naga body that facilitated the release.
  - **Meghalaya CM **Conrad K. Sangma** & **Nagaland CM **Neiphiu Rio****: Appealed for reciprocal release of Naga hostages.
  - **Baptist World Alliance**: Led humanitarian appeals for hostage release.
  - **Manipur Police**: Handed over released individuals to **Taphou village** via formal handover memorandum.

- **Why it matters**
  - **Escalation of ethnic conflicts** in Manipur: Latest phase involves **Naga-Kuki clashes** (since **Feb 7, 2026**), adding to **Meitei-Kuki violence** (since **May 3, 2023**), with **>260 deaths**.
  - **Humanitarian diplomacy**: Demonstrates role of **church groups** and **regional CMs** in conflict de-escalation, testing **federal governance** under **Article 355** (President’s duty to protect states from internal disturbances).
  - **BJP’s peace efforts**: **Wilubou Newmai** (BJP leader, ex-**2023 peace committee** member) highlighted release as a step toward **inter-community reconciliation**.

- **Significance**
  - **Precedent for reciprocal releases**: UNC’s decision to release hostages in exchange for **government assurances** (not direct quid pro quo) reflects **negotiation tactics** in insurgency-affected areas.
  - **Church’s moral authority**: Baptist World Alliance’s intervention underscores **civil society’s role** in **northeast peace processes**, akin to past cases like **Assam’s Bodoland Accord (1993)**.
  - **Security challenges**: **6 Naga men still missing** highlights **impunity of armed groups** and **police’s investigative limitations** in conflict zones.
  - **Federal coordination**: Involvement of **Meghalaya & Nagaland CMs** shows **inter-state dynamics** in Manipur’s crisis, testing **Article 257** (states’ duty to cooperate with Union).

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Manipur’s ethnic violence has killed over **260 people** since **May 3, 2023**, with the latest **Naga-Kuki conflict** (since **February 7, 2026**) adding a **new dimension** to the **Meitei-Kuki clashes**, reflecting **unresolved demands for Scheduled Tribe (ST) status** for **Kuki-Zo communities** and **Meitei inclusion in ST list**.
- The **United Naga Council (UNC)**, the **apex Naga body**, delayed the release of **14 Kuki hostages** on **June 1, 2026**, citing **"prevailing sentiments of the Naga public"**, before releasing them on **June 9** after **Union Home Minister **Amit Shah**’s assurance** to investigate the **abduction of 6 Naga civilians**.
- **Meghalaya Chief Minister **Conrad K. Sangma** and **Nagaland CM **Neiphiu Rio** appealed for the release**, framing it as a **humanitarian gesture** to restore **trust and dialogue**, mirroring past **northeast peace initiatives** like the **Bodoland Accord (1993)**.
- The **Baptist World Alliance** led **church-mediated negotiations**, demonstrating the **critical role of civil society** in **conflict resolution** in the **northeast**, where **tribal and religious identities** often intersect with **insurgency and governance challenges**.
- **Manipur Police** conducted a **formal handover** of the released Kukis to **Taphou village** via a **duly executed memorandum**, ensuring **legal documentation** of the release—a **procedural safeguard** in **hostage situations** under **Indian Penal Code (IPC) Section 364 (kidnapping)**.
- The **2023 peace committee**, which included **BJP leader **Wilubou Newmai**, collapsed without resolution, but

input:
A Tejas (LCA) aircraft takes off during the inauguration of Aero India 2025 at the IAF Yelahanka Air Force Station in Bengaluru on 10 February 2025. Photo: Murali Kumar K./The Hindu.
                                          | Photo Credit: MURALI KUMAR K.
The Hindustan Aeronautics Limited (HAL) has lodged a cheating and forgery complaint against a Hyderabad-based aerospace components supplier, alleging that the company submitted 199 forged test reports in connection with the supply of parts for the Light Combat Aircraft (LCA) Tejas Mk1A programme.
Based on a complaint filed by Ranu Gupta, Deputy General Manager, Aircraft Division, HAL, last Tuesday the HAL police have registered a case against M. Sivarama Prasad, CEO of M/s Tec Aero Devices, under Sections 420, 465 and 468 of the Indian Penal Code.
According to the complaint, HAL had issued 18 purchase orders to Tec Aero Devices between March 2022 and September 2023 for the supply of various components required for the Tejas Mk1A programme. As per procurement conditions, the supplier was required to furnish material test certificates, inspection reports, and other supporting documents before bulk supplies could be accepted.
HAL alleged that the company submitted 199 test reports covering 172 components. During quality control checks, HAL sought original reports relating to tensile strength, hardness, break load, shear, non-destructive testing (NDT), microstructure, and salt spray tests. However, the supplier allegedly failed to produce the original documents.
Subsequently, Tec Aero Devices submitted a letter dated November 22, 2023, attributing two test reports to Hyderabad-based Axis Inspection Solutions. To verify the authenticity of the documents, HAL conducted an audit at Axis Inspection Solutions on November 29, 2023.
During the audit, Axis Inspection Solutions reportedly informed HAL that none of the 199 reports had been issued by the company. In a written communication, Axis further alleged that its name and

Ghatge Convention Centre, is themed “Innovation and Entrepreneurship Driven Growth for Viksit Bharat 2047” and aims to bring together researchers, industry representatives, start-ups, academics, policymakers, investors, and students to strengthen India’s innovation ecosystem.

A major highlight of the conclave will be the participation of 12 start-ups incubated at the CSIR-CFTRI incubation centre in Mysuru. These ventures will exhibit products developed using CFTRI technologies, including protein bars, low-glycemic-index foods for diabetics, and millet-based nutritional products. instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
A special court has ordered the restitution of 282 immovable properties, valued at approximately ₹9,420.57 crore, which were attached by the Enforcement Directorate (ED) in the PACL Limited case, to the Justice Lodha Committee for the purpose of returning funds to the investors who were cheated.
The agency has so far attached assets worth close to ₹28,626 crore in India and Australia. These assets are held in the name of PACL Limited, its group/associate entities, and family members/associates of the late Nirmal Singh Bhangoo, including Barinder Kaur (daughter), Harsatinder Pal Singh Hayer (son-in-law), Sukhwinder Kaur (daughter), Gurpartap Singh (son-in-law), and Prem Kaur (wife).
The ED probe is based on a case registered by the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI) against the company and its promoters for allegedly defrauding lakhs of investors. The accused had mobilised over ₹68,000 crore from investors across India, under the pretext of investment schemes, of which ₹48,000 crore remained unpaid to them.
In February, the Supreme Court ordered the Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI) to constitute a committee under the chairmanship of Justice (retired) R.M. Lodha, former Chief Justice of India. The court directed that the assets of PACL be disposed of and the sale proceeds be used to refund investors.
The ED has arrested Mr. Hayer and secured non-bailable warrants against Ms. Barinder and Ms. Prem Kaur. Proceedings under the Fugitive Economic Offenders Act have been initiated against Ms. Sukhwinder Kaur and Mr. Gurpartap Singh, said the agency.
Published
- June 09, 2026 10:40 pm IST
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - A **special court** ordered the **restitution of ₹9,420.57 crore** worth of **282 immovable properties** attached by the **Enforcement Directorate (ED)** in the **PACL Limited case** to the **Justice Lodha Committee** for investor refunds.
  - The **Supreme Court (Feb 2026)** directed **SEBI** to form a **Justice Lodha Committee** to manage asset disposal and refunds to **68,000+ cheated investors** (₹48,000 crore unpaid).

- **Key actors**:
  - **PACL Limited** (promoters: **Nirmal Singh Bhangoo** [deceased], family members: **Barinder Kaur, Harsatinder Pal Singh Hayer, Sukhwinder Kaur, Gurpartap Singh, Prem Kaur**).
  - **Institutions**: **ED, CBI, SEBI, Supreme Court, Justice Lodha Committee**.
  - **Legal tools**: **Fugitive Economic Offenders Act (FEOA), PMLA (Prevention of Money Laundering Act)**.

- **Why it matters**:
  - **Economic fraud case** involving **₹68,000 crore** scam, one of India’s largest Ponzi schemes, exposing **regulatory gaps** in **investor protection** and **asset recovery mechanisms**.
  - **Judicial activism**: **Supreme Court’s intervention** in asset restitution highlights **conflict between law enforcement (ED) and investor welfare (SEBI-Lodha Committee)**.
  - **FEOA’s role**: First case where **proceedings initiated** under **FEOA 2018**, testing its efficacy in **trapping fugitive economic offenders**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for asset recovery**: Court’s order sets a **template for restitution of attached assets** to **independent committees** (vs. direct confiscation by ED).
  - **Regulatory oversight**: **SEBI’s expanded role** in financial frauds post-**Supreme Court directives**, blurring lines between **enforcement (ED) and market regulation (SEBI)**.
  - **Global asset tracing**: **₹28,626 crore** attached in **India & Australia**, showcasing **cross-border collaboration** in economic crimes.
  - **Investor confidence**: **₹48,000 crore** unpaid claims underscore **systemic risks** in **alternative investment schemes** (AIS) and **lack of redressal mechanisms**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- The **Enforcement Directorate (ED)** has attached assets worth **₹28,626 crore** in **India and Australia** in the **PACL Limited case**, involving **₹68,000 crore** mobilized from **lakh+ investors** under **false investment schemes**, with **₹48,000 crore remaining unpaid**.
- A **special court** ordered the **restitution of ₹9,420.57 crore** worth of **282 properties** to the **Justice Lodha Committee**, marking a **first-of-its-kind judicial directive** for **structured asset recovery** in economic frauds.
- The **Supreme Court (February 2026)** constituted a **Justice Lodha Committee** under **SEBI** to **manage sale proceeds** of PACL’s assets and **refund investors**, expanding **SEBI’s role** beyond market regulation to **investor redressal**.
- **Proceedings under the Fugitive Economic Offenders Act (FEOA 2018)** were initiated against **Sukhwinder Kaur and Gurpartap Singh**, testing the **Act’s provisions** for **confiscating assets** of **absconding economic offenders** without conviction.
- The **PACL scam** involved **family members of Nirmal Singh Bhangoo** (deceased promoter), including **Barinder Kaur (daughter), Harsatinder Hayer (son-in-law), and Prem Kaur (wife)**, with **non-bailable warrants** issued against **Barinder and Hayer** by the **ED**.
- The **Justice Lodha Committee** (chaired by **retired CJI R.M. Lodha**) was formed following the **Supreme Court’s order**, highlighting **judicial intervention** in **high-value economic frauds** to **bridge gaps** between **law enforcement and investor protection**.

---

### **Syllabus Tag**
### Syllabus Tag: GS Paper 2 — Governance: Financial Regulation & Economic Offences
### Key Terms: 
### Category: Polity

Prime Minister Narendra Modi. File
                                          | Photo Credit: PTI
Prime Minister Narendra Modi has received congratulatory messages on becoming the longest-serving elected Prime Minister of India, from the leaders of Sri Lanka, Papua New Guinea, and Trinidad and Tobago, who hailed his “transformative governance, his advocacy for the Global South, and his vision of an inclusive and economically dynamic India”, according to an official statement issued by the Prime Minister’s Office.
Sri Lankan President Anura Kumara Dissanayake, in a letter, conveyed the congratulations of the government and the people of Sri Lanka to Prime Minister Modi. “This milestone is a testament not only to your years in office but also to the trust and confidence that the people of the world’s largest democracy have repeatedly placed in your leadership,” Mr. Dissanayake said, as per the PMO statement.
The government statement also said that James Marape, the Prime Minister of Papua New Guinea, conveyed in a “personal video message” that Mr. Modi was a “role model and an example of leadership”. “Lifting over 200 million people out of poverty to good life today is an amazing feat,” he said, according to the PMO.
Prime Minister of Trinidad and Tobago Kamla Persad-Bissessar also congratulated Mr. Modi on the occasion, saying that under his leadership, India had evolved as a “leading voice on global matters”.
Mr. Dissanayake also highlighted India’s remarkable economic and social transformation and said that Mr. Modi’s vision had “inspired many beyond India’s borders, including Sri Lanka”, as per the PMO statement.
The official statement said PM Modi visited Sri Lanka from April 4 to 6, 2025, his fourth visit to the island nation, during which he was conferred the ‘Mitra Vibhushana’, the island country’s highest civilian honour accorded to a foreign dignitary. The statement also mentioned Mr. Modi’s visit to Papua New Guinea in May 2023, the first by an Indian PM, an

  - **2024 Election Context**: **Legitimacy boost** amid **opposition critiques on democracy erosion**.

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Tamil Nadu Assembly Speaker J.C.D. Prabhakar has decided not to pursue the matter of disqualification against 21 MLAs  of the AIADMK.   
                                          | Photo Credit: B. JOTHI RAMALINGAM
Tamil Nadu Assembly Speaker J.C.D. Prabhakar’s decision not to pursue the matter of disqualification against 21 legislators of the AIADMK  appears to be a throwback to the treatment that 11 MLAs of the party experienced about eight years ago.
As in the present case, the legislators who faced the prospect of disqualification in 2017 were spared the proceedings in view of the then Speaker, P. Dhanapal, not going after them. However, there were two differences. Back then, no such announcement was made. Furthermore, the matter went to the Madras High Court that dismissed a plea for action against the legislators.
Motion of confidence
On February 18, 2017, a motion of confidence was moved by the then Chief Minister, Edappadi K. Palaniswami, in the Assembly. Eleven MLAs  of his party, including former Chief Minister O. Panneerselvam and former Ministers S. Semmalai and K. Pandiarajan, voted against the motion. Though a whip was said to have been issued for the trust motion and petitions were given to Mr. Dhanapal about a month later, there was no follow-up on his part. In the meantime, the title and symbol of the AIADMK were frozen by the Election Commission. Five months later, the camps of Mr. Palaniswami and Mr. Panneerselvam  came together, burying their differences. Subsequently, the unified formation was recognised as the AIADMK and assigned the symbol of ‘Two Leaves’.
On August 22, 2017, legislators owing allegiance to the rebel leader of the AIADMK, T.T.V. Dhinakaran, submitted letters to Governor Ch.  Vidyasagar Rao, “withdrawing their support” to Mr. Palaniswami. On September 18, the Speaker disqualified 18 legislators on the ground that, through their action, they had “voluntarily” given up the membership of the party to which they belonged.
A week la
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
#### **1. Speaker’s Decision to Drop Disqualification Proceedings Against AIADMK MLAs (June 2024)**
- **Event**: **Tamil Nadu Assembly Speaker J.C.D. Prabhakar** decided **not to pursue disqualification proceedings** against **21 AIADMK MLAs**, mirroring a **2017 precedent** where **11 MLAs** were spared similar action.
- **Key Actors**:
  - **Speaker J.C.D. Prabhakar** (current)
  - **Former Speaker P. Dhanapal** (2017)
  - **AIADMK MLAs** (including **O. Panneerselvam**, **S. Semmalai**, **K. Pandiarajan**)
  - **DMK** (filed petitions in **Madras High Court** and **Supreme Court**)
  - **Governor Ch. Vidyasagar Rao** (received resignation letters in 2017)
- **Why It Matters**:
  - Tests **Speaker’s discretionary powers** under **Article 100 (3) of the Constitution** (disqualification of MLAs for defection).
  - Highlights **judicial restraint** in **political defection cases**, with courts avoiding **judicial overreach** (as per **Supreme Court** rulings in **Kihoto Hollohan vs. Zachillhu** and **S.R. Bommai case**).
  - Reflects **AIADMK’s internal power struggles** and **party consolidation** post-2017.
- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for Speaker’s inaction**: No formal announcement in 2017, but **Madras High Court dismissed DMK’s plea** in **2018**, citing **Supreme Court’s pending adjudication** on judicial intervention in Speaker’s powers.
  - **Political reconciliation**: The **2017 split** between **Palaniswami** and **Panneerselvam** was resolved, leading to **AIADMK’s reunification** and **EC’s recognition** of the **‘Two Leaves’ symbol**.
  - **Constitutional tension**: Courts have **refused to direct Speakers** to disqualify MLAs, upholding **separation of powers** but leaving **Speakers’ discretion unchecked**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The **Tamil Nadu Assembly Speaker** holds **discretionary powers** under **Article 100(3) of the Constitution** to disqualify MLAs for **voluntarily giving up party membership**, but **no judicial interference** is allowed unless **malafide intent** is proven (as per **Kihoto Hollohan case**).
- In **2017**, **11 AIADMK MLAs** (including **O. Panneerselvam**) **voted against a confidence motion** moved by **Edappadi K. Palaniswami**, but **Speaker P. Dhanapal took no action**, leading to a **Madras High Court ruling** in **April 2018** that **courts cannot direct Speakers** to disqualify MLAs, as it would amount to **judicial overreach**.
- The **Supreme Court** later **dismissed DMK’s appeal**, reinforcing that **Speakers’ decisions on defection are quasi-judicial** but **not reviewable by courts** unless **procedural violations** are evident (**S.R. Bommai case**).
- **AIADMK’s internal split** in **2017** led to **18 MLAs** (led by **T.T.V. Dhinakaran**) **resigning support** for **Palaniswami**, but **Speaker disqualified only these 18**, while **11 rebels (including Panneerselvam) were spared**, showing **selective enforcement** of defection rules.
- The **Election Commission of India (ECI)** **froze AIADMK’s symbol** in **2017** due to the split but later **recognized the unified party** with the **‘Two Leaves’ symbol** after reconciliation, illustrating **ECI’s role in party consolidation**.
- **Judicial discipline** was emphasized by the **Madras High Court** in **2018**, which **refused to disqualify MLAs directly**, stating that **Speakers must act independently**, and **courts cannot usurp legislative powers** (**Indira Banerjee Bench ruling**).

---

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
New Delhi, May 07 (ANI): Additional Secretary, Department of Commerce Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Darpan Jain speaks during FEBI Members Meet Decoding Eu-India FTA, in New Delhi on Thursday. (ANI Photo/Naveen Sharma)
                                          | Photo Credit: ANI
Darpan Jain, Additional Secretary in the Department of Commerce, Ministry of Commerce and Industry, and chief negotiator for free trade agreements (FTAs) with the US and Europe, held closed-door meetings and interactions with large domestic tech firms, Global Capability Centers (GCCs) and Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) in Bengaluru on Tuesday.
In all, India has concluded nine FTAs with 38 countries in the past five years, and the objective of these special sessions was to explain and educate tech service firms, GCC players and small and medium enterprises on the provisions of these FTAs, so that the business and trade community can understand the full export and import scope of these new bilateral treaties in simple language.
“The real benefit of the agreement will be delivered only if our business entrepreneurs and exporters understand how these support export and import opportunities and how these trade pacts help boost the country’s global trade and exports,” said Mr. Jain, an IAS officer from the Karnataka cadre who briefly served as the Deputy Commissioner of Mysuru in April 2018.
According to him, services are the most important pillar in all these FTAs, and they provide an opportunity to enhance India’s exports to these FTA countries. He was in Bengaluru because the city is a hub of IT, IT-enabled services, business services and global capability centres, and it was important to inform them about the benefits of FTAs and how they can use them.
This was the first round of such interactions, which he and his colleague Anupama started. With this, the Department of Commerce was setting up a mechanism through which it could continue its dialogue with industry.
The Depar
output:
### GK Gist:

- **Event**:

  - Part of **first-of-its-kind engagement** by **MoCI** to bridge knowledge gaps among exporters on **FTA benefits**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Darpan Jain** (IAS, **Karnataka cadre**, ex-**Deputy Commissioner, Mysuru**) – **Chief FTA negotiator** for **EU-US FTAs**.
  - **Anupama** (MoCI colleague) – Co-leading **industry outreach mechanism**.
  - **Tech/IT hubs in Bengaluru** (e.g., **GCCs, MSMEs**) – Primary beneficiaries of **services-led FTA opportunities**.

- **Why It Matters**:

  - **Services sector** (IT, ITES, business services) is the **cornerstone of India’s FTA push**, aligning with **PLI schemes** and **Make in India 2.0**.
  - **Bengaluru’s role** as a **services export hub** (contributes **~30% of India’s IT exports**) makes it critical for **FTA awareness campaigns**.

- **Significance**:
  - **$3 trillion** in **tariff-free market access** opened via FTAs, targeting **zero-duty sectors** like **textiles ($359B market)**, **gems & jewelry ($442B)**, and **engineering goods**.
  - **Trade Connect Platform** (MoCI’s **one-stop exporter portal**) and **intelligence dashboard** aim to **democratize trade data** at **8-digit HS code level** for precision targeting.
  - **Precedent for proactive MoCI-industry dialogue**, mirroring **DPIIT’s PLI scheme engagement** but focused on **FTAs**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **India’s FTA expansion** has **concluded 9 agreements with 38 countries in the last 5 years**, including **Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreements (CEPAs)** with **UAE, Australia, and Mauritius**, and **FTAs with UK and Canada**, reflecting **Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s push for "Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam" (global trade integration)**.

- **Bengaluru’s Global Capability Centers (GCCs)** employ **over 1.5 million professionals** and contribute **~$100B+ annually** to India’s exports, making them **key stakeholders in FTA-led growth**, especially under **Digital India and Startup India initiatives**.

- **Trade Connect Platform** (launched by **MoCI**) provides **real-time data on 8-digit HS codes**, **competitive benchmarks**, and **FTA-specific tariff reductions**, mirroring **DPIIT’s Export Import (EXIM) Policy 2023** but with a **digital-first approach**.
- **Darpan Jain’s role** as **FTA negotiator** highlights **MoCI’s institutional capacity-building**, following **India’s WTO accession (1995)** and **post-Brexit trade diplomacy**, where **services and IPR negotiations** are critical for **balancing market access with domestic industry protection**.

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla addresses the concluding session of the Commonwealth Parliamentary Association (CPA) India Region Zone-II (North Zone) Conference in Chandigarh, on June 9, 2026. Picture: X/@ombirlakota
Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla said on Tuesday (June 9, 2026) that frequent interruptions and disruptions in the Houses of Parliament and the State Assemblies pose a serious challenge to democratic institutions. He underlined that the solution to this problem lies within the legislative institutions themselves, urging them to promote conduct aligned with public expectations, dialogue, and meaningful discussion.
He was speaking at the second conference of the Commonwealth Parliamentary Association (CPA) India Region Zone-II (North Zone), which concluded in Chandigarh.
He called on the public representatives to ensure that their conduct in legislative institutions is exemplary, so that the welfare of the last person in society can be secured. “Society is shaped by the conduct and behaviour of its leadership. The public has elected and sent us here, hence our conduct has a direct impact on society,” he said.
‘Public cooperation essential’
Mr. Birla said the conference concluded with four important resolutions aimed at making legislative institutions more effective, accountable, and people-centric. They form a strong foundation for the future, through which the legislative and democratic institutions of the country will progress with public cooperation, he said.
“The dream of a developed India, ‘Viksit Bharat’, is anchored on strong parliamentary and legislative institutions. To achieve this, it is absolutely essential to increase public participation, maximise the use of technology, enhance the capacity of legislators, ensure the involvement of citizens in framing policies and laws, and raise awareness toward the Constitution and constitutional institutions,” he added.
‘Centre, States must work together’
In India’s federal structure, the States and the Centre can
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla** addressed the **concluding session of the Commonwealth Parliamentary Association (CPA) India Region Zone-II (North Zone) Conference** in **Chandigarh** on **June 9, 2026**.
  - The **two-day conference** focused on strengthening **legislative institutions**, **public participation**, and **accountability** in India’s democratic framework.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Om Birla** (Lok Sabha Speaker) – Emphasized **conduct of legislators**, **public cooperation**, and **federal coordination** between **Centre and States**.
  - **Presiding Officers from 12 States** (Haryana, Punjab, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu & Kashmir, Delhi, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Goa, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan, Sikkim, West Bengal) – Participated in discussions on **legislative reforms**.

- **Why It Matters (UPSC/India Angle)**:
  - **Parliamentary disruptions** (frequent interruptions in **Houses of Parliament/State Assemblies**) threaten **democratic stability** and **institutional credibility**.
  - **CPA’s role** in promoting **parliamentary best practices** aligns with **India’s vision of "Viksit Bharat"** (developed India) through **strong legislative institutions**.
  - **Federalism in action**: Highlighted **Centre-State coordination** as critical for **policy implementation** (e.g., **schemes, laws**) under **Cooperative Federalism**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Four key resolutions** adopted to enhance **legislative effectiveness**, **citizen engagement**, and **Constitutional awareness**.
  - **Technology adoption** and **capacity-building** for legislators proposed to improve **transparency and accountability**.
  - **Youth, women, and marginalized sections** identified as **priority stakeholders** for **21st-century India’s progress**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The Commonwealth Parliamentary Association (CPA) India Region Zone-II (North Zone) conference**, held in **Chandigarh (June 9, 2026)**, saw **Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla** stress that **frequent disruptions in Parliament and State Assemblies** pose a **"serious challenge to democratic institutions"**, requiring **self-reform within legislative bodies** to align conduct with **public expectations**.
- **Four resolutions** were passed to make **legislative institutions more effective, accountable, and people-centric**, with a focus on **public participation, technology integration, and citizen policy involvement**, directly linking to **Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s "Viksit Bharat" vision** for **2047**.
- **Om Birla highlighted that "society is shaped by the conduct of its leadership"**, citing **exemplary behavior of legislators** as essential to ensure **welfare of the last person in society**, a principle rooted in **Article 40 (DPSP) of the Constitution** (organization of village panchayats and promotion of cottage industries).
- **The conference emphasized the need for **Centre-State collaboration** under **India’s federal structure**, as **schemes, laws, and policies** (e.g., **PM-KISAN, Ayushman Bharat**) can only succeed through **cooperative federalism**, a concept reinforced by the **73rd/74th Constitutional Amendments**.
- **Presiding Officers from 12 States**, including **Haryana, Punjab, and Jammu & Kashmir**, discussed **legislative reforms**, reflecting **regional diversity** in India’s **parliamentary governance**, while **Madhya Pradesh and Maharashtra** (non-CPA Zone-II members) participated, showcasing **inter-state knowledge exchange**.
- **The 21st century was declared "India’s century"** by **Om Birla**, with a call to prioritize **youth aspirations, women empowerment, and inclusive growth**, aligning with **NITI Aayog’s 15-year vision document (2047)** and **SDG targets** (e.g., **Gender Equality, Quality Education**).

---

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
A dramatic day of political developments in Madhya Pradesh’s capital Bhopal, that began with several Congress MLAs heading to the airport to leave for Bengaluru, ended with the nomination of Meenakshi Natarajan, the party candidate for one of three Rajya Sabha seats from the State, being rejected during scrutiny.
This prompted top Congress leaders, led by its national general secretary K.C. Venugopal, to stage a sit-in outside the Election Commission of India (ECI)’s office in New Delhi.
The rejection came at a time when a batch of Congress MLAs was en route to Bengaluru amid fears of cross- voting in the polls, prompting the State leaders to ask them to return to Bhopal.
Ms. Natarajan accused the BJP of having “moved from vote theft to seat theft”.
After Meenakshi Natarajan’s nomination rejection, Congress delegation turns away by EC
“It started when the BJP fielded a third candidate despite not having the adequate number of members, and we started to understand the kind of politics it is practising with democracy and the Constitution. What was limited to vote theft through SIR (Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls) and other methods has today reached (the stage of) seat theft,” she said, at a press conference late on Tuesday with senior State party leaders.
Ms. Natarajan’s nomination was rejected by Assembly Secretary Arvind Sharma, who is also the Returning Officer for the election, based on an objection raised by BJP candidate for the third seat Mahesh Kewat and State party general secretary Rahul Kothari. They alleged that Ms. Natarajan had “hidden” details of a case lodged against her in Telangana’s Hyderabad.
The objection led to chaotic scenes at the Assembly Secretariat in Bhopal, with dozens of Congress and BJP workers coming face to face and engaging in arguments.
Several senior State BJP leaders and MLAs, including Cabinet Ministers Rakesh Singh and Kailash Vijayvargiya, as well as Mr. Kewat remained in the Assembly Secretariat during the procee
output:

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
At 31, Bijli Devi weighs just 44 kg. Weak and pregnant, she has come to Katlupur sub-centre in Sonipat district for a routine vaccination. The doctor has flagged her pregnancy as high risk. This is her seventh in 12 years. She has six daughters, 2 to 12. “I have little choice,” she says. Her husband, an agricultural labourer, and in-laws want a son. She doesn’t know what will happen if the seventh is a girl too. Her two oldest daughters have dropped out of school. They now work as farm labourers alongside their father. “We never went for sex-determination,” Bijli Devi adds. “We don’t have the means.” A sex-determination test, illegal in India, costs ₹50,000-60,000 on the black market.
In May this year, a secret sex-determination racket, running in the middle of Gurugram, was busted. At Aayu Hospital in Hari Nagar, a mere 2 kilometres north of Sector 37 police station, along the frenetic arterial stretch of Khandsa Road off the Delhi-Jaipur Highway, sleuths dismantled a syndicate peddling prenatal gender testing. Arrested consultant radiologist Manjit Kumar would allegedly conduct the sex-determination test for ₹40,000.
Here, in the pulsating heart of the Millennium City, directly opposite the cacophonous Sector 10A market, thrived the illicit sex-determination centre. This, more than a decade after the clarion call of “Beti Bachao Beti Padhao” (BBBP) echoed from Panipat, more than a thousand surgical raids executed, and upwards of 4,000 miscreants incarcerated.
The bust wasn’t just about catching criminals. It exposed a deeper failure: the slow collapse of monitoring and the fading fear of law among offenders. For the enforcement teams, the brazenness of it all was a jolt.
The rupture surfaced against an ominous backdrop: Haryana’s Sex Ratio at Birth (SRB) has plummeted to 898 girls per 1,000 boys in the first four months of 2026. Only four of the 23 districts — Nuh, Rewari, Sonipat, and Panchkula — breached the 900 mark. At the bottom of the table, Charkhi Dadri re
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
#### **1. Resurgence of Female Foeticide in Haryana Despite Beti Bachao Beti Padhao (BBBP)**
- **Event**:
  - **Recent busts** of illegal sex-determination rackets in **Gurugram (May 2026)** and **Faridabad (Leelawati Hospital)** exposed a **persistent underground network** despite **11+ years of BBBP**.
  - **Aayu Hospital (Gurugram)** and **Leelawati Hospital (Faridabad)** operated openly, with tests priced at **₹40,000–₹1.5 lakh** (up from ₹5,000–₹15,000 pre-crackdown).
  - **Haryana’s Sex Ratio at Birth (SRB) dropped to 898 (Jan–Apr 2026)**, the lowest in a decade, with **Charkhi Dadri at 768**—worse than **Bihar (895)** and **Rajasthan (900)**.
- **Key Actors**:
  - **Bijli Devi (Sonipat, 31 years)**: 7th pregnancy, **high-risk**, 6 daughters, **son preference** drives family decisions.
  - **Manjit Kumar (radiologist, Gurugram)**: Arrested for running a **₹40,000 sex-determination racket**.
  - **Om Prakash (Faridabad)**: Non-doctor using **handheld doppler ultrasound** for illegal tests.
  - **Haryana Health Dept.**: Claims **SRB rebounded to 923 in 2025** but faces **skepticism over data manipulation**.
- **Why It Matters**:
  - **Violation of PC-PNDT Act (1994)**: Despite **4,000+ arrests** and **1,000+ raids** since BBBP (2015), **enforcement has weakened**.
  - **Gender Equality & SDG 5**: Haryana’s **SRB crash** undermines **India’s progress toward gender parity** (SDG 5.1: **900+ SRB target**).
  - **Economic & Social Cost**: **Child labor** (Bijli Devi’s daughters dropped out), **health risks** (high-risk pregnancies), and **intergenerational poverty**.
- **Significance**:
  - **BBBP’s Failure**: **Monitoring lapses** (no monthly video conferences since 2021), **political will decline** (post-**Manohar Lal Khattar** leadership change).
  - **Cross-Border Networks**: Rackets operate in **UP, Punjab, Rajasthan, Delhi, Uttarakhand**—requiring **inter-state coordination**.
  - **Technological Adaptation**: Use of **portable ultrasound machines** and **MTP kits** (over-the-counter abortions) evades detection.
  - **Son Preference Persists**: **No family planning** after first daughter; **celebrations for sons** (e.g., **17-day festivities in Nahari village**).

---

#### **2. Beti Bachao Beti Padhao (BBBP): From Success to Complacency**
- **Event**:
  - **BBBP launched (Jan 22, 2015)** by **PM Narendra Modi in Panipat**, Haryana—**flagship scheme** to combat female foeticide.
  - **Peak SRB (2019 & 2025): 923** (improvement from **877 in 2014**).
  - **Post-2021 Decline**: **Raids dropped from 271 (2016) to 28 (2026, Jan–May)**; **FIRs shifted from PC-PNDT to MTP Act** (37 vs. 117 in 2025).
- **Key Actors**:
  - **Rakesh Gupta (Nodal Officer, 2015–2021)**: Oversaw **monthly video conferences** with **DCs, CMOs, FDA officials**.
  - **Manohar Lal Khattar (CM, 2014–2024)**: **Personal monitoring** of BBBP; replaced by **Nayab Singh Saini**.
  - **Centre for Social Research (CSR)**: Found **zero monitoring during COVID**, **sex-selection rampant**.
- **Why It Matters**:
  - **Policy Fatigue**: BBBP **sidelined post-2021** in favor

nder the scorching midday sun, women in R. Pudhupattinam, a coastal village about 60 kilometres from Pudukottai in Tamil Nadu, continue a daily ritual that has sustained generations. Barefoot and carrying as many containers as they can, the women walk two or three kilometres along the shoreline to collect drinking water from shallow pits dug on the beach.
The fishing village, known for its close relationship with the sea, depends on freshwater from beachside pits. Residents believe the naturally filtered water is healthier than commercially supplied alternatives. Many are of the view that packaged and canned water, which they say contains chlorine, often causes cold and other health issues.
“Our ancestors drew freshwater from this beachside pit since the British colonial period,” said R. Danabakkiyam, a resident of the village. The water is traditionally used for cooking meals for fishermen before they venture into sea, with locals believing it enhances the taste and nutritional value of food.
The site is regarded as sacred by the community. Villagers strictly prohibit sand removal and avoid planting vegetation in the area. Residents say the pits to collect the water are an integral part of life here.
Besides Pudhupattinam residents, people from neighbouring villages also travel to the site to collect water, with activity beginning as early as 4 a.m. and continuing until 10 p.m. Some even visit at midnight to meet their household water needs.
However, concerns about the future of the water source are growing.
Pushpam, a resident of the nearby coastal village of Gopalapattinam near Mimisal, said the quality of water has deteriorated over the past year due to littering and inadequate maintenance, and added that the government should protect the site. Villagers have to carry containers of water across a bridge. They said the bridge becomes unusable and unsafe during rains, and appealed to authorities for regular maintenance.

output:

- **Event**:
  - **Beachside freshwater pits** in **R. Pudhupattinam**, a **coastal fishing village** (~60 km from **Pudukottai, Tamil Nadu**), serve as a **traditional drinking water source** for generations.
  - Women walk **2–3 km daily** to collect water from **hand-dug pits** along the shoreline, a practice dating back to the **British colonial period**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Residents of R. Pudhupattinam** and neighboring villages (e.g., **Gopalapattinam** near **Mimisal**).

  - **Fishermen** use the water for cooking pre-voyage meals, believing it enhances **nutritional value and taste**.

- **Why It Matters (UPSC/India Angle)**:
  - Highlights **indigenous water management practices** in **coastal communities**, contrasting with **urban water supply systems**.

- **Significance**:

  - **Health perceptions**: Locals reject **packaged/canned water** (citing **chlorine-related illnesses**), illustrating **trust in natural filtration**.
  - **Policy gap**: Lack of **government intervention** despite **community appeals**, exposing **implementation challenges** in rural water security.
  - **Case study for UPSC**: Exemplifies **traditional ecological knowledge (TEK)** vs. **modern water governance**, relevant for **disaster management, climate adaptation, and sustainable development**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Traditional water sources in India** account for **~15% of rural drinking water supply**, with **beach wells** and **rainwater harvesting pits** being critical in **coastal and arid regions**, yet they receive **<1% of government funding** for maintenance.
- **Tamil Nadu’s coastal districts** (e.g., **Pudukottai, Thanjavur**) face **groundwater depletion rates of 3–5% annually**, driven by **salinization** and **over-extraction for agriculture**, threatening **~2 million coastal households**.

- **SDG 6.1** (universal access to safe drinking water) shows **India’s progress at 93% coverage**, but **coastal and tribal areas lag at 78–82%**, with **Tamil Nadu’s coastal blocks** recording **12% water quality non-compliance** due to **contamination and infrastructure gaps**.
- **Fisheries and Aquaculture in India** contribute **₹1.5 lakh crore annually** (2023–24), but **~40% of coastal fishing communities** rely on **traditional water sources** for **domestic and pre-harvest needs**, highlighting **sectoral interdependencies**.

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
On May 12,
Tamil Nadu
Chief Minister
C. Joseph Vijay
ordered the
closure of around 717 liquor shops located near schools and places of worship
across the State. Within a month, these shops have disappeared from Tamil Nadu’s streets. But, in the towns and villages across the State, customers have merely migrated to the next nearest Tasmac outlet. Residents, meanwhile, complain that they have not yet seen an end to drunken nuisance at public spaces.
This is not the first time Tamil Nadu has acted to cut down on the number of Tasmac outlets. In 2016, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa promised prohibition in phases and closed around 500 shops. The following year, her successor Edappadi K. Palaniswami announced the closure of another 500 shops.
Long-running effort
In 2023, Minister for Electricity, Prohibition and Excise V. Senthil Balaji informed the Assembly that 500 more Tasmac retail outlets would be closed. The latest closure of 717 shops is therefore the latest chapter in a long-running effort to balance public sentiment with the State’s dependence on revenue from liquor sales.
“From past experience, every time a new party comes to power in Tamil Nadu, closing liquor shops becomes one of its standard promises. But shops are eventually opened in other locations. Moreover, shutting down outlets does not necessarily result in a loss of business, as tipplers simply migrate to nearby shops. What the
Tamilaga Vettri Kazhagam
(TVK) government has done too is merely a cosmetic fix to a much deeper problem,” says a source tracking the liquor industry.
In the days following the closure order,
The Hindu
visited several Tasmac outlets across Tamil Nadu to examine whether the shutters had truly come down and whether the decision had made any tangible difference on the ground. They had, in many areas. However, during this visit, members of the public have said there are more such shops which the government has to close.
For instance, at Velachery in Chennai, residents of Balakrishna N
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Tamil Nadu** **CM C. Vijay** ordered the closure of **717 TASMAC liquor shops** near **schools, places of worship, and public spaces** on **May 12, 2024**.
  - This is the **latest phase** in a **long-standing effort** (since **2016**) to regulate alcohol sales amid **public health and safety concerns**.

- **Key actors**:
  - **Tamil Nadu Government** (led by **DMK**), **TASMAC** (Tamil Nadu State Marketing Corporation), **Tamilaga Vettri Kazhagam (TVK)**, **local activists**, **residents**, and **TASMAC employees (3,474 displaced)**.
  - **Opposition (AIADMK)** criticized the move as **cosmetic**, highlighting **private bar proliferation**.

- **Why it matters**:
  - **Public health crisis**: Alcohol-related **accidents, harassment, and pollution** (plastic waste in farmlands/waterbodies) persist despite closures.
  - **Revenue vs. regulation**: **Liquor sales contribute ~10% to TN’s excise revenue** (₹12,000+ crore annually), creating **policy dilemmas**.
  - **Symbolic vs. systemic**: Closures often **relocate rather than eliminate** alcohol availability, with **private outlets filling gaps**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for alcohol regulation**: TN’s **phased prohibition attempts** (2016, 2017, 2023) reflect **political populism vs. economic reality**.
  - **Urban-rural divide**: **Chennai/Coimbatore** see **elite bars** replacing TASMAC, while **villages** report **informal drinking spots** (culverts, pump rooms).
  - **Law enforcement gaps**: **Traffic police** lack **nighttime sobriety checks**, and **spurious liquor** remains unchecked.
  - **Workforce impact**: **3,474 TASMAC employees** redeployed, raising **labor rights concerns** (low wages, poor infrastructure).

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Tamil Nadu’s liquor policy** has seen **three major closure drives**—**2016 (500 shops, Jayalalithaa)**, **2017 (500 shops, Palaniswami)**, and **2024 (717 shops, Vijay)**—yet **revenue dependence** (₹12,000+ crore annually) persists, making **prohibition politically unviable**.
- The **500-meter radius rule** for liquor shops near **schools, temples, and bus stops** was **erroneously implemented** in **Virudhunagar**, prompting a **re-survey**, exposing **administrative lapses** in TN’s **excise enforcement**.
- **Private liquor outlets (FL2 shops, bars)** in **Kancheepuram, Pudukottai, and Madurai** have **replaced TASMAC shops**, shifting **State revenue to unregulated private actors**, a **structural flaw** in TN’s **alcohol control mechanism**.
- **Alcohol-related accidents** in **Chennai** (e.g., **Station Service Road, Velachery**) and **drunken harassment** in **Salem (Kolathur)** highlight **gender and traffic safety failures**, despite **police awareness campaigns**.
- **TASMAC employees** (3,474 displaced) face **redeployment to overstaffed shops** (e.g., **Madurai’s Tiruppalai outlet**), raising **questions about labor rights** in **public sector enterprises** under **TN’s excise department**.
- **Plastic pollution** from **discarded liquor bottles/cups** in **farmlands and waterbodies** (reported in **Tiruvallur, Coimbatore**) links **alcohol regulation** to **environmental degradation**, a **GS3 (Ecology) + GS2 (Governance) intersection**.

input:
A view of the CBI headquarters in New Delhi. CBI conducted searches at six locations in Chandigarh, Panchkula and Delhi-NCR in connection with an alleged ₹661 crore fraud involving the siphoning of government funds from departments of the Haryana government and the Chandigarh administration.
                                          | Photo Credit: The Hindu
The CBI has conducted searches at six locations in Chandigarh, Panchkula and Delhi-NCR in connection with an alleged ₹661 crore fraud involving the siphoning of government funds from departments of the Haryana government and the Chandigarh administration, officials said on Sunday (June 7, 2026).
The searches were carried out on Friday (June 5, 2026) at premises linked to senior Haryana cadre public servants and Noida-based Vipam Consultancy Pvt Ltd and its director as part of an ongoing probe into the alleged misappropriation of funds parked with IDFC First Bank and AU Finance Bank, an official statement said.
According to the agency, the fraud affected eight departments of the Haryana government and two departments of the Union Territory of Chandigarh — Municipal Corporation Chandigarh and Chandigarh Renewable Energy and Science and Technology Promotion Society (CREST).
“During investigation evidences have surfaced suggesting that the public servants had colluded with bank officials and had facilitated in opening of accounts, transfer of funds and subsequent diversion thereof,” the statement said.
The agency alleged that the public servants received undue advantages for facilitating the transactions and failing to act against the irregularities.
The investigating agency also alleged that Vipam Consultancy Pvt Ltd received proceeds of crime in its bank account, which were later transferred to the personal account of its director.
“Incriminating documents, digital devices, property documents and other relevant material were seized during the search operations,” the agency said.
The probe stems from one case taken
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **CBI** conducted searches at **six locations** in **Chandigarh, Panchkula, and Delhi-NCR** on **June 5, 2026**, linked to a **₹661 crore fraud** involving **misappropriation of government funds** from **Haryana government departments** and **Chandigarh UT administration**.
  - The probe uncovered **collusion between public servants, bank officials, and private entities** to siphon funds from **IDFC First Bank** and **AU Small Finance Bank**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **CBI** (Central Bureau of Investigation) – Investigating agency.
  - **Haryana cadre public servants** – Alleged conspirators.
  - **Vipam Consultancy Pvt Ltd** (Noida-based) – Received proceeds of crime.
  - **IDFC First Bank** and **AU Small Finance Bank** – Banks involved in fund diversion.
  - **Haryana State Vigilance Bureau** and **Chandigarh Economic Offences Wing** – Cases taken over by CBI.

- **Why It Matters**:
  - Highlights **systemic corruption** in **public financial management**, affecting **eight Haryana departments** and **two Chandigarh departments** (Municipal Corporation and **CREST**).
  - Exposes **nexus between bureaucrats, bankers, and private entities**, undermining **public trust in governance**.
  - **CBI’s role** in probing **inter-state financial fraud** (Haryana + Chandigarh) tests **coordination between state and central agencies**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Modus operandi**: Funds were **parked in banks**, then **diverted via shell companies** (e.g., **Vipam Consultancy**), with **public servants receiving kickbacks**.
  - **Legal precedent**: CBI’s **first chargesheet** filed in **Panchkula special court**, setting a benchmark for **economic offences prosecution**.
  - **Broader implications**: Raises concerns over **bank supervision failures** and **weaknesses in financial audits** under **Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG)**.
  - **Political angle**: Could impact **Haryana’s governance reputation**, especially ahead of **state elections** (if applicable).

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **₹661 crore fraud** in **Haryana and Chandigarh** involved **eight state departments** and **two UT departments**, exposing **large-scale financial mismanagement** in **public sector fund utilization**.
- **CBI’s probe** uncovered **collusion between Haryana public servants, bank officials (IDFC First Bank & AU Small Finance Bank), and private consultancy firms (Vipam Consultancy Pvt Ltd)** to **divert government funds**, violating **Section 120B (criminal conspiracy) and Section 409 (misappropriation) of IPC**.
- **Chandigarh Renewable Energy and Science and Technology Promotion Society (CREST)** and **Municipal Corporation Chandigarh** were among the **affected entities**, highlighting **vulnerabilities in UT financial systems** despite **direct central oversight**.
- **CBI has already filed its first chargesheet** in a **special court in Panchkula**, detailing the **role of Haryana Power Generation Corporation Ltd (HPGCL) and Haryana School Education Board (Shiksha Pariyojna Parishad) officials**, marking a **rare instance of high-profile economic offences prosecution** under **PMLA (Prevention of Money Laundering Act)**.
- **IDFC First Bank** and **AU Small Finance Bank** are **scheduled commercial banks** regulated by **RBI**, raising questions about **bank due diligence failures** in **government account monitoring**, a **critical gap in financial sector oversight**.
- **The case originated from three sources**: **one case transferred from Haryana State Vigilance Bureau** and **two cases registered by Chandigarh’s Economic Offences Wing**, demonstrating **inter-agency coordination challenges** in **economic crime investigations**.

---

### Category: Polity

External Affairs Minister S Jaishankar with Nepal Foreign Minister Shisir Khanal during a meeting, in New Delhi on June 6, 2026.  Credit: X/@DrSJaishankar
India and Nepal have an opportunity to “decisively shift” the track of their bilateral ties to achieve its full potential, External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar said on Saturday (June 6, 2026), after official talks with his visiting Nepali counterpart Shisir Khanal.
Citing this week’s visit by Rabi Lamichhane, the chairperson of Nepal’s ruling Rastriya Swatantra Party, Mr. Jaishankar said there are “strong complementarities” between the government of Prime Minister Balendra Shah in Kathmandu and the government of India.
Saying that Mr. Shah wants to pursue “result-driven diplomacy”, Mr. Khanal described India as Nepal’s “most important partner”.
Strong complementarities
The conversation between the two Foreign Ministers came on the second day of Mr. Khanal’s visit, the first such visit by any member of the RSP government that came to power in March 2026.
“I want to take the opportunity to convey our best wishes for the success of your government,” Mr. Jaishankar said. “There are strong complementarities between the objectives outlined by your government and initiatives that India has taken in the neighbourhood.”
“I want today to very clearly and strongly send a message of collaboration and cooperation. We believe that today we have an opportunity to decisively shift the trajectory of India-Nepal relations and to realise the full potential of our ties,” he added.
‘No old baggage’
Describing India and Nepal as “stakeholders of a proud ancient civilisation”, Mr. Khanal said, “We seek to translate this civilisational bond into modern development oriented partnership. Nepal has placed the highest priority on its relationship with India. Under the new government, we are ready to engage substantively, purposefully and at the highest possible levels.”
“Nepal’s new government carries a clear and decisive mandate for good
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **India-Nepal Foreign Ministers’ Meeting** held in **New Delhi (June 6, 2026)**, marking the **first official visit by Nepal’s **Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP)** government** (led by **PM Balendra Shah**) to India.
  - **EAM **S. Jaishankar** and **Nepal FM **Shisir Khanal** discussed a **"result-driven diplomacy"** framework to **shift India-Nepal ties to their "full potential."**

- **Key Actors**:
  - **India**: **EAM **S. Jaishankar**, **NSA **Ajit Doval**, **India Foundation** (hosted pre-meeting discussions).
  - **Nepal**: **FM **Shisir Khanal**, **PM **Balendra Shah**, **RSP Chair **Rabi Lamichhane** (visited India earlier in 2026).
  - **Institutions**: **Ministry of External Affairs (India & Nepal)**, **India Foundation** (think tank for Indo-Nepal relations).

- **Why It Matters**:
  - **Strategic Neighborhood**: Nepal’s **"no old baggage"** stance signals a **clean-slate approach** to reset ties after **decades of trust deficit** (e.g., **2015 blockade**, **Kali River treaty disputes**, **hydropower conflicts**).
  - **Economic & Security Synergy**: Nepal’s **hydropower potential (6,000+ MW untapped)**, **connectivity gaps**, and **development needs** align with **India’s **Neighborhood First Policy** and **Act East Policy**.
  - **Geopolitical Context**: Comes amid **China’s growing influence in Nepal** (e.g., **transit agreements**, **infrastructure projects**) and **India’s push for **SAGAR (Security and Growth for All in the Region)** doctrine.

- **Significance**:
  - **Development Cooperation**: Discussions covered **hydropower (Pancheshwar, West Seti)**, **digital connectivity**, **health/education**, and **capacity building**—critical for **Nepal’s **$100B infrastructure needs** by 2030.
  - **Diplomatic Reset**: **FM Khanal’s statement** ("**no old baggage**") reflects Nepal’s **pragmatic shift** post-**2024 elections**, where **RSP won with a **pro-India, pro-development** mandate**.
  - **Precedent for High-Level Engagement**: Follows **RSP Chair **Rabi Lamichhane’s visit** (May 2026), signaling **institutionalized dialogue** under the new government.
  - **Energy Security**: **Hydropower** (e.g., **Pancheshwar Dam**) is a **$3B+ opportunity** for India, addressing **Nepal’s energy deficit** and **India’s **24x7 Power for All** goal.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Nepal’s **Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP)**, led by **PM Balendra Shah**, won the **2024 elections** with a **pro-India, pro-development** platform, marking a **shift from **CPN-UML’s** historically **pro-China leanings** and **Nepali Congress’s** **ambiguous stance**.
- **India-Nepal bilateral trade** stands at **~$10B annually**, but **Nepal’s trade deficit with India** exceeds **$8B**, driven by **Indian imports** (oil, machinery) and **limited Nepal exports** (hydropower, tourism).
- **Hydropower potential**: Nepal has **untapped **6,000+ MW** capacity**, with **Pancheshwar (6,480 MW)** and **West Seti (756 MW)** projects **stuck due to disputes** over **dam ownership, revenue-sharing, and environmental norms**.
- **India’s **Neighborhood First Policy** allocates **$500M+ annually** for Nepal, focusing on **infrastructure, healthcare (e.g., **Ayushman Bharat** tie-ups), and education** (e.g., **10,000+ Nepali students in Indian universities**).
- **Geopolitical balancing act**: Nepal’s **2022 Constitution** allows **third-country transit** (e.g., **China’s **Tibet-Nepal-India** route**), but **India remains Nepal’s **top trade partner (70% of imports/exports)** and **security guarantor**.
