instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Subhash C. Kashyap. File.
                                          | Photo Credit: The Hindu
Former Lok Sabha Secretary General and constitutional expert Subhash C. Kashyap passed away on Thursday (June 4, 2026) after a prolonged illness. He was 97.
Mr. Kashyap's end came at around 10 a.m. at his Sainik Farm residence, officials of the Lok Sabha Secretariat said.
He was suffering from age-related issues and died of cardio-pulmonary arrest, they said.
Mr. Kashyap was part of the high-level committee headed by former president Ram Nath Kovind to prepare a legal framework on simultaneous elections.
Author of over 100 books, he served as the secretary general of Lok Sabha from 1983 to 1990. He served Parliament for over 37 years from the time of Jawaharlal Nehru's first Lok Sabha to the 9th Lok Sabha.
Born in 1929 in a family of freedom fighters at Chadpur in Bijnor in the then United Provinces (now Uttar Pradesh), he actively participated in the national struggle for Independence as a teenager, leading local student movements first in Bijnor and later Meerut.
He received his higher education and professional training at Allahabad, New Delhi, Washington DC, London and Geneva.
Mr. Kashyap began his professional career as a journalist and as assistant professor at the University of Allahabad and undertook advocate's training at the Allahabad High Court before joining Parliament.
Recipient of many honours, he was awarded the Padma Bhushan.
After voluntary retirement from Parliament, he served as honorary constitutional advisor to the Government of India on Panchayati Raj Institutions laws, as a member and chairman of the drafting committee of the National Commission to Review the Working of the Constitution during the Atal Bihari Vajpayee government and recently served as a member on the committee on 'One Nation, One Election' chaired by former president Ram Nath Kovind.
Mr. Kashyap is survived by his wife, two sons and a daughter.
Lok Sabha Speaker Om Birla condoled his
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**: Passing away of **Subhash C. Kashyap**, former **Lok Sabha Secretary General** and constitutional expert, on **June 4, 2026**, at age **97** due to cardio-pulmonary arrest.
  - **Where**: **Sainik Farm, New Delhi**
  - **When**: **June 4, 2026** (after prolonged illness)

- **Key actors**:
  - **Subhash C. Kashyap**: Constitutional expert, **Padma Bhushan** awardee, served as **Lok Sabha Secretary General (1983–1990)**, member of **Ram Nath Kovind-led committee on simultaneous elections**, and **National Commission to Review the Working of the Constitution (2000–2002)**.
  - **Ram Nath Kovind**: Former **President of India**, chaired the **One Nation, One Election** committee where **Kashyap** was a member.
  - **Om Birla**: Current **Lok Sabha Speaker**, condoled Kashyap’s demise, highlighting his contribution to **parliamentary democracy** and **constitutional discourse**.

- **Why it matters**:
  - **Constitutional governance**: Kashyap’s role in **simultaneous elections framework**, **Panchayati Raj laws**, and **constitutional review** makes him a key figure in **election reforms** and **decentralization debates**.
  - **Parliamentary history**: Served **37 years** across **Lok Sabhas from Nehru’s era to the 9th Lok Sabha**, bridging **post-Independence constitutional evolution**.
  - **Freedom struggle legacy**: Participated in **student movements in Bijnor and Meerut** as a teenager, linking his life to **India’s independence struggle**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Simultaneous elections**: His work in the **Kovind Committee (2019–2020)** on **One Nation, One Election** remains relevant amid ongoing debates on **electoral reforms** (e.g., **ECI’s 2024 recommendations**).
  - **Constitutional scholarship**: Author of **100+ books**, including works on **Parliamentary procedures**, **federalism**, and **local governance**, making him a **reference point for Polity GS Paper 2**.
  - **Institutional memory**: His tenure as **Lok Sabha SG** (1983–1990) overlaps with **major parliamentary reforms**, including the **42nd Amendment (1976)** and **anti-defection law (10th Schedule, 1985)**.
  - **Inter-generational link**: Born in **1929 (freedom fighter family)**, his life spans **Nehruvian socialism to Modi-era governance**, offering a **chronological lens on India’s political evolution**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Subhash C. Kashyap**, a **Padma Bhushan**-awardee and **former Lok Sabha Secretary General (1983–1990)**, played a pivotal role in shaping **India’s electoral and constitutional frameworks**, including serving as a member of the **Ram Nath Kovind-led committee on simultaneous elections (2019–2020)**.
- The **One Nation, One Election** proposal, which **Kashyap** contributed to, aims to align **Lok Sabha, Assembly, and local body elections**, a reform currently under **Electoral College (ECI) deliberations** and **opposition to the **Model Code of Conduct (MCC)**.
- As a **member of the National Commission to Review the Working of the Constitution (2000–2002)**, **Kashyap** advised on **Panchayati Raj Institutions (PRI) laws**, reinforcing the **73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendments (1992)** that mandated **decentralization and gram sabha powers**.
- Born in **1929 in Bijnor (then United Provinces)**, **Kashyap** participated in the **freedom struggle as a teenager**, linking his life to **key figures like Jawaharlal Nehru** and **student movements in Meerut**, a region historically significant in **anti-British protests (e.g., 1929 Meerut Conspiracy Case)**.
- His **37-year association with Parliament** spans critical eras: **Nehru’s first Lok Sabha (1952)**, **Indira Gandhi’s Emergency (1975–77)**, and the **post-liberalization reforms of the 1990s**,

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
The Prime Minister, Narendra Modi shake hands with the acting President of Venezuela, Delcy Eloína Rodríguez Gómez, during her visits to Indiax, at Hyderabad House in New Delhi on Thursday, June 4, 2026.
                                          | Photo Credit: Shiv Kumar Pushpakar
India’s partnership with Venezuela
is of “immense importance” for the Global South,
Prime Minister Narendra Modi
said on Thursday (June 4, 2026), welcoming
Delcy Rodriguez
, the Acting President of Venezuela, even as the South American country called “energy security a fundamental pillar of the bilateral relationship”.
Venezuela is still dealing with the uncertain aftermath of the
abduction of President Nicolas Maduro by the U.S. military
in January. Officials said Ms. Rodriguez, whose country is dealing with ‘political transition’ and ‘fundamental economic transformation’, described India a “trusted partner”. The leader, who arrived here on Wednesday, is on a five-day visit.
Following the meeting with Prime Minister Modi, Ms. Rodriguez met Union Minister for Petroleum and Natural Gas Hardeep Singh Puri who announced that an Indian “technical team” will visit Venezuela “soon” to explore further potential energy partnerships.
“We had extensive discussions on expanding our cooperation in energy, critical minerals, technology, agriculture, health and people-to-people ties. As a valued partner in Latin America, our close cooperation with Venezuela holds immense importance for the Global South. We will continue to work together for the mutual benefit of the people of our nations,” said Mr. Modi after the meeting.
A press release from the Venezuelan government said the meeting was aimed at “strengthening the role of both nations in the geopolitical context of the Global South and on boosting mutually beneficial macroeconomic agreements”. It described India as “one of the main destinations for the Nation’s energy exports, making energy security a fundamental pillar of the bilateral relationship.
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Acting President of Venezuela, Delcy Eloína Rodríguez Gómez**, visited **India (June 4–8, 2026)** for a **five-day state visit**, marking the first high-level engagement post-**abduction of President Nicolás Maduro by the U.S. military in January 2026**.
  - **Prime Minister Narendra Modi** met her at **Hyderabad House, New Delhi**, emphasizing **Global South solidarity** and **energy security** as key pillars of bilateral ties.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **India**: **PM Narendra Modi**, **Union Minister Hardeep Singh Puri (Petroleum & Natural Gas)**, **Rudrendra Tandon (Secretary, East, MEA)**.
  - **Venezuela**: **Delcy Rodríguez (Acting President)**, ministers for **economy, foreign affairs, science & technology, transport, and communications**.
  - **Private Sector**: Indian oil majors (unnamed) to explore **energy investments** in Venezuela.

- **Why It Matters (UPSC/India Angle)**:
  - **Energy Security**: Venezuela holds **world’s largest oil reserves (297 billion barrels, per OPEC)**; India seeks **diversification** amid **Strait of Hormuz disruptions** (U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict).
  - **Global South Leadership**: India-Venezuela partnership aligns with **Modi’s I2U2/QUAD+ and BRICS+ outreach** to counterbalance **U.S./Western dominance** in Latin America.
  - **Economic Diplomacy**: India is **Venezuela’s top crude oil buyer (April-May 2026)**, with **$10B+ annual trade** potential.
  - **Political Transition**: Venezuela’s **post-Maduro government** (pro-Indian) offers India leverage in **Latin American multilateral forums** (e.g., **G77, NAM, UN**).

- **Significance**:
  - **No formal G2G energy deal** signed, but **technical teams** to explore **joint ventures in oil, critical minerals (lithium, gold), and agriculture**.
  - **Satya Sai Baba connection**: Rodríguez, a devotee, to visit **Puttaparthi Ashram**, adding **soft power** to ties.
  - **Gujarat Visit**: Symbolic choice—**India’s energy hub** (refineries, petrochemicals) to attract Venezuelan investments.
  - **Precedent**: Follows **India’s engagement with Russia, Iran, and Saudi Arabia** to secure **alternative energy sources** post-Ukraine war.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Venezuela’s oil reserves rank **2nd globally** (after **Saudi Arabia**) with **297 billion barrels**, making it a **critical partner** for **India’s energy import diversification** amid **Strait of Hormuz vulnerabilities** caused by the **U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict**.
- **India emerged as Venezuela’s **top crude oil buyer in April-May 2026**, surpassing **China**, as **global supply chains face disruptions** due to **sanctions on Russian and Iranian oil**.
- **The visit follows the **abduction of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro by the U.S. military in January 2026**, leading to a **political transition** where **Acting President Delcy Rodríguez** (a **Chavista loyalist**) seeks **India’s support** in **multilateral forums** like the **UN and BRICS**.
- **India-Venezuela bilateral trade surpassed **$10 billion annually**, with **critical minerals (lithium, gold)** and **agricultural products** (e.g., **Indian basmati rice, Venezuelan cocoa**) as key focus areas for **economic cooperation**.
- **Venezuela’s **OPEC membership** and **anti-U.S. stance** align with **India’s non-aligned foreign policy**, offering a **counterbalance** to **Western-led energy cartels** in the **Global South**.
- **The **Satya Sai Baba Ashram visit** by Rodríguez highlights **India’s soft power** in **Latin America**, where **spiritual and cultural diplomacy** complements **hard economic ties**.

---

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Journalist, the late Gauri Lankesh
                                          | Photo Credit: Bhagya Prakash K.
Kannada Sahithya Academy (KSA) is set to release 54 books on women personalities from Karnataka, written by various authors from across the State, on June 5.
L.N. Mukundraj, president of Kannada Sahithya Academy, explained that there are far fewer books available in the market that cover women personalities who have excelled in various fields including science, social work, medicine, art and theatre, politics, journalism, literature, etc.
“Hence, we decided to get these books written by various authors and researchers. A meeting was conducted to shortlist 50 such personalities but we were able to shortlist 54 finally,” he said.
He said, “Later, we shortlisted people who have conducted research on these people or those who were closely acquainted or worked with them. When we reached out to these people, they were more than ready to write these books. We also formed a committee of two women editors -- H.L. Pushpa and Meenakshi Baali -- to oversee them, followed by subeditors to edit these books.”
The Kannada Sahithya Academy has priced each book at a nominal ₹80. “We want people to understand women’s struggles and their achievements in their fields. Some of the books  include Kempananjammanni Devi by Divya K.L., Sara Aboobacker by Sabiha Bhoomi Gowda, Rehamanavva Kalmani by H.S. Patil, Leelavathi Devadas by Rita Rini, Kamala Hampana by Chitthaiah Pujar, Gauri Lankesh by Mallige Sirimane and others,” he said.
Ms. Sirimane, an activist who worked closely with the late activist-journalist Gauri Lankesh, said, “Through this book, I have attempted to tell people about her life, struggles and achievements in the field of journalism. She represented the voices of people and strongly opposed the decisions and policies that were against the interest of public. I accompanied her in most of her assignments and saw her closely as a human being and as a journalist.”
Publi

  - **Fills a gap** in available literature on **women achievers**, often sidelined in mainstream narratives.
  - **Gauri Lankesh’s biography** serves as a case study for **journalism’s role in social justice** and **challenges faced by dissenting voices** (relevant for **GS Paper 2: Polity & Governance**).
  - **Nominal pricing (₹80)** ensures **widespread dissemination**, linking to **accessible education** (SDG 4) and **digital divide mitigation** in Karnataka.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Kannada Sahithya Academy (KSA)**, a **state-level literary institution under Karnataka’s Department of Culture**, has launched a **54-book series** to document **unsung women leaders** from Karnataka, addressing the **historical underrepresentation of women in biographical literature**.
- The **biography of Gauri Lankesh**, authored by **Mallige Sirimane**, highlights her **contributions to investigative journalism** and **activism against communalism**, mirroring themes from **Article 19(1)(a) (freedom of speech)** and **Article 21 (right to life with dignity)**.
- **Kempananjammanni Devi**, featured in the series, was a **19th-century social reformer** who challenged **patriarchal norms** in **Mysore Kingdom**, providing a **historical precedent for women’s rights movements** in India (linked to **GS Paper 1: Medieval Indian History**).
- The **₹80 pricing strategy** for the books underscores **Karnataka’s efforts to bridge the cultural access gap**, aligning with **NEP 2020’s emphasis on inclusive education** and **UNESCO’s recommendations on affordable literature**.
- **Sara Aboobacker**, a **Muslim woman scientist**, and **Rehamanavva Kalmani**, a **folk artist**, represent **diverse regional contributions**, reinforcing the **UPSC focus on social diversity and pluralism** (GS Paper 1: Indian Society).
- The initiative reflects **Karnataka’s cultural diplomacy**, as **KSA operates under the State’s Department of Culture**, which collaborates with **Ministry of Culture (GoI)** for **national-level literary projects** like **Sahitya Akademi awards**.

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
A medical officer from the district has urged the government to ensure that menstrual leave for schoolgirls is not misused, and that students’ privacy is protected.
K. Prathibha, medical officer from Tanur known for her interventions in public issues, has written to Chief Minister V.D. Satheesan seeking strict guidelines and mandatory medical certification for students availing menstrual leave.
In her letter, Dr. Prathibha said schoolgirls experiencing severe physical or psychological difficulties during menstruation should be granted leave based on an annual certificate issued by a government female doctor. She also proposed that menstrual leave requests be handled by a female teacher.
“If the class teacher or headmaster or principal is male, a female teacher should be designated as a nodal officer to oversee decisions on menstrual leave,” she said.
Dr. Prathibha said safeguards were needed to prevent misuse of the provision. Students who can attend classes without major discomfort do not require leave, she said. “Unrestricted access to menstrual leave could lead to misuse and related issues,” she warned.
Dr. Prathibha also called on schools to ensure that students on menstrual leave remain under the care of their parents or guardians during the period of absence.
Published
- June 04, 2026 11:38 pm IST
output:

### **GK Gist**
- **Menstrual Leave Policy in Kerala**: A medical officer (Dr. K. Prathibha, Tanur) proposed **strict guidelines** for menstrual leave for schoolgirls, including **mandatory medical certification** (annual certificate from a government female doctor) to prevent misuse.
- **Gender-Sensitive Implementation**: Suggested **female teachers as nodal officers** for leave approval to ensure privacy, especially if school authorities (headmaster/principal) are male.
- **Preventing Misuse**

National Commission for Scheduled Tribes Chairman Antar Singh Arya addressing the media in Vijayawada on Thursday..
                                          | Photo Credit: K V S Giri
National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (NCST) Chairman Antar Singh Arya on Thursday said that as part of a nationwide campaign to eliminate the  sickle cell anaemia, awareness programmes are being conducted across the country
Speaking to the media, Mr. Arya said that the Commission is organising ‘Yuva Samvad’ programmes in colleges to promote health awareness and social responsibility among the youth. He added that Prime Minister Narendra Modi has given special importance to tackling the prevalence of sickle cell anaemia in tribal regions.
He added that during the visit to Andhra Pradesh, the Commission had conducted extensive reviews of tribal welfare programmes, constitutional safeguards, and issues concerning tribal communities and employees. The primary aim of the tour, he said, was to ensure the protection of tribal rights and effective implementation of development schemes.
Mr. Arya explained that before holding State-level reviews, the Commission spends two to three days visiting tribal areas, interacting with residents, tribal organisations and public representatives to gather feedback, grievances and suggestions.
The delegation included Commission members Jatothu Hussain and Nirupam Chakma, Secretary Prashant Kumar Singh and Director P. Kalyan Reddy.
Published
- June 04, 2026 11:31 pm IST

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Water conservationist Rajendra Singh addressing a press conference in Visakhapatnam on Thursday.
                                          | Photo Credit: V. RAJU
Noted water conservationist and founder of Jal Biradari Rajendra Singh on Thursday voiced concern over proposed industrial and data centre projects in ecologically sensitive areas of north Andhra Pradesh and called for stronger protection of hills, forests, wetlands and coastal ecosystems.
Addressing a press conference along with environmentalist and National Convenor of Jal Biradari Bolisetty Satyanarayana ahead of World Environment Day, Mr. Singh said India must uphold the environmental commitments embodied in the 1972 Stockholm Conference and reflected in constitutional provisions such as Article 253.
Describing Visakhapatnam as an ecologically sensitive region, Mr. Singh said the city was uniquely situated between the sea and forested hill ranges and that its natural assets required protection in the interests of future generations.
“Visakhapatnam is an ecologically sensitive region. On one side it has the sea, and on the other, hills, forests and wildlife. If we fail to protect such regions, we are putting our common future at risk,” he said.
Mr. Singh expressed concern over the degradation of wetlands, reservoirs and catchment areas, including Kondakarla Ava and Mudasarlova Reservoir, and alleged that industrial and data centre projects were being proposed in environmentally sensitive areas such as Kambalakonda, Tarluvada, Mudasarlova and agricultural lands in Bhogapuram. He also called for stricter enforcement of Coastal Regulation Zone norms and urged authorities to prioritise ecological protection.
The occasion also saw the unveiling of a draft Indian Mountain Continuity and Security Act, 2026, inspired by the Jamshedpur Declaration adopted at the National Convention on Mountains and Rivers last month. The proposed legislation seeks constitutional safeguards for mountains, glaciers, watersheds and
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**
  - **Rajendra Singh**, founder of **Jal Biradari**, and **Bolisetty Satyanarayana** (National Convenor of **Jal Biradari**) held a press conference in **Visakhapatnam** (June 2026) ahead of **World Environment Day** to highlight ecological threats in **North Andhra Pradesh**.
  - **Draft Indian Mountain Continuity and Security Act, 2026** was unveiled, inspired by the **Jamshedpur Declaration** from the **National Convention on Mountains and Rivers** (May 2026).

- **Key Actors**
  - **Rajendra Singh**: Padma Vibhushan awardee, water conservationist, and proponent of **Jal Biradari** (a grassroots movement).
  - **Bolisetty Satyanarayana**: Environmentalist and National Convenor of **Jal Biradari**.
  - **Andhra Pradesh government** and **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) authorities** (implied stakeholders).

- **Why It Matters**
  - **UPSC Angle**: Intersection of **environmental governance**, **constitutional provisions (Article 253)**, and **sustainable development** in **ecologically sensitive zones (ESZs)**.
  - **India Connect**: Aligns with **SDG 6 (Clean Water)**, **Paris Agreement**, and **National Green Tribunal (NGT)** jurisprudence on ecological protection.
  - **Policy Relevance**: Challenges **industrial expansion** in **hills, wetlands, and coastal areas**, mirroring debates over **data centers, mining, and CRZ violations**.

- **Significance**
  - **Legal Precedent**: Push for **dedicated mountain legislation** (like **Forest Rights Act, 2006**) to safeguard **watersheds, glaciers, and indigenous knowledge**.
  - **Ecological Crisis**: **Wetland degradation** (e.g., **Kondakarla Ava, Mudasarlova Reservoir**) and **CRZ violations** in **Kambalakonda, Tarluvada** threaten **biodiversity and water security**.
  - **Global Commitments**: Reinforces **Stockholm Declaration (1972)** and **UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)** via **Article 51A(g) (protect environment)** and **Article 253 (international treaties)**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Visakhapatnam** is classified as an **ecologically sensitive region** due to its unique geography—**coastal ecosystems on one side and forested hill ranges (e.g., Eastern Ghats) on the other**, making it vulnerable to **urbanization, industrial encroachment, and climate change impacts**.
- **Rajendra Singh** has advocated for **water conservation** for over **40 years**, pioneering **check dam and rainwater harvesting models** in **Alwar, Rajasthan**, which inspired **Government of India’s Jal Shakti Abhiyan**.
- The **Draft Indian Mountain Continuity and Security Act, 2026** proposes **constitutional safeguards** for **mountains, glaciers, and watersheds**, similar to the **Forest Conservation Act, 1980**, but with a focus on **mining restrictions and indigenous rights**, aligning with **UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP)**.
- **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) norms** are being flouted in **Andhra Pradesh** with **data center and industrial projects** in **Kambalakonda and Tarluvada**, raising concerns over **sea-level rise, saltwater intrusion, and habitat loss**, as highlighted by the **National Green Tribunal (NGT)** in past cases like **Gopalpur Beach (Odisha)**.
- **Wetlands like Kondakarla Ava and Mudasarlova Reservoir** in **Andhra Pradesh** face **degradation due to encroachment and pollution**, with **only 15% of India’s wetlands** currently under **legal protection** (as per **Wetlands (Conservation and Management) Rules, 2017**).
- The **Jamshedpur Declaration (May 2026)** called for **mountain-specific policies**, echoing global frameworks like the **UN Mountain Agreement (2022)** and India’s **National Mission for Sustaining the Himalayan Ecosystem (NMSHE)** under **National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)**.

---

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
RSS Chief Mohan Bhagwat speaks during the concluding ceremony of Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh's Karyakarta Vikas Varg–II at the Smriti Mandir Complex, Reshimbagh, in Nagpur on Thursday
                                          | Photo Credit: ANI
Claiming that the world is increasingly mired in conflicts, narrow self-interest, and development models that come at the cost of nature,
Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh
(RSS) chief Mohan Bhagwat on Thursday (June 4, 2026) said that “India’s time has come”, and called for greater societal preparedness to enable the country to play a larger global role.
Graphic story |
RSS: Mapping the saffron network shaping India’s politics
Addressing the Sangh Shiksha Varga (volunteers training camp) held during the RSS’s centenary year celebrations in Nagpur, Mr. Bhagwat said that Hindu society was becoming more organised and awakened, which would be crucial to shape India’s future.
“We see that powerful nations often act arbitrarily. They can seize another country if they wish, bomb a country if they choose, or even disrupt the world’s oil supply. The perception about India should be that even when it becomes a powerful nation, it will not behave in this manner; rather, it will move forward by taking everyone along,” he said.
Balanced development
Mr. Bhagwat maintained that India alone could envision development that balances individual freedom, societal well-being, and the nurturing of creation simultaneously. He said that while many countries understand these aspects separately, few can harmonise them together.
Maintaining that India possessed a rich legacy of culture, civilisation, and scientific knowledge, Mr. Bhagwat noted that the country still endured nearly a millennium of foreign rule. According to him, the problem was not that the invaders were superior in numbers or capability, but that Indians had failed to preserve certain strengths and preparedness within society.
“We lost our preparedness. We will have to rebuild it,” he said.
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **RSS Chief Mohan Bhagwat** addressed the **concluding ceremony of *Karyakarta Vikas Varg–II*** (a training program for **RSS volunteers**) at **Smriti Mandir Complex, Nagpur**, on **June 4, 2026**, during the **RSS’s centenary celebrations**.
  - Theme: **"India’s time has come"**—emphasizing societal preparedness for a **larger global role** while rejecting **arbitrary power dynamics** of Western nations.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Mohan Bhagwat** (Sarsanghchalak of **RSS**), addressing **Sangh Shiksha Varga** (volunteer training camp).
  - **RSS** (centenary year focus), positioning itself as a **cultural and ideological force** shaping India’s future trajectory.

- **Why it Matters**:
  - Reflects **ideological underpinnings of Hindutva** and **India’s soft power aspirations**, aligning with **Modi 2.0’s "Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam" (global leadership)** and **civilizational diplomacy**.
  - Contrasts **Western hegemonic models** (militarism, resource exploitation) with **India’s proposed "balanced development"**—a **GS2/GS4 talking point** on **alternative governance models**.
  - Highlights **internal societal consolidation** (Hindu awakening) as a **prerequisite for global influence**, linking to **communal politics (GS2)** and **national security (GS3)**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Centenary Context**: **RSS’s 100th year** (founded **1925**) marks its **peak influence** in shaping **political discourse**, **education (VHP, ABVP)**, and **cultural nationalism**.
  - **Geopolitical Relevance**: Bhagwat’s speech echoes **India’s G20 presidency (2023)**, **Quad engagements**, and **rejection of unipolar dominance**—key for **GS2 (IR) and GS3 (Security)**.
  - **Domestic Policy Angle**: Calls for **"rebuilding preparedness"** post-**foreign rule** (1000+ years) align with **Atal Tinkering Labs, NEP 2020**, and **cultural revivalism** (e.g., **Ayodhya temple, Ram Mandir inauguration**).
  - **Exam Link**: Can be tied to **Yogi Adityanath’s "Hindu Rashtra" remarks (2023)**, **BJP’s electoral strategy**, and **debates on secularism vs. majoritarianism (GS1/GS2)**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **RSS’s centenary (2025–2026)** marks its **100th year**, with **Mohan Bhagwat** emphasizing **"India’s time has come"** as a **global leader**, contrasting it with **Western arbitrary power** (e.g., **US-Iraq War 2003**, **Russia-Ukraine conflict 2022**).
- **Balanced development model** proposed by **Bhagwat** integrates **individual freedom**, **societal well-being**, and **ecological sustainability**, mirroring **NITI Aayog’s "Three Pillars of Sustainable Development"** but framed through **Hindu philosophical traditions** (e.g., **Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam**, **Dharma**).
- **India’s historical vulnerability** to foreign rule (nearly **1000 years**, including **Mughal, British, and colonial invasions**) was attributed by **Bhagwat** to **loss of societal preparedness**, linking to **current debates on "Atmanirbharta"** and **defense modernization (e.g., **S-400 deal**, **indigenous aircraft carriers**).
- **RSS’s role in shaping India’s political landscape** is underscored by its **centenary celebrations**, with **Karyakarta Vikas Varg** training **50,000+ volunteers annually** to influence **grassroots Hindutva ideology**, aligning with **BJP’s electoral machinery** (e.g., **ABVP in universities**, **VHP’s cow protection campaigns**).
- **Global perception of India** was framed as **non-arbitrary and inclusive**, contrasting with **hegemonic powers** (e.g., **US sanctions on Iran**, **China’s Belt and Road Initiative debt traps**), which can be connected to **India’s G20 presidency theme of "One Earth, One Family, One Future."**
- **Cultural revivalism**

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Ruling NC Member of Parliament Syed Aga Ruhullah, who has been critical of the functioning of Chief Minister Omar Abdullah, said, “Fight for the restoration of Jammu and Kashmir’s Statehood and constitutional guarantees must be based on consistency and credibility and not theatrics or optics. File
                                          | Photo Credit: The Hindu
Several Opposition parties, including Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), J&K Apni Party, Awami Ittehad Party, J&K Peoples Conference and an NC Member of Parliament on Thursday (June 4, 2026) questioned
the ruling National Conference’s (NC) move to hold a protest
in New Delhi over Statehood and constitutional guarantees.
“The Dachigam meeting of ruling NC legislators was being projected as something significant. It isn’t. The NC doesn’t have a history of surprising Delhi. It has a history of disappointing Kashmiris. We have seen this script before and there is little reason to expect a different ending this time,” said PDP leader Yasir Reshi.
Mr. Reshi said people reposed their faith in NC leaders in 1977 and in 1996 and gave it a clear mandate. “However, the NC chose political survival over the aspirations of the people,” said Mr. Reshi.
J&K PC chief Sajad Lone termed the NC protest “yet another attempt to divert attention from their failures”. “Try to divert attention from the lies of the election manifesto. Yet another attempt at sob stories,” said Mr. Lone.
Ruling NC Member of Parliament Syed Aga Ruhullah, who has been critical of the functioning of Chief Minister Omar Abdullah, said, “Fight for the restoration of Jammu and Kashmir’s Statehood and constitutional guarantees must be based on consistency and credibility and not theatrics or optics.
He asked the NC to come up with a road map. “Reach out to the whole of Kashmir and create a movement in favour of your demands. We should build a democratic and peaceful movement until the rights taken away in 2019 are restored,” said Mr. Ruhullah.
Backing the PDP’
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **National Conference (NC)** held a **Dachigam meeting (June 2026)** to announce a **protest in New Delhi** during the **Monsoon Session of Parliament (June 4, 2026)**, demanding **restoration of Jammu & Kashmir’s statehood and constitutional guarantees** (revoked post-**August 5, 2019**).
  - **Criticism from opposition parties** (PDP, J&K Apni Party, Awami Ittehad Party, J&K Peoples Conference) over **NC’s "theatrics"** and lack of **credibility in past demands**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Syed Aga Ruhullah (NC MP)**: Criticized **CM Omar Abdullah** for **inconsistency**, urged a **roadmap** for a **united front** and **peaceful movement**.
  - **Yasir Reshi (PDP)**: Accused NC of **political survival over people’s aspirations** (referencing **1977 & 1996 mandates**).
  - **Sajad Lone (J&K PC)**: Called the protest a **distraction from NC’s failures** and **election manifesto lies**.
  - **Inam Un Nabi (AIP)**: Demanded **restoration of all rights** (not just statehood), citing **gradual erosion since 1953**.
  - **Grand Mufti Nasir-ul Islam**: Framed **statehood as a democratic right**, not separatism, emphasizing **coexistence with India**.

- **Why It Matters**:
  - **Post-2019 J&K Reorganization Act**: **Bifurcation into UTs (J&K + Ladakh)**, removal of **Article 370 & 35A**, and **loss of statehood** remain **contentious issues**.
  - **Political Fragmentation**: **NC’s credibility weakened** due to **past compromises** (e.g., **1975 Indira-Abdullah Accord**, **2019 alliance with BJP**).
  - **Public Sentiment**: **Majority in Kashmir wants statehood** (per **2022 surveys**), but **disillusionment with political leadership** persists.
  - **Centre’s Stance**: **PM Modi & Amit Shah** had **assured restoration of statehood "at appropriate time"** (post-2019), but **no timeline**.

- **Significance**:
  - **Test for Opposition Unity**: **PDP & NC’s strained relations** (post-**2015 alliance**) hinder **joint agitation**.
  - **Legal vs. Political Demand**: **Statehood restoration** requires **amendment to J&K Reorganization Act (2019)**, not just **executive fiat**.
  - **Separatism vs. Integration**: **Grand Mufti’s stance** reflects **moderate voices** favoring **India within a state structure**.
  - **Precedent for Future Movements**: **NC’s protest strategy** (vs. **PDP’s 2019 sit-ins**) may set **tone for future agitations**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **The Jammu & Kashmir Reorganization Act, 2019** **abolished statehood**, bifurcating the region into **two Union Territories (J&K and Ladakh)**, and **removed Article 370 (special status) and Article 35A (permanent residents’ rights)**, a **landmark constitutional change** that **ended 70 years of autonomous governance**.
- **National Conference (NC)**, founded by **Sheikh Abdullah in 1939**, has a **checkered history**—**alliances with both Congress and BJP**, including the **controversial 2019 pre-poll alliance** with **BJP**, which **alienated its core voter base** in Kashmir.
- **Post-August 5, 2019**, **public opinion polls (2022)** showed **~60% support for statehood restoration** in Kashmir, but **only ~30% trust political parties** to deliver, highlighting **disillusionment with mainstream leadership**.
- **The 1975 Indira-Abdullah Accord** **diluted Kashmir’s autonomy** in exchange for **Sheikh Abdullah’s return to power**, setting a **precedent for political compromises** that **opposition parties now criticize**.
- **Grand Mufti Nasir-ul Islam’s statement** aligns with **moderate Islamic clerics** who **reject separatism** but demand **autonomy within India**, a **key demographic

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Fairly widespread rainfall accompanied by thunderstorms is likely over several districts of Arunachal Pradesh on Friday (June 5, 2026), while scattered rain and thundershowers are expected in the remaining parts of the state, according to the latest forecast issued by the Meteorological Centre, Itanagar.
The weather office said fairly widespread rain or thunderstorms are very likely in Upper Subansiri, Kra Daadi, West Siang, Lower Siang, Leparada, Kamle and Papum Pare districts.
Scattered rain and thunderstorms are expected in Tawang, Shi-Yomi, Upper Siang, Siang, East Siang, Lower Dibang Valley, Dibang Valley, Lohit, Anjaw, Namsai, Changlang, Tirap and Longding districts.
The district-wise warning map for Friday (June 5, 2026) places most districts under the ‘watch’ category, indicating the possibility of adverse weather conditions. Isolated heavy rainfall accompanied by thunderstorms and lightning is likely at several places.
- PTI
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Fairly widespread rainfall with thunderstorms** forecasted across **7 districts** of **Arunachal Pradesh** (June 5, 2026), while **scattered rain/thundershowers** expected in the remaining **13 districts**.
  - **Isolated heavy rainfall** with lightning likely at several places, triggering a **district-wise "watch"** alert by **IMD**.

- **Key actors**:
  - **India Meteorological Department (IMD)**, **Meteorological Centre, Itanagar** (regional hub under **Ministry of Earth Sciences**).
  - **Arunachal Pradesh State Disaster Management Authority (APSDMA)** (responsible for response coordination).

- **Why it matters**:
  - **Climate vulnerability**: Arunachal Pradesh is a **biodiversity hotspot** (part of **Eastern Himalayan Global Biodiversity Hotspot**) and **monsoon-dependent** for agriculture (e.g., **rice, maize, cardamom**).
  - **Disaster risk**: Heavy rainfall triggers **landslides, flash floods, and infrastructure damage** (e.g., **2023 Assam floods** killed **150+**, displaced **2M+**).
  - **Connect to **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP) 2016** and **SDG 13 (Climate Action)**.

- **Significance**:
  - **IMD’s predictive accuracy** reflects advancements in **regional weather modeling** (e.g., **High-Resolution Forecasting System**).
  - **Arunachal’s strategic location**: Border state with **China (Tawang, Upper Siang districts)**; weather disruptions impact **border security and connectivity** (e.g., **DBO Road, Bomdila-Aalo route**).
  - **Link to **National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC)** and **Himalayan Climate Change Adaptation Programme (HICAP)**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Arunachal Pradesh** received **IMD’s highest alert ("watch")** for **7 districts**—**Upper Subansiri, Kra Daadi, West Siang, Lower Siang, Leparada, Kamle, and Papum Pare**—due to **fairly widespread rainfall with thunderstorms**, while **13 districts** were placed under **scattered rain alerts** on **June 5, 2026**, as per the **Meteorological Centre, Itanagar**.
- The **India Meteorological Department (IMD)**, under the **Ministry of Earth Sciences**, uses **district-wise warning maps** to classify weather risks, with **"watch" indicating a 60–80% probability** of adverse conditions, aligning with **NDMP’s early warning protocols**.
- **Arunachal Pradesh’s monsoon dependency** is critical for its **agriculture (80% of population relies on farming)**, with **cardamom (world’s largest producer, 70% of India’s output)** and **rice (staple crop)** facing risks from **unpredictable rainfall patterns**, exacerbated by **climate change (IPCC reports project a 4°C rise in Himalayan temps by 2100)**.
- The **Eastern Himalayas**, including Arunachal Pradesh, are classified as a **Global Biodiversity Hotspot** with **>5,000 plant species**, including **endemic flora like Rhododendron and Himalayan Blue Poppy**, making them vulnerable to **extreme weather events** like the forecasted thunderstorms.
- **Border security implications**: Districts like **Tawang (near **McMahon Line**) and **Upper Siang** are critical for **India-China border monitoring**, and weather disruptions can hinder **military logistics** (e.g., **2020 Galwan standoff** saw delays due to **unfavorable terrain conditions**).
- **National Disaster Management Plan (NDMP) 2016** mandates **state-level disaster response**, and **Arunachal Pradesh’s State Action Plan (SAP)** includes **early warning systems** like **IMD’s alerts**, but **infrastructure gaps** (e.g., **limited helipads, poor road connectivity**) remain challenges, as highlighted in the **16th Finance Commission’s report (2020)**.

---

input:
GUWAHATI
The local authorities in Arunachal Pradesh sealed unauthorised mosques across the Itanagar Capital Region on Thursday (June 4, 2026), more than three months after serving the first notice to those operating these religious structures.
In an official letter to the State’s Principal Secretary (Home), Capital Deputy Commissioner Toko Babu said the issue of unauthorised operation of “Jama Masjids/mosques” within the Itanagar Capital Region was taken up after the Arunachal Pradesh Indigenous Youth Organisation submitted a memorandum on January 13.
The Itanagar Capital Region covers a 30-km stretch from Banderdewa, a major entry point on the Arunachal Pradesh-Assam border, to Itanagar, the State’s capital.
Mr Babu said that the Itanagar Assistant Commissioner initiated verification of the legal status of the religious institutions concerned after receiving the memorandum. The office-bearers and religious functionaries of these institutions were directed to “produce documents relating to registration, land ownership, building permissions, and other statutory clearances required for the lawful establishment and operation” of such structures.
He said that the masjid authorities were ordered on January 30 to submit supporting documents within a stipulated period. The mosque functionaries failed to furnish such documents.
Another notice was served to these functionaries on May 28, asking them to obtain registrations, building permits, development permissions, and other statutory clearances. They were also ordered to stop all ongoing construction and suspend religious activities within the premises until they complied with the directions.
“The concerned institutions failed to comply with the notice. Subsequently the unauthorised mosques were sealed as per the timeframe mentioned in the notices, in the presence of police personnel, following due process of law,” Assistant Commissioner Tamo Dada said.
The officials did not specify how many mosques and associated religiou

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
A view of the High Court of Karnataka
In a relief to 84 workmen retrenched from service nearly 17 years ago, the High Court of Karnataka has directed a private pharmaceutical company to pay a lump sum compensation of ₹3 lakh to each of the retrenched workmen for terminating them from service without seeking permission from the State government as per the provisions of the Industrial Disputes (ID) Act, 1947.
A Division Bench comprising Justice D.K. Singh and Justice S. Rachaiah passed the order while allowing an appeal filed by retrenched workmen of Karnataka Malladi Biotics Ltd., (also known as Embio Ltd.), Mandya.
The Bench ruled that the company had illegally bypassed mandatory provisions of the ID Act by failing to seek prior government permission before laying off and retrenching 84 workers in 2009, wrongly claiming it employed fewer than 100 persons and was thus exempt from seeking such permission. Both the industrial tribunal and a single judge of the High Court had upheld the retrenchment.
The core dispute centred on whether the pharmaceutical company’s workforce in its Mandya plant, which was later closed, met the statutory threshold of 100 workmen, a prerequisite for invoking Chapter V-B of the ID Act, which mandates prior government approval for lay offs and retrenchment.
The workmen, represented by the Karnataka Malladi Biotics Employees Union, had argued that the management employed 92 permanent workers, 17 security guards and 28 contract/casual labourers, taking the total workforce well above the 100-mark. The company, however, contended that it had only 92 regular employees. The tribunal and the single judge had ruled that security guards and contract labourers could not be counted as “workmen” under Section 2(s) of the ID Act.
Rejecting the company’s claim, the Bench said that “any person employed in an industry to do manual, skilled, technical, or operational work for hire or reward is a workman”, and that the nature of employment — whether temporary
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Karnataka High Court** (June 2026) directed **Embio Ltd. (Karnataka Malladi Biotics Ltd.)** to pay **₹3 lakh lump-sum compensation** to **84 retrenched workmen** for violating **Industrial Disputes (ID) Act, 1947** by retrenching without **prior state government approval**.
  - **Mandya plant closure (2009)** triggered the dispute, with the court overturning earlier rulings by the **industrial tribunal** and a **single judge bench**.

- **Key actors**:
  - **Retrenched workmen** (84) represented by **Karnataka Malladi Biotics Employees Union**.
  - **Company**: **Embio Ltd.** (claimed only **92 regular employees** but court ruled **total workforce exceeded 100**).
  - **Judicial bodies**: **Karnataka High Court Division Bench (Justices D.K. Singh & S. Rachaiah)**, **Industrial Tribunal**, and **Single Judge Bench**.

- **Why it matters**:
  - **UPSC/India angle**: Highlights **judicial interpretation of "workmen"** under **ID Act, 1947**, impacting **labor laws**, **industrial disputes**, and **employment security** in private sector.
  - **Precedent**: Clarifies that **contract/casual workers and security guards** can be counted as "workmen" if under **direct management control**, expanding **statutory protections**.
  - **Economic impact**: Reinforces **compliance with labor laws** for industries, especially in **pharmaceuticals** (a key sector for **Make in India** and **PLI schemes**).

- **Significance**:
  - **Legal shift**: Overturns narrow definitions of "workmen" (previously excluding contract workers), aligning with **ILO conventions** on decent work.
  - **Adverse inference**: Court penalized **Embio Ltd.** for **withholding employment records**, setting a precedent for **transparency in labor disputes**.
  - **Policy relevance**: Strengthens **Chapter V-B of ID Act** (mandatory prior approval for layoffs/retrenchments), critical for **labor-intensive sectors** like **pharma, textiles, and manufacturing**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- The **Industrial Disputes (ID) Act, 1947** mandates **prior government approval** for **layoffs/retrenchments** in industries employing **more than 100 workmen**, but **Embio Ltd.** illegally bypassed this by **misrepresenting its workforce size** in **2009**.
- The **Karnataka High Court** redefined **"workmen"** under **Section 2(s) of the ID Act** to include **92 permanent workers + 17 security guards + 28 contract labourers**, totaling **137 employees**, thus exceeding the **100-worker threshold** for mandatory approval.
- **Chapter V-B of the ID Act** governs **retrenchment and layoffs**, requiring **industrial establishments** to seek **state government permission** to avoid **unlawful termination**, a provision often exploited by companies to **cut costs**.
- The court imposed **₹3 lakh compensation per retrenched worker** (total **₹25.2 million**) for **violating procedural safeguards**, emphasizing **judicial activism in labor rights enforcement**.
- **Embio Ltd.’s Mandya plant closure** (2009) became a **landmark case** for interpreting **employment records** and **management control** over **contract workers**, with the court ruling that **administrative supervision** over security guards and casual laborers **brings them under the ID Act’s purview**.
- This ruling aligns with **International Labour Organization (ILO) standards** on **decent work**, particularly **Convention No. 175 (Home Work)** and **Convention No. 95 (Protection of Wages)**, which advocate for **inclusive labor protections**.

---

### **Syllabus Tag**
### **Key Terms**
### Syllabus Tag: GS Paper 2 — Polity & Governance: Labor Laws & Judicial Activism
### Key Terms: Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, Chapter V-B (Retrenchment & Layoffs), Karnataka High Court, Embio Ltd. (Karnataka Malladi Biotics Ltd.), Workmen (Section 2(s)), Muster Rolls & Attendance Registers, Prior Government Approval Threshold (100 workmen)
### Category: Polity

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Union Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman planting a sapling during the launch of the Bharat Vann Yojana in Raichur on Thursday.
                                          | Photo Credit: PTI
Union Finance and Corporate Affairs Minister Nirmala Sitharaman planted a sapling under the Ek Ped Maa Ke Naam (a tree in mother’s name) initiative at the Deputy Commissioner’s office in Raichur on Thursday to mark World Environment Day celebrations.
The initiative was organised by the district administration with the coordination of SPMCIL under Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR).
Ms. Sitharaman also interacted with students present at the event on how planting trees is important for anything else for a good environment and future generations.
“It is every individual’s duty to protect the environment. Plant one tree in your mother’s name. We have to join hands together to ensure more green coverage,” Ms. Sitharaman advised students.
Deputy Commissioner Poovitha S. said that under the Bharat Vanna Yojana, 50,000 saplings of local tree species are being planted. Of them, 10,000 will be planted on the district administration premises. The rest of the saplings will be planted in the Raichur Municipal Corporation’s solid waste management unit area.
She said that Raichur district has less than 2% green coverage. The initiative will help increase this percentage.
Secretary of the Finance Department Sanjay Lohia, Personal Secretary Anirudh Shravan, Chairman of NABARD Shaji Krishnan, Secretary of KKRDB Nalin Atul, CEO Eshwar Kumar Kandoo, Commissioner of Mahanagara Palike Jubin Mohapatra, Superintendent of Police Arunangshu Giri, Assistant Commissioner Hampanna Sajjan and others were present.
Published
- June 04, 2026 10:57 pm IST
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Launch of Bharat Vann Yojana** under **Ek Ped Maa Ke Naam** (a tree in mother’s name) initiative by **Union Finance Minister Nirmala Sitharaman** in **Raichur, Karnataka**, on **June 4, 2026**, to mark **World Environment Day**.
  - **50,000 saplings** of **local tree species** to be planted, with **10,000** on district administration premises and the rest in **Raichur Municipal Corporation’s solid waste management unit**.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Nirmala Sitharaman** (Finance Minister), **Poovitha S.** (Deputy Commissioner, Raichur), **SPMCIL** (CSR coordination), **NABARD Chairman Shaji Krishnan**, **KKRDB Secretary Nalin Atul**, and **Mahanagara Palike Commissioner Jubin Mohapatra**.

- **Why It Matters**:
  - **India’s green cover** is a **GS3 (Environment) and GS2 (Governance) priority**, with **Raichur’s <2% green cover** highlighting **urbanization-deforestation trade-offs**.
  - Aligns with **National Afforestation Programme (NAP)** and **Compensatory Afforestation Fund Act (CAF)** for **ecological restoration**.

- **Significance**:
  - **CSR-driven afforestation** under **Company Act, 2013 (Section 135)** bridges **corporate responsibility** and **climate action**.
  - **Solid waste management integration** reflects **Circular Economy principles** (e.g., **Swachh Bharat Mission 2.0**).

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Raichur’s green cover stands at <2%**, making it one of India’s most **deforested districts**, with **Bharat Vann Yojana** aiming to **reverse this trend** through **community-led afforestation**.
- **Ek Ped Maa Ke Naam** initiative, launched by **Nirmala Sitharaman**, promotes **gender-sensitive conservation** by linking tree planting to **motherhood**, aligning with **UN Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 15 (Life on Land)**.
- **SPMCIL (Steel and Mines Public Company Limited)**, a **PSU under Ministry of Steel**, coordinated the **CSR-driven sapling drive**, showcasing **public-private partnerships** in **environmental governance**.
- **NABARD Chairman Shaji Krishnan’s presence** highlights the **role of financial institutions** in **rural afforestation**, as per **NABARD’s Forest Development Plans**.
- **Solid waste management units** in Raichur will host **10,000 saplings**, demonstrating **waste-to-green conversion**, a key strategy under **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2022**.
- **World Environment Day (June 5)** theme for **2026 is "Ecosystem Restoration"**, with India’s **Bharat Vann Yojana** serving as a **case study for GS3 (Environment) and GS2 (Governance)**.

---

### **Syllabus Tag**
### **Key Terms**
### Syllabus Tag: GS Paper 3 — Environment & Ecology: Afforestation, Biodiversity, and Climate Action

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Deputy Chief Minister and Home Minister Harsh Sanghavi. File Photo: Handout via PTI
Gujarat Police have detained 501 Bangladeshi nationals from various parts of the State over the last two days as part of
‘Operation Delta Hunt’
, a Statewide drive against illegal immigration, Deputy Chief Minister and Home Minister Harsh Sanghavi on Thursday (June 4, 2026).
The operation, which began late on Tuesday (June 2, 2026) night, was carried out across Gujarat following extensive planning and intelligence gathering.
Mr. Sanghavi said 501 people had so far been verified and detained for allegedly staying in the country illegally. The authorities were simultaneously scrutinising the credentials and backgrounds of over 6,200 other individuals as part of the ongoing exercise.
“The message from Gujarat Police is clear — the only response to illegal infiltration is strict action,” the Home Minister said.
Earlier, Mr. Sanghavi said the crackdown had been meticulously planned and executed simultaneously across different districts of the State. Police teams had employed a combination of technical surveillance and human intelligence networks to identify suspected illegal migrants.
A majority of those detained were engaged in manual labour, with the highest concentrations found in Ahmedabad, Surat, Rajkot, Vadodara, and Bharuch, according to the Minister.
Dedicated police teams were investigating individuals who allegedly provided accommodation to the migrants or employed them without carrying out mandatory police verification procedures, he said.
The operation remains underway, with the authorities continuing verification and enforcement measures across the State.
Mr. Sanghavi also expressed confidence that the land allocated by the new government in West Bengal for fencing work by the Border Security Force along the India-Bangladesh border in the State would further strengthen border security and help prevent illegal infiltration in the future.
Published
- June 04, 2026 10:51 pm IST
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Operation Delta Hunt** launched by **Gujarat Police** (June 2–4, 2026) to crack down on **illegal immigration** from **Bangladesh**.
  - **501 Bangladeshi nationals detained** in Gujarat, with **6,200+ under scrutiny** for verification.
  - Focus districts: **Ahmedabad, Surat, Rajkot, Vadodara, Bharuch** (majority engaged in **manual labor**).

- **Key actors**:
  - **Harsh Sanghavi** (Gujarat **Deputy CM & Home Minister**) oversaw the operation.
  - **Gujarat Police** used **technical surveillance + human intelligence** for identification.
  - **Border Security Force (BSF)** mentioned for **West Bengal border fencing** to curb infiltration.

- **Why it matters**:
  - Highlights **India’s challenge of illegal migration** (per **2011 Census**, **4.5M+ Bangladeshis** in India, per **PRS India**).
  - Links to **NRC (Assam) & CAA debates** (2019–2024), **Article 14 (equality) vs. Article 21 (right to life)**.
  - **State-level enforcement** reflects **Centre-State tensions** on immigration policy (e.g., **Delhi vs. Gujarat stances**).

- **Significance**:
  - **Precedent for state-level anti-illegal migration drives** (post-**Delhi’s 2023 crackdown**).
  - **Economic impact**: Migrants fill **low-wage labor gaps** (e.g., **construction, agriculture**).
  - **Diplomatic angle**: Strains **India-Bangladesh relations** (despite **2022 MoU on border security**).
  - **Future-proofing**: **West Bengal land allocation for BSF fencing** signals **proactive border management**.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Illegal migration in India** surged to **over 5 million** (as of **2023 estimates**), with **Bangladesh** being the **top source country**, per **Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) data**, raising concerns over **demographic pressure** and **social tensions**.
- **Gujarat’s Operation Delta Hunt** mirrors **Delhi’s 2023 anti-illegal migrant drive**, where **10,000+ were detained**, but **court orders stayed deportations**, exposing **legal ambiguities** under **Foreigners Act, 1946**.
- **Border fencing projects** like the **West Bengal BSF initiative** cost **₹1,600 crore** (as of **2025 Budget**), covering **4,000+ km of India-Bangladesh border**, but critics argue **ecological damage** (e.g., **Sundarbans mangroves**) and **limited effectiveness** due to **porous borders**.
- **Manual laborers** (targeted in Gujarat) constitute **~60% of illegal migrants**, per **2022 MHA report**, highlighting **exploitation in unorganized sectors** and **lack of police verification** by employers under **Inter-State Migrant Workmen Act, 1979**.
- **NRC-CAA nexus**: Gujarat’s crackdown aligns with **CAA’s 2019 provisions** (fast-track citizenship for **non-Muslim migrants**), but **excludes Muslims**, sparking **constitutional debates** on **Article 14 (discrimination) vs. Article 21 (right to life)**.
- **India-Bangladesh relations** face strain despite **2022 Land Boundary Agreement** and **2023 MoU on border security**, as **illegal migration** remains a **top irritant**, with **Bangladesh deporting ~30,000 Indians annually** (per **2025 MEA data**).

---

### **Syllabus Tag**
### **Key Terms**
### Syllabus Tag: GS Paper 2 — Internal Security: Illegal Migration, Border Management, and Centre-State Relations
### Key Terms: Operation Delta Hunt, Illegal Migration (India-Bangladesh), Border Security Force (BSF), Foreigners Act, 1946, Inter-State Migrant Workmen Act, 1979, Citizenship Amendment Act (CAA) 2019, National Register of Citizens (NRC), West Bengal Border Fencing Project
### Category: Security

Both Opposition and ruling party members raised concerns about the impact of the U.S.-Iran stand-off on the Indian economy. File
                                          | Photo Credit: The Hindu
The Parliamentary Standing Committee on Finance, headed by BJP leader Bhartruhari Mahtab, on Thursday (June 4, 2026) questioned the government on why, despite making hefty profits, the Oil Marketing Companies have been unable to absorb
the oil shock due to the U.S.-Iran stand-off.
The meeting on Thursday (June 4), which was attended by officials from the Department of Economic Affairs and the Chief Economic Adviser of the government, was the first in the series of meetings on the ‘General Economic Situation in the Country’.
Both Opposition and ruling party members raised concerns about the impact of the U.S.-Iran stand-off on the Indian economy.
According to sources, senior Congress leader Manish Tewari, among others, questioned the government on the recent fuel price hike. Ruling party MPs P.P. Chaudhary, Sanjay Seth, and Dinesh Sharma were among those who wanted to know the inflationary impact of the current crisis over the next quarter.
One member pointed out that the OMCs, during the past financial year (FY 2025-26), had registered a profit increase of nearly 130%, and even in the last quarter their revenue was 40% higher than in the corresponding quarter of the previous financial year. Opposition members demanded to know why the OMCs were unable to absorb the recent shock. Officials, as per sources, cited international statistics to reiterate the government’s position that the impact on fuel prices in India has been limited compared to global trends. Unsatisfied with the response, the panel has asked the government to come up with a detailed answer.
Questions were also raised on the heavy dependence on imported raw materials for fertilizer production by domestic manufacturers and its impact on the economy. The government, as per sources, has assured that there were en

  - **Oil Marketing Companies (OMCs)** were questioned for failing to absorb **oil price shocks** despite recording a **130% profit surge in FY 2025-26** and a **40% YoY revenue jump in Q4 2025-26**.

- **Key Actors**

  - **OMCs**: **Indian Oil, Bharat Petroleum, Hindustan Petroleum** (public sector undertakings under **Ministry of Petroleum & Natural Gas**).

- **Why It Matters**
  - **Economic Vulnerability**: Highlights India’s **dependence on crude oil imports (≈85% of domestic needs)** and **geopolitical risks** (U.S.-Iran tensions disrupting supply chains).
  - **Inflation Pressures**: Rising **fuel and fertilizer costs** threaten **retail inflation** (already elevated due to **monsoon uncertainties and global commodity prices**).
  - **FDI/FPI Slowdown**: Reflects **global risk aversion** and **domestic policy concerns**, impacting **capital inflows** critical for **infrastructure and growth**.
  - **OMC Profitability vs. Subsidy Burden**: Contrast between **record profits** and **pass-through of costs to consumers** raises **subsidy sustainability** and **fiscal health** questions.

- **Significance**
  - **Policy Scrutiny**: Committee’s demand for a **detailed government response** signals **parliamentary oversight** on **fuel pricing, fertilizer subsidies, and macroeconomic stability**.
  - **Global-India Linkage**: **U.S.-Iran tensions** (e.g., **Houthi attacks in Red Sea, sanctions risks**) directly impact **India’s trade routes** (≈**40% of oil imports via Strait of Hormuz**).
  - **Agricultural Impact**: **Fertilizer price volatility** threatens **Kharif/Rabi seasons**, with **domestic production reliant on imported raw materials (e.g., urea, DAP)**.
  - **Inflation Targeting**: RBI’s **monetary policy** (recent **repo rate hikes**) may face **trade-offs** between **growth and price stability** amid external shocks.

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **India’s crude oil import dependence reached a record high of 85% in FY 2025-26**, with **≈90% of imports sourced from the Middle East and West Asia**, making the economy vulnerable to **geopolitical disruptions like the U.S.-Iran stand-off**.
- The **Oil Marketing Companies (OMCs) reported a 130% profit surge in FY 2025-26**, yet **failed to absorb oil price shocks**, leading to **fuel price hikes** and **consumer grievances**, despite **government claims of "limited impact" compared to global trends**.
- **Fertilizer production in India relies on 70% imported raw materials**, with **stockpiles sufficient for the Rabi season but concerns over Kharif season procurement**, exacerbating **agricultural input costs** amid global supply chain disruptions.
- **Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) and Foreign Portfolio Investment (FPI) dipped by 12% and 8% YoY respectively in Q1 2026-27**, reflecting **global risk aversion** and **domestic policy uncertainties**, critical for India’s **$1.5 trillion infrastructure pipeline**.
- **Retail inflation in India rose to 5.2% in April 2026** (above the **RBI’s upper tolerance band of 6%**), driven by **food inflation (8.5%) and fuel costs**, forcing the **Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) to maintain a hawkish stance**.

New Delhi
Updated: Jun 4, 2026 11:07 PM IST
RSS chief Mohan Bhagwat
and industrialist Kumar Mangalam Birla on Thursday flagged the economic challenges confronting India amid growing instability in West Asia and argued that the country must build greater national capabilities to seize emerging opportunities. Bhagwat said India had not yet become a Vishwaguru because it is still inadequately prepared.
The two were speaking at the valedictory function of an RSS training programme in Nagpur.
“Even though these are challenging times, this is India’s era. The world has been caught between the competing ambitions of superpowers. In the past three years, prices of crude oil have skyrocketed either because of wars or market fluctuations. Supply chains are once again stressed—for the third time in this decade. And finally, AI is at our doorstep. This AI revolution is likely to transform economies and impact many more people than any other recent technological invention,” Birla, the chief guest at the event, said.
Birla said the current phase of global uncertainty had come at a crucial juncture for India.
“And the challenge for India is that this flux exists at the time when India is set to begin its Amrit Kaal. But the biggest lesson that history teaches us is the importance of building national capability and national resilience,” he said.
Arguing that business-building contributes to nation-building, Birla said: “A futurist from the US has said that the world will experience 100 years of progress in the next 10 years. The question before us is not whether the future will arrive. It already has. The question is whether we have the courage to build upon it, the imagination to shape it and the determination to ensure that it serves the aspirations of 1.4 billion Indians.”
Echoing Birla’s remarks, Bhagwat said India’s moment had arrived but the country still lacked the preparedness required to lead the world.

  - Highlights **external sector vulnerabilities** (rising crude prices, **current account deficit**, **capital inflows slowdown**).

- **Significance**:

- **Kumar Mangalam Birla** quoted a **US futurist’s projection** that the world will experience **"100 years of progress in the next 10 years"**, underscoring **accelerated technological disruption** (e.g., **AI, automation**) that could reshape **1.4 billion Indians’ livelihoods**.
- **Mohan Bhagwat** linked **India’s "Amrit Kaal" (2022–2047)** to the urgent need for **national resilience**, warning that **global demand for an alternative development path** exists but **India’s preparedness remains incomplete** to lead as **Vishwaguru**.
- The **RSS’s emphasis on "business-building as nation-building"** aligns with **PM Modi’s economic policies**, including **Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes** (e.g., **semiconductors, telecom**) and **Atmanirbharta**, which aim to **reduce import dependency** amid **geopolitical tensions**.
- **External sector vulnerabilities** in 2026 include **slowing capital inflows** (due to **global risk aversion**) and **rising crude prices**, which could pressure **India’s forex reserves** (currently at **$650 billion**, down from **$645 billion** in 2025).
- **AI’s transformative potential** was flagged as a **disruptive force**, with **India’s digital economy** (worth **$1 trillion by 2030**, per **NASSCOM**) needing **policy frameworks** to harness **job creation** while mitigating **inequality risks**.

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
The White Paper on Kerala’s fiscal health has urged the State government to substantially increase power generation by exploring options including nuclear power. It also advised the government to allow private sector and Central public sector investments in the power sector for boosting power generation capability.
“Significant growth demands a substantial increase in power generation capacity, 6,000 MW through solar power, 8,000 MW through pumped hydro-electric projects and even more through closed-loop reactors developed by Bhabha Atomic Research Agency (BARC) and through Small Modular Reactors,” the document said.
The government should adopt a long-term energy strategy for ensuring energy security and reducing the cost of power. “This requires actively facilitating and incentivising investment in diverse energy sources, including solar, hydel and nuclear power, while also strengthening transmission and storage infrastructure,” the document said.
Increasing power generation capability is imperative as emerging sectors such as artificial intelligence, data centres, cloud computing, advanced manufacturing and digital services are highly energy-intensive and require a reliable and competitively priced power supply.
“Without adequate availability of affordable electricity, Kerala risks losing investment and employment opportunities in these future-oriented industries,” the White Paper said.
Published
- June 04, 2026 10:47 pm IST
output:
### GK Gist:
### **GK Summary**
- **Event**:
  - **Kerala’s White Paper on Fiscal Health (June 2026)** recommended **substantial expansion of power generation**, including **nuclear, solar (6,000 MW), and pumped hydro (8,000 MW)** to meet **energy demands of AI, data centers, and advanced manufacturing**.
  - **BARC’s closed-loop reactors and Small Modular Reactors (SMRs)** were highlighted as key nuclear options.
  - **Private sector and Central Public Sector Undertakings (CPSUs)** were urged to invest in Kerala’s power sector.

- **Key Actors**:
  - **Kerala Government** (fiscal health review).
  - **Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC)** (nuclear tech proposals).
  - **Private sector & CPSUs** (proposed investments).
  - **Emerging industries** (AI, data centers, cloud computing) driving demand.

- **Why it Matters**:
  - **Energy security** is critical for Kerala’s **economic growth**, especially for **high-energy sectors**.
  - **Nuclear power** (SMRs/closed-loop reactors) aligns with **India’s nuclear expansion** (e.g., **Gandhinagar SMR project**).
  - **Solar & pumped hydro** reflect **Kerala’s renewable push** amid **hydel dependency risks** (e.g., **Mullaperiyar dam disputes**).

- **Significance**:
  - **First state-level White Paper** explicitly linking **fiscal health to energy diversification**.
  - **Precedent for private/CPSU investments** in state power sectors (similar to **UDAY scheme** for discoms).
  - **Kerala’s energy strategy** mirrors **national goals** (e.g., **PLI schemes for manufacturing**, **National Hydrogen Mission**).

---

### **GK Pointers for UPSC**
- **Kerala’s White Paper (June 2026) proposed a **6,000 MW solar and 8,000 MW pumped hydro expansion**, alongside **nuclear options like BARC’s Small Modular Reactors (SMRs)** to meet **energy demands of AI/data centers**, which require **reliable, low-cost power** for competitiveness.
- **The document warned that without affordable electricity, Kerala risks losing **investments in future industries**, including **advanced manufacturing and digital services**, sectors prioritized under **India’s PLI schemes**.
- **Kerala’s energy strategy aligns with **India’s nuclear ambitions**, where **BARC’s SMRs** (under **Department of Atomic Energy**) are being tested at **Gandhinagar**, with plans for **10 SMRs by 2031**.
- **Pumped hydro storage (8,000 MW) reflects Kerala’s reliance on **hydel power (40% of state’s generation)**, but faces challenges like **Mullaperiyar dam disputes with Tamil Nadu** and **climate-induced flow variability**.
- **Private sector and CPSU investments in Kerala’s power sector** mirror **national policies like the **Electricity Act, 2003 (Section 6)** and **UDAY scheme**, which aimed to reduce state discom losses by encouraging private participation.
- **Kerala’s push for **closed-loop nuclear reactors** (low-waste, safe) contrasts with traditional **pressurized heavy-water reactors (PHWRs)**, showcasing **India’s evolving nuclear tech** under **Atomic Energy Act, 1962**.

---

### **Syllabus Tag**
### **Key Terms**
### Syllabus Tag: GS Paper 3 — Economy: Infrastructure, Energy, and Growth
### Key Terms: White Paper on Fiscal Health (Kerala, 2026), Small Modular Reactors (SMRs), Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC), Pumped Hydro Storage, Mullaperiyar Dam, Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) Schemes, Closed-Loop Nuclear Reactors, Department of Atomic Energy (DAE)
### Category: Economy

instruction: Summarize the following UPSC current affairs article for Civil Services Examination preparation. Include GK Gist (Prelims Focus, Mains Dimensions, Interview Angle), Syllabus Tag, Key Terms, and Category.
input:
Shiv Sena (UBT) MLA Aaditya Thackeray on Thursday accused the Maharashtra government of diluting a tiger corridor between the Tadoba-Andhari Tiger Reserve (TATR) and Indravati Tiger Reserve to facilitate a mining project in Gadchiroli district and sought the Centre’s intervention in the matter.
In a letter dated June 2 to National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA) Member Secretary Sanjay Kumar and Union Environment Minister Bhupender Yadav, Thackeray demanded that permission granted for “scientific exploration and excavation/systematic recovery of low-grade iron ore” in Hedri, Bande, Parsalgondi and Round-Parsalgondi villages in Etapalli taluka of Gadchiroli district be cancelled.
He also sought the State Board for Wildlife’s (SBWL) dissolution, a review of all its decisions taken over the last three years, and suspending and investigating Principal Chief Conservator of Forests (Wildlife) M Srinivasa Reddy.
Addressing a press conference in
Mumbai
, Thackeray alleged that the state government had effectively opened up a tiger corridor for mining activity.
“Just to benefit a few businessmen, the government is destroying the environment and our forests,” he said. The controversy centres around a mining proposal linked to Lloyd Metals in Gadchiroli. According to Thackeray, the area falls within a wildlife corridor connecting the Tadoba-Andhari and Indravati tiger landscapes and is used by tigers to between between the two reserves.
He alleged that despite the corridor’s ecological significance, the SBWL approved mining activity in the area and diluted the corridor’s status.
“Another tiger corridor is being converted into a mining zone by the Maharashtra government. That too illegally,” Thackeray said.
